docs.title: Home
docs.text: This is some of the notes I take during my last two years of my master. I hope this will make it easier for me to look up results for later use.

docs.title: Intended learning outcomes
docs.text: At the end of the module students understand basic concepts of commutative algebra and their relation to the theory of affine varieties. They are able to apply the learned theorems and methods to particular problems concerning rings, in particular finitely generated algebras over a field, and translate them into results on the corresponding affine varieties.

docs.title: Content
docs.text:
• Basic algebraic concepts for commutative algebra (Rings, ideals, prime ideals, modules)
  • Localization
  • Valuations
  • Tensor products
  • Affine algebraic varieties
  • Finiteness conditions, dimension theory

docs.title: Algebras
docs.text:
Definition: \(R\)-Algebra

A ring \(A\) together with a homomorphism \(\alpha : R \to A\) is called an \(R\)-algebra.

Definition: Sub-algebra

A ring \(B \subseteq A\) s.t. \(\alpha (R) \subseteq B\)

Definition: Algebra homomorphism

\(\phi : A \to B\) is called an algebra homomorphism if for the ring homomorphisms \(\alpha : R \to A\) \(\beta : R \to B\) we get that \(\phi \circ \alpha = \beta\)

Definition: Affine (\(K\))-algebra

\(A\) is called an affine (\(K\))-algebra if it is finitely generated. I.e. \(A = K[\underline{a}]\) for some \(\underline a \in K^n\)

Definition: Affine (\(K\))-domain

\(A\) is called an affine (\(K\))-domain if it an affine algebra, which is also an integral domain.

Definition: Algebraic

Let \(A\) be a \(K\)-algebra.

The element \(a\in A\) is said to be algebraic if \(\exists\ 0 \neq f \in K[x] \text{ s.t. } f(a) = 0\).

\(A\) is called algebraic over \(K\) if all it's elements are algebraic.

docs.title: Hilbert's 1st Nullstellensatz
docs.text:
Proposition 1.2 Example 1.3

Let \(A = K[x], B = K(x), m = \{0\} \ideal B\) a maximal ideal.

\(A \cap m = \{0\} \ideal B\) is prime, but not maximal.

Lemma 1.4 Proposition 1.5 Definition 1.6 Theorem 1.7 Corollary 1.8

docs.title: Preimages of maximal ideals
docs.text:
Let \(\phi : A \to B\) be a homomorphism of \(K\)-algebras,

and \(B\) be f.g.

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \text{For any maximal ideal } m \ideal B \\ & \phi^{-1}(m) \ideal A \text{ is also a maximal ideal} \end{align*} \] Proof

Let

\[\begin{align*} \gamma : A & \to B/m \\ a & \to \phi(a) + m \end{align*}\]

be a homomorphism, and

\(n := \Ker \gamma = \phi^{-1}(m)\)

be it's kernel.

\[\begin{align*} \implies & A/n \cong \Im \gamma \subseteq B/m \text{, which is f.g.} \\ \implieshref{../2lemma1.1}{L.1.1b)} & A/n \text{ is an algebraic domain} \\ \implieshref{../2lemma1.1}{L.1.1a)} & A/n \text{ is a field} \\ \iff & n \ideal A \text{ is a maximal ideal} & \square \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Lemma 1.4
docs.text:
Let \(p=(\xi_i)_1^n\in K^n\)

\(\implies m_p = (x_1-\xi_1, ..., x_n-\xi_n) \ideal K[\underline x]\) is a maximal ideal.

Proof

Let \(\phi : K[\underline x] \to K\) be the surjective vealuation homomorphism at \(\xi \in K^n\).

\(\implies \Ker \phi = m_p\)

\(\implies K[\underline x]/m_p \cong \Im \phi = K\) is a field

\(\iff m_p\) is a maximal ideal.

docs.title: Maximal ideals in a polynomial ring
docs.text:
Let \(K=\overline K\) be algebraically closed,

and \(m\ideal K[\underline x]\) be a maximal ideal.

\(\implies \exists\ p=(\xi_i)_1^n \in K^n\) such that \(m=m_p\).

Proof

Let \(\phi : K[x_i] \to K[\underline x]\) be the identity hommomorphism.

\(\implieshref{#preimages-of-maximal-ideals}{L.1.2} K[x_i] \cap m = \phi^{-1}(m) \ideal K[x_i]\) is maximal.

\(K[x_i]\) is a p.i.d.

\(\implies p_i = x_i - \xi_i\) must be a monomial

\(\implies x_i - \xi_i \in m\ \forall\ i\)

\(m_p = (\underline x - \underline \xi) \subseteq m (*)\)

\(\implieshref{#lemma-14}{L.1.4} m_p \ideal K[x]\) is maximal \((**)\)

\(\overset{(*)+(**)}{\implies} m_p = m\)

docs.title: Affine variety
docs.text:
Let \(K\) be a field, and \(S \subseteq K[\underline x]\) a subset.

\[ \mathcal V(S) = \mathcal V_{K^n}(S) := \{p=(\xi)_1^n\in K^n | f(p) = 0\ \forall\ f \in S\} \]

is called the affine variety given by \(S\).

docs.title: Correspondence points–maximal ideals
docs.text:
Let

  • \(K = \overline K\) be algebraically closed
  • \(S \subseteq K[\underline x]\) be a subset
  • \(\M_S := \{m \ideal K[\underline x] | m \text{ is maximal } \land S \subseteq m\}\) be the set of maximal ideals containing S
  • \(\Phi : \V(S) \to \M_S\) be the homomorphism given by \(p \mapsto m_p\)

\(\implies \Phi\) is a bijection.

Proof

Surjectivity:

Let \(m \in \M_S\)

\[\begin{align*} \implieshref{#maximal-ideals-in-a-polynomial-ring}{L.1.5} & \exists\ p \in K^n \text{ s.t.}\\ & m = m_p \\ \implies & f \in S \subseteq m_p \text{ is generated by functions with root in } p \\ \implies & f(p) = 0 \\ \implies & p\in \mathcal V(S) \\ \implies & m = \Phi(p) \end{align*}\]

Injectivity:

Let \(P, Q \in \mathcal V(S)\) s.t. \(\Phi(P) = \Phi(Q)\).

\[\begin{align*} \implies & x_i - \xi_i \in m_p = m_q \ni x_i - \eta_i \\ \implies & (x_i - \xi_i) - (x_i - \eta_i) = \xi_i - \eta_i \in m_p \\ \implies &\xi_i - \eta_i = 0\ \forall i & \text{as } K \cap m_p = \emptyset \\ \implies & P = Q & & \square \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Hilbert's Nullstellsatz
docs.text:
Let \(K=\overline K,\ I \properideal K[x]\)

\(\implies \mathcal V(I) \neq \emptyset\)

Proof

Let \(\M:=\{J \properideal K[\underline x] | I \subseteq J\}\) be ordered by \(\subseteq\) inclusion. and \(c \subseteq \M\) be a chain.

Need to find an upper bound to use Zorn's lemma (Z.L.).

If \(c = \emptyset\), then any \(I \in \M\) is an upper bound. If not, then \(\overline J = \cup c \in \M\) is an upper bound.

\[\begin{align*} \implieshref{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zorn%27s_lemma}{Z.L.} & \M \text{ has a maximal element } m \in \M \\ \implies & m \text{ is a maximal ideal} \\ \implieshref{#correspondence-pointsmaximal-ideals}{T.1.7} & \exists\ p \in \mathcal V(I) \text{ s.t. } \\ & m=m_p \\ \implies & \mathcal V(I) \neq \emptyset & \square \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Spectra
docs.text:
Let \(R\) be a ring.

Spectrum of \(R\)Maximal spectrum of \(R\)Rabinowitsch spectrum of \(R\)

\(\Spec R := \{P \ideal R | P \text{ is a prime ideal}\}\)

\(\Specmax R := \{P \ideal R | P \text{ is a maximal ideal}\}\)

\(\Specrab R := \{m \cap R | m \in \Specmax{R[x]}\}\)

docs.title: Radical
docs.text:
Let \(I \ideal R\)

\(\sqrt I := \{a \in R | \ \exists\ k \in \N : a^k \in I\}\)

is called the radical ideal

docs.title: Lemma 1.10
docs.text:
Let \(\M \subseteq \Spec R\) be a set of prime ideals and \(I \ideal R\) be an ideal.

\[ \implies \sqrt I \subseteq \bigcap_{I \subseteq P \in \M} P \] Proof

Let \(a \in \sqrt I, I\subseteq P\in \M\)

\[\begin{align*} \implies & \exists\ k : a^k \in I \subseteq P = \sqrt P \\ \implies & a \in P & \square \end{align*}\]

docs.title: The raison d’être of the Rabinowitsch spectrum
docs.text:
Let \(I \ideal R\)

\[\implies \sqrt I = \bigcap_{\substack{P \in \Specrab R \\ I \subseteq P}} P\] Proof

\(\subseteq:\) follows from L.1.10

\(\supseteq:\) Let \(a \in \cap P\) and

\[ J := \left(I \cup \{ax-1\}\right)_{R[x]} \ideal R[x] \]

We want to show that \(J = R[x]\). Now, assume it is not.

\[\begin{align*} \implieshref{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zorn%27s_lemma}{Z.L.} & \ \exists\ m \in \Specmax{R[x]} \text{s.t.} \\ & J \subseteq m \\ \implies & I \subseteq R \cap J \subseteq R \cap m \in Specrab R \\ \implies & a \in R \cap m \land ax - 1 \in m\\ \implies & (ax) - (ax - 1) = 1 \in m \contradiction \end{align*}\]

We thus get that \(J = R[x]\). The next steps are similar to the solving real problems by moving into the´ complex domain.

There exists \(g_j, g \in R[x]\) and \(b_j \in I\) such that

\[1 = \sum_{j=1}^n g_j b_j + g(ax-1) \ (*)\]

Apply the homomorphism

\[\begin{align*} \phi : R[x] & \to R[x, x^{-1}] \\ f(x) & \mapsto f(x^{-1}) \end{align*}\]

to \((*)\), and multiply by \(x^k\):

\[\implies x^k = \sum_{j=1}^n x^k \phi(g_j) b_j + x^{k-1} \phi(g) (a-x) \ (**)\]

Choose \(k \le \max \{\deg g_j\}_{j=1}^n \cup \{1+\deg g\}\) and let \(h(x):=x^k \phi(g)\) and \(h_j(x) := x^{k-1} \phi(g)\).

\(\implies h, h_j \in R[x]\)

Now, evaluate \((**)\) at \(x=a\):

\[\begin{align*} a^k &= \sum_{j=1}^n h_j(a) b_j + 0 \in I \\ \implies a & \in \sqrt I & \square\\ \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Intersecting prime ideals
docs.text:
Let \(I \ideal R, \M=\Spec R\)

\[\implies \sqrt I = \bigcap_{\substack{P \in \M \\ I \subseteq P}} P\] Proof

\(\subseteq:\) follows from L.1.10

\(\supseteq:\)

\[\begin{align*} \Specrab R & \subseteq \Spec R \\ \implies \bigcap_{\substack{P \in \Spec R \\ I \subseteq P}} P & \subseteq \bigcap_{\substack{P \in \Specrab R \\ I \subseteq P}} P = \sqrt I & \square \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Intersecting maximal ideals
docs.text:
Let \(I \ideal A\) be an ideal in an affine algebra

\[\implies \sqrt I = \bigcap_{\substack{m \in \Specmax A \\ I \subseteq m}} m\] Proof

\(\subseteq:\) follows from L.1.10

\(\supseteq:\) Let \(P \in \Specrab A\)

\[\begin{align*} \implieshref{../4nullstellensatz_2/#preimages-of-maximal-ideals}{L.1.2} & P \in \Specmax A \\ \implies & \Specrab A \subseteq \Specmax A \\ \implies & \bigcap_{\substack{m \in \Specmax A \\ I \subseteq m}} m \subseteq \bigcap_{\substack{P \in \Specrab A \\ I \subseteq P}} P \overset{\href{#the-raison-detre-of-the-rabinowitsch-spectrum}{P.1.11}}{=} \sqrt I & \square \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Jacobson ring
docs.text:
A ring \(R\) is called a Jacobson ring if \(\forall\ I\ideal R:\)

\[\sqrt I = \bigcap_{\substack{m \in \Specmax R \\ I \subseteq m}} m\]

docs.title: Vanishing ideal
docs.text:
Let \(X \subseteq K^n\) be any subset.

\[\I(X) := \{f \in K[\underline x] : f|_X = 0\}\]

is called the vanishing ideal of \(X\).

docs.title: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz
docs.text:
Let \(K = \overline K, I \ideal K[\underline x]\)

\[\implies \I(\V(I)) = \sqrt I\] Proof \[\begin{align*} \I(\V(I)) &= \bigcap_{p \in \V(I)} m_p \overset{\href{../3nullstellensatz_1/#correspondence-pointsmaximal-ideals}{T.1.7}}{=} \bigcap_{\substack{m \in \Specmax{K[\underline x]} \\ I \subseteq m}} m \overset{\href{#intersecting-maximal-ideals}{T.1.13}}{=} \sqrt I & \square \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Lemma 1.18
docs.text:
Let \(X \subseteq K^n\) be an affine variery.

\[\implies \V(\I(X)) = X\] Proof

Let \(S \subseteq K[\underline x]\) be a subset s.t. \(X = \V(S)\).

\[\begin{align*} \implies & S \subseteq \I(X) \\ \implies & \V(\I(X)) \subseteq \V(S) = X \subseteq \V(\I(X)) & \square \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Ideal–variety correspondence
docs.text:
Let \(K = \overline K, n \in \N_+\)

\[\begin{align*} \implies & \text{There is an inclusion-reversing bijection between} \\ & A:= \{I \ideal K[\underline x] | I \text{ is a radical ideal}\} \\ & G:= \{X \subseteq K^n | X \text{ is an affine variety}\} \\ & A \to G \text{ given by } I \mapsto \V(I) \\ & G \to A \text{ given by } X \mapsto \I(X) \end{align*}\] Proof \[ \I(\V(I)) \overset{\href{#hilberts-nullstellensatz}{L.1.17}}{=} \sqrt I = I \tab \square \]

docs.title: Coordinate Rings
docs.text:
Definition 1.20 Remark 1.21 a)b)

Given any \(f + I \in K[X]\), the evaluation homomorphism

\[X o K, \underline \xi \mapsto f(\underline \xi)\]

is well-defined.

Given an ideal \(J \ideal K[\underline x], X = \V(J)\), then in general, we have that

\[K[X] eq K[\underline x]/J\] Lemma 1.22 Remark

Let \(J \in A\)

Preservation of congruence ringsPreservation of prime/maximal/radical ideals

\(R/J \cong (R/I)/\Phi(J)\) congruence rings are preserved.

\(J\) is prime/maximal/radical \(\iff\) \(\Phi(J)\) is prime/maximal/radical

Theorem 1.23 Definition 1.24 NilpotentNilradicalReduced

The element \(a \in R\) is called nilpotent if

\[\exists\ k \in \N ext{ s.t. } a^k=0\] \[ il(R) := \{a \in R | a ext{ is nilpotent}\} = \sqrt{\{0\}}\]

A ring \(R\) is called reduced if

\[ il(R) = \{0\}\] Remark a)b)c)

\(R\) is an integral domain \(\implies R\) is reduced.

\(I\ideal R\)

\[I ext{ is radical } \iff R/I ext{ is reduced}\]

\(R/ il R\) is reduced.

docs.title: Coordinate ring
docs.text:
Let \(X \subseteq K^n\) be an affine variety \(I = \I(X) \ideal K[\underline x]\)

\[K[X]:= K[\underline x]/I\]

is called the coordinate ring on \(X\), or the ring of regular functions on \(X\).

docs.title: Ideals in quotient rings
docs.text:
Let \(I \ideal R\)

Then, there exists an inclusion preserving bijection between

\[\begin{align*} A &:= \{J \ideal R | I \subseteq J \} \\ B &:= \{\overline J \ideal R/I\} \end{align*}\]

given by

\[\begin{align*} \Phi : A & \to B \\ J & \mapsto J/I \\ \Phi^{-1} : B & \to A \\ \overline J & \mapsto \{a \in R | a + I \in \overline J\} \end{align*}\]

docs.title: Correspondence subvarieties–radical ideals
docs.text:
Let \(K=\overline K\) and \(K \subseteq K^n\) ba en affine variety.

Then, there exists ai inclusion reversing bijection between

\[ \begin{align*} A &:= \{J \ideal K[X] | J \text{is radical}\} \\ G &:= \{Y \subseteq X | Y \text{is a subvariety}\} \end{align*} \]

given by

\[ \begin{align*} \Phi : A & \to G \\ J & \mapsto \V_X(J) := \{x \in X | f(x) \ \forall\ f \in J\} \\ \Phi^{-1} : G & \to A \\ Y & \mapsto \I(Y)/\I(X) \end{align*} \] Proof

C.1.19 + L.1.22

docs.title: Coordinate rings and reduced algebras
docs.text:
Theorem 1.25 a)

Let \(X \subseteq K^n\) be an affine variety

\[\implies K[X] \text{ is a reduced affine algebra over } K\] Proof

\(I=\I(X)\) is a radical ideal.

\[\implies K[\underline x]/I \text{ is reduced, generated by } x_i + I \tab \square\] Theorem 1.25 b)

Let

  • \(K=\overline K\)
  • \(A\) be a reduced affine algebra
\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \exists\ X \subseteq K^n \text{ an affine variety s.t.} \\ & A \cong K[X] \end{align*} \] Proof

\(A = K[\underline a], a \in A^n\)

Let \(\phi_a : K[\underline x] \to A\) be the canonical evaluation epimorphism at \(\underline x = \underline a\).

\(I := \Ker \phi_a\) which is reduced

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & A \cong K[\underline x]/I \\ \implies & \sqrt I = I \end{align*} \]

Let \(X:= \V(I)\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implieshref{../4nullstellensatz_2/#hilberts-nullstellensatz}{T.1.17} & I = \I(X) \\ \implies & A \cong K[X] & \square \end{align*} \] Remark a)b)

\(X\) is not uniquely determined.

With an appropriate isomorphism, \(X\) is unique up to isomorphism.

docs.title: The Noether and Artin Properties for Rings and Modules
docs.text:
Definition: Module

A module \(M\) over \(R\) is a vector space over \(R\).

Remark BasisFree modulesFree vector spaces

Not every module has a basis.

A module with a basis is called free.

All vector spaces (modules over fields) are free.

Example

\(\Z/(2)\) is a \(\Z\)-module:

\(a (b + (2)) = ab + (2)\)

Notation

\(m_1, ..., m_n \in M\)

\((m_1, ..., m_n)_R = (m_1, ..., m_n)\) is the submodule generated by \(m_1, ..., m_n\), which consists of all \(R\)-linear combinations.

Definition 2.1 Example 2.2 \(R=\Z\)\(K[x]\)\(R=K^S\)\(\Z\) or \(K[x]\)Fields and finite rings

\(I_n \ideal \Z \iff I_n = (a_n)\) for some \(a_n \in \Z\)

Assume \(\{I_n\}\) is an acsending chain.

\(\implies a_{n+1} | a_n\).

This has to stop.

\(\implies \Z\) is Noetherian

\(K[x]\) is noetheriam by the same arguments as for \(R=\Z\)

Let \(S\) be an infinite set

\(R = K^S = \{f : S \to K\}\) with pointwise addition and multiplication.

\(Y \subseteq S\) any subset

\(I_Y:=\{f \in R : f(Y) = \{0\}\}\) is an ideal.

\(Y_1 \subsetneq Y_2 \implies I_{Y_1} \supsetneq I_{Y_2}\)

Then there exists an infinite strictly descending chain of strict subsets, thus an infinite strict ascending chain of ideals.

\(\implies R\) is not Noetherian.

Neither \(\Z\) nor \(K[x]\) are Artinian. Consider \(I_i = (2^i)\)

are both Artinian and Noetherian.

Example 2.3

\(K[x, xy, xy^2, ...] \subseteq K[x, y]\) is not Noetherian.

Proposition 2.4 Definition 2.5 Lemma 2.6 Lemma 2.7 Theorem 2.8

docs.title: Noetherian and artinian rings and modules
docs.text:
Let \(R\) be a ring and \(M\) be a module.

Ascending chain condition = a.c.c Descending chain condition = (d.c.c)

Noetherian moduleNoetherian ringArtinian moduleArtinian ring

\(M\)'s submodules satisfies a.c.c.

\(R\)'s ideals satisfy a.c.c

docs.title: Submodules and quotient modules
docs.text:
Let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module, and \(N\subseteq M\) be a sumbodule.

\[ M \text{ is Noetherian/Artinian } \iff N \land M/N \text{ is Noetherian/Artinian} \] Proof

The proof for artinian follows from tha same arguments.

\(\implies\)\(\impliedby\)

\(M\) is Noetherian \(\implies N\) is Noetherian.

Let \(U_1 \subseteq U_2 \subseteq ... \subseteq M/N\) be a chain, and

\[ \begin{align*} \phi : M & \to M/N \\ m & \mapsto m + N \end{align*} \]

be a homomorphism.

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \phi^{-1}(U_1) \subseteq \phi^{-1}(U_2) \subseteq ... \subseteq M \text{ stabilizes} \\ \implies & U_1 \subseteq U_2 \subseteq ... \subseteq M/N \text{ must stabilize.} \end{align*} \]

Let \(M_1 \subseteq M_2 \subseteq ... \subseteq M\) be a chain.

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \begin{cases} \phi(M_1) & \subseteq \phi(M_2) & \subseteq ... & \subseteq M/N \\ N \cap M_1 & \subseteq N \cap M_2 & \subseteq ... & \subseteq N \end{cases} \\ \implies &\exists\ n\in\N \text{ s.t.} \\ & \begin{rcases} \phi(M_i) = \phi(M_n) \\ N \cap M_i = N \cap M_n \end{rcases} \ \forall i > n \end{align*} \]

We claim that \(M_i = M_n \ \forall\ i>n\):

Let \(m \in M_i\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \phi(m) \in \phi(M_i) = \phi(M_n) \\ \implies & \exists\ m' \in M_n \text{ s.t.} \\ & m'-m \in N \cap M_i = N \cap M_n \\ \implies & m = (m - m') + m1 \in M_n & \square \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Ideal Product
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & I \ideal R \\ & M \text{ be an } R \text{-module} \end{align*}\)

ProductIdeal ProductIdeal power \[I \cdot M = \left\{\sum_{i=1}^n a_i m_i | n\in\N, a_i\in I, m_i\in\ M\right\} \subseteq M\]

\(M = J \ideal R\)

\[M \cdot I \ideal R\]

This is commutative and associative.

\(I \cdot J \subseteq I \cap J\)

\(\sqrt{I\cdot J} = \sqrt{I \cap J}\)

\[I^n\\ I^0:=R\]

docs.title: Ideal powers and radical ideals
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & I, J \ideal R \\ & I \text{ f.g.} \end{align*}\)

\[ I \subseteq \sqrt J \iff \exists\ k \in \N : I^k \subseteq J\] Proof \(\impliedby\)\(\implies\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{We have that } & I^k \subseteq J \\ & a \in I \\ \implies & a^k \in I^k \subseteq J \\ \implies & a \in \sqrt J & \square \end{align*}\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{We have that } & I = (a_1, a_2, ..., a_n) \\ & I \subseteq \sqrt J \\ \implies & \begin{cases} \exists\ m>0 \text{ s.t.} \\ a^m \in J \end{cases} \end{align*}\)

Let \(k:=n(m-1)+1\)

Claim: Every product of k elements from I lies in J. This would imply \(I^k \subseteq J\).

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & x_1, ..., x_k \in I \\ & x_i = \sum_{j=1}^n r_{ij} a_j & r_{ij}\in R \\ \end{align*}\)

\(\implies \prod_{i=1}^k x_i \in J\), as at least one \(a_j\) is picked \(m\) times \(\tab\square\)

docs.title: Lemma 2.7
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & m_1, ..., m_n \in \Specmax R \text{ s.t.} \\ & m_1 \cdot m_2 \cdot ... \cdot m_n = \{0\} \end{align*}\)

\[R \text{ is Noetherian } \iff R \text{ is Artinian}\]

Moreover,

\[\Spec R = \{m_i\}_1^n\] Proof

Set \(I_i = \prod_{j=1}^i m_j \ideal R\)

\(\implies \{0\} = I_n \subseteq I_{n-1} \subseteq ... \subseteq I_1 \subseteq I_0 = R\)

By P.2.4, we get that

\[ R \text{ is } \begin{matrix} \text{Noetherian} \\ \text{Artinian} \end{matrix} \iff I_{i-1}/I_i \text{ is } \begin{matrix} \text{Noetherian} \\ \text{Artinian} \end{matrix}\ \ \forall\ i \]

We have that \(m_i (I_{i-1}/I_i) = \{0\}\)

\(\implies I_{i-1}/I_i\) is a vectorspace over \(K_i:=R/m_i\).

So \(I_{i-1}\) is Artinian/Noetherian \(\iff \dim_{k_i}(I_{i-1}/I_i) < \infty \tab\square\)

docs.title: Artinian rings are Noetherian
docs.text:
\[R ext{ is Artinian } \iff R ext{ is Noetherian and } \Spec R = \Specmax R\] Proof \(\implies\)\(\impliedby\)

Assume \(|\Specmax R|=\infty\)

\(\implies \ \exists \left\{m_i ight\}_{i\in\N} \subseteq \Specmax R\) which are pairwise distinct.

Setting \( egin{align*} I_i = igcap_{j=1}^i m_j \end{align*}\) yields a decreasing chain.

\[ egin{align*} \implies & \exists\ n : I_{n+1} = I_n \ \implies & igcap_{j=1}^n m_j \subseteq m_{n+1} \ \implies & egin{cases} \exists\ j \le n ext{ s.t.} \ m_j \subseteq m_{n+1} \end{cases} \implies m_j = m_{n+1} \contradiction\ \implies & \Specmax R = \{m_i\}1^k, ext{ for some finite } k \ \end{align*} \]

Let \( egin{align*} I = igcap_{j=1}^k m_j \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} \implies & I^i ext{ forms a descending sequence.} \ \implies & egin{cases} \exists\ n\in\N ext{ s.t.} \ I^i = I^n =: J ab orall i \ge n \end{cases} ab (*) \end{align*} \]

If \(J = \{0\}\), it follows from L.2.7 that \(R\) is Artinian. Assume it is not.

Let \(\M := \{J' \ideal R | J' \cdot J eq \{0\}\} eq \emptyset\). As \(R\) is Artinian, \(\M\) must have a minimal element \(\hat J \in \M\).

Pick any \(x \in \hat J\) s.t. \(x J eq \{0\}\).

\(\implies J \supseteq (x) \in \M\) is a submodule

\(\implies \hat J = (x)\), as \(J\) is minimal in \(\M\)

From \((*)\), we get

\[ egin{align*} & J^2 = I^{2n} = J \ & \implies \hat JJ \cdot J = \hat JJ^2 = \hat JJ eq \{0\} \ & \implies \hat J\cdot J \in \M \ & \implies \hat J\cdot J = \hat J, ext{ as J is minimal} \end{align*} \]

As \(\hat J = (x)\) is pricipal, we get

\[ egin{align*} & (x) J = (x) \ & \implies egin{cases} \exists\ y \in J ext{ s.t.} \ x = xy \end{cases} \end{align*} \]

As \(J \subseteq I \subseteq m_j \ orall\ j\):

\[ egin{align*} \implies& y \in m_j \ orall\ j \ \implies &y-1 ext{ lies in no maximal ideal} \ \implies &(y-1) = R ab ext{generates R}\ \implies &y-1 \in R^ imes \ (y-1)x=0 \implies & x = 0 \contradiction \ \implies & J = \{0\} & \square \end{align*} \]

See Noetherian and Irreducible Spaces.

docs.title: Noetherian Rings and Modules
docs.text: Theorem 2.9 Remark: Theorem 2.9 for rings a)b) \[R \text{ is Noetherian } \iff \text{ all ideals } I \ideal R \text{ is finitely generated}\] \[\text{Every generating set contains a finite generating set}\] Theorem 2.10 Theorem 2.11 Corollary 2.12 Theorem 2.13

docs.title: Alternate definition of Noetherian Modules
docs.text:
Let \(M\) be an \(R\)-module

The following are equivalent:

a)b)c) \[M \text{ is Noetherian}\]

Let \(S \subset M\) be any subset.

\[ \begin{align*} \exists\ m_1, ..., m_n \in S \text{ s.t.} \\ (S)_R = (m_1, ..., m_n) \end{align*} \] \[\text{Every submodule of } M \text{ is f.g.}\] Proof \((a) \implies (b)\)\(b) \implies c)\)\(c) \implies a)\)

Assume \(\exists\ S\subseteq M\) not satisfying \(b)\).

Recursively define (S_i) of finite subsets \(S_i \subset S\) as follows:

  • Set \(S_i = \emptyset\)
  • Choose an element \(m_{i+1}\in S\backslash(S_i)\)
  • Let \(S_{i+1} = S_i \cup \{m_{i+1}\}\)
\[ \begin{align*} & \implies (S_i) \subsetneq (S_{i+1}) \\ & \implies M \text{ is not Noetherian } \contradiction \\ & \implies S \text{ must satisfy } b) & \square \end{align*} \]

Let \(S=M\) be any submodule.

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & M_1 \subseteq M_2 \subseteq ... \subseteq M \text{ be an ascending chain} \\ & N:=\bigcup_{i\in\N} M_i \tab\text{is a submodule (verify this!)} \\ \implies & N = (m_1, ..., m_n) \end{align*}\)

For each \(j\), we have that \(m_j \in M_j\).

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } &k:= \max\left\{i_j\right\}_{j=1}^n \\ \implies & m_k \in M_k \\ \text{Let } &i \ge k \\ \implies & M_i \subseteq N = (m_1, ..., m_n) \subseteq M_k \subseteq M_i \\ \implies &M_i = M_k & \square \end{align*}\)

docs.title: Noetherian modules and finite generation
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & R \text{ be Noetherian} \\ & M \text{ be an } R\text{-module} \end{align*}\)

\[M \text{ is Noetherian} \iff M \text{ is finitely generated}\] Proof \(\implies\)\(\impliedby\)

Follows from T.2.9.

Let \(M=(m_1, ..., m_n)\)

Induction on \(n\):

\(n=0:\tab M=\{0\}\)

\(n>0:\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & N:=(m_1, ..., m_{n-1}) \subseteq M \text{ be a submodule. Its is Noetherian by the induction hypothesis} \\ & \begin{rcases} \phi: R &\to M/N \\ a &\mapsto a\ m_n + N \end{rcases} \text{ be a surjective } R \text{-linear homomorphism} \\ \implieshref{../6noetherian_and_artinian_properties#p24}{P.2.4} & R/\Ker \phi \text{ is Noetherian} \\ \implies & M/N \cong R/\Ker \phi \\ \implieshref{../6noetherian_and_artinian_properties#p24}{P.2.4} & M \text{ is Noetherian} & \square \end{align*}\)

docs.title: Polynomial rings over Noetherian rings
docs.text:
Let \(R\) be a Noetherian ring.

\[\implies R[x] \text{ is Noetherian}\] Proof

We want to show that all ideals \(I\ideal R[x]\) are f.g. as modules, and use T2.10 to show that \(R[x]\) is Noetherian.

For \(i \in \N_0\), set

\[J_i:=\{a_i \in R | \ \exists\ a_0, ..., a_{i-1} \in R : \sum_{j=0}^i a_j x^j \in I\} \ideal R\]

Let \(a_i \in J_i\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \exists\ f = \sum_{j=0}^i a_j x^j \in I \\ \implies & x f \in I \\ \implies &a_i \in J_{i+1} \\ \implies &J_i \in J_{i+1} \\ \implies & \begin{cases} \exists\ n\in\N \text{ s.t.} \\ J_i = J_n \tab \forall\ i \ge n \\ \end{cases}\tab\text{as R is Noetherian} \\ \implies & J_i = (a_{i,1}, ..., a_{i,m_i})_R \tab a_{i,j}\in J_i\tab\text{is finitely generated } (*) \end{align*} \]

Let \(a_i \in J_i\) For \(i \ge n\):

\[\implies J_i = (a_{n,1}, ..., a_{n,m_n}) \tab(**)\]

\(\begin{align*} \text{For } & 0 \le i \le n \\ & 1 \le j \le m_j \\ \text{Let } & f_{i,j} \in I \text{ be a function of degree } \deg f_{i,j} \le i \\ \text{and } & a_{i,j} \in J_i \text{ be the } i\text{th coefficient of } f_{i,j} \end{align*}\)

We want to prove that \(\left\{f_{i,j}\right\}_{i=0,j=1}^{n,m_i}\) generate I:

Let

\[I':= (f_{i,j} | 0 \le i \le n , 1 \le j \le m_i)_{R[x]} \subseteq I\]

and take any \(f \in I\). We claim that \(f\in I'\). Using induction on \(d:=\deg f\):

\(d\le n\)\(d>n\)

Let \(b_d\) be the coefficient of \(x^d\) in \(f\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & b_d \in J_d \\ \overset{(*)}{\implies} & \begin{cases} b_d & \in (a_{d, 1}, ..., a_{d_{m_d}}) \\ b_d & = \sum_{j=1}^{m_d} r_j a_{d,j} \end{cases} \end{align*} \]

Let \(\begin{align*}\tilde f:=f - \sum_{j=1}^{m_d} r_j f_{d,j} \in I\end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \deg \tilde f < d \\ \implies & \tilde f \in I' \\ \implies & f = \tilde f + \sum_{j=1}^{m_d} f_{d,j} \in I' \end{align*} \]

Let \(b_d\) be the coefficient of \(x^d\) in \(f\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & b_d \in J_d \\ \overset{(*)+(**)}{\implies} & \begin{cases} b_d & \in (a_{d, 1}, ..., a_{d_{m_d}}) = (a_{n, 1}, ..., a_{n_{m_n}}) \\ b_d & = \sum_{j=1}^{m_d} r_j a_{d,j} = \sum_{j=1}^{m_n} r_j a_{n,j} \end{cases} \end{align*} \]

Let \(\begin{align*}\tilde f:=f - \sum_{j=1}^{m_n} r_j f_{n,j} \in I\end{align*} \in I\).

Then \(\deg \tilde f < d\), which by the induction hypothesis implies that it lies in \(I'\)

\[\begin{align*}\implies f \in I' && \square\end{align*}\]

docs.title: Finitely generated algebras
docs.text: Every f.g. algebra over a Noetherian ring is Noetherian

docs.title: Hilbert's basis theorem
docs.text: Every ideal in \(K[\underline x]\) is f.g.

docs.title: Affine varieties
docs.text:
Proposition 3.1 Definition 3.2 Remark 3.3 a)b)c)d)e)f)g)h)i)

The closed subsets are \(\V(S)=\V(I)\) with \(I:=\I(S)\)

\(\implieshref{../../noetherian_and_artinian_rings/7noether_rings_modules/#t213}{T.2.13}\) \(X=\V(f_1, ..., f_m)\) if \(I=(f_1, ..., f_m)\)

By L.1.18 We have a formula for the closure of sets:

\[\overline X = \V(\I(X))\]

Let \(Y \subseteq K^n\) be an affine variety.

The closed subsets are the subvarieties (by P.3.1)

On \(\R^n\) or \(\C^n\), the Zariski-topology is coarser than the usual topology.

Finite sets in \(K^n\) are closed.

\[X \subset K^1 \text{ is closed } \iff |X| < \infty \lor X = K^1\]

Every polynomial function \(K^n \to K\) is continuous.

Open sets are given by polynomial inequalities.

If \(|K|\), then it is not Hausdorff (T2).

Moreover, if \(\emptyset \neq U_1, U_2 \subseteq K^n\) are open, then \(U_1 \cap U_2 \neq \emptyset\).

Definition 3.4

docs.title: Unions and intersections of affine varieties
docs.text:
Let \(n\in\N\)

a)b)

For \(I, J \ideal K[\underline x]\):

\[\V(I) \cup \V(J) = \V(I\cap J)\]

For \(\emptyset eq \M\), the set of subsets of \(K[\underline x]\):

\[ igcap_{S\in\M} \V(S) = \V\left( igcup_{S\in\M} S ight)\] Proof a)b)

Convince yourself that the inclusion \(\subseteq\) is trivial.

Let \(p\in \V(I\cap J)\) Assume \(p ot\in \V(I)\). We must show that it implies \(p\in \V(J)\).

\[ egin{align*} \implies \exists\ & g \in I ext{ s.t.} \ &g(p) eq 0 \end{align*} \]

Let \(f \in J\).

\[ egin{align*} \implies & f \cdot g \in I \cap J \ \implies & f(p)g(p) = 0 \ \implies & f(p) = 0 & ext{ as } g(p) eq 0 \ \implies & f \in \V(J) & & \square \end{align*} \]

This is also somehow clear.

docs.title: The Zariski-Topology
docs.text:
Closed sets are the affine varieties.

The closed sets of the subset topology \(Y \subseteq K^n\) are \(Y \cap X\), where \(X\) is an affine variety.

docs.title: Morphisms
docs.text:
Let \(X \subseteq K^m, Y \subseteq K^n\) be affine varietiies.

The map

\[f : X \to Y\]

is called a morphism if for all \(P\in X\) there are \(f_a, ..., f_n \in K[x_1, ..., x_m]\) such that

\[f(P) = \left(f_1(P), ..., f_n(P)\right) \in Y\]

\(\operatorname{Mor}(X, Y)\) is the set of all morphisms from \(X\) to \(Y\).

A morhpism \(f\) is called an isomorphism if \(\exists\ g : Y \to X\) s.t.

\[f \circ g = g \circ f = \mathcal{Id}\]

docs.title: Noetherian and Irreducible Spaces
docs.text:
Definition 3.7 Example 3.8
  1. \(\R^n\) and \(\C^n\) are not Noetherian.
  2. \(|X| < \infty \implies X\) is Noetherian.
  3. \(|X| = 1 \implies X\) is irreducible.
  4. \(|K|=\infty \implies K^1\) the affine line is irreducible.
Theorem 3.9 Theorem 3.10 Theorem 3.11 Definnition 3.12 Example 3.13

Let \(f\in K[\underline x] ackslash K\).

\[ egin{align*} &\implies f = \prod_{i=1}^r p_i^{e_i}, & p_i sim p_j \iff i eq j ext{ irreducible polynomials} \ &\implies \V_{K^n}(f) = igcup_{i=1}^r \V_{K^n}(p_i) \end{align*} \] Corollary 3.14 Theorem 2.8 \[R ext{ is Noetherian } \iff R ext{ is Noetherian and } \Spec R = \Specmax R\] Proof \(\implies\)\(\impliedby\)

See original statement.

By C.3.14, we have that

\[ egin{align*} & il(R) = \sqrt{\{0\}} = igcap_{i=1}^n m_i, & m_i \in \Specmax R \ &\implies I:=\prod_{i=1}^n m_i \subseteq \sqrt{\{0\}} \ &\implieshref{../../noetherian_and_artinian_rings/6noetherian_and_artinian_properties/#l26}{L.2.6} \exists\ k : I^k \subseteq \{0\} \iff I^k \{0\} \ &\implies \prod_{i=1}^n m_i^k = \{0\} & ext{a product of maximal ideals} \ &\implieshref{../../noetherian_and_artinian_rings/6noetherian_and_artinian_properties/#l27}{L.2.7} R ext{ is Artinian} && \square \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Noetherian and Irreducible Spaces
docs.text:
Let \(X\) be a topological space.

NoetherianIrreducible

\(X\) is called noetherian if closed subsets satisfies d.c.c.

Thus, the open subsets satisfies the a.c.c.. For \(X=\Spec R\), this induces ascending chains of ideals.

\(X\) is called irreducible if \(X\neq \emptyset\), and cannot be written as the union of two closed proper subsets. This implies connectedness.

docs.title: Noether property of the Zariski topology
docs.text:
a)b)

Let \(X\subseteq K^n\) be a set of points equipped with the Zariski-topology.

\[\implies X \text{ is Noetherian}\]

Let \(R\) be a Noetherian ring and \(X\subseteq \Spec R\) equipped with the Zariski-topology.

\[\implies X \text{ is Noetherian}\] Proof a)b)

Assume without loss of generality that \(X=K^n\).

Let \(\{X_i\}\) be a descending sequence of closed sets in \(X\).

Then, \(\I(X_i)\) is an ascending sequence of ideals in \(K[\underline x]\), which is Noehterian. It must therefore stabilize. Hence, the chain \(\{X_i\}=\{\V(\I(X_i))\}\) must stabilise.

Assume without loss of generality that \(X=\Spec R\).

Let \(\{X_i\}\) be a descending sequence of closed sets in \(X\).

Then, \(\I(X_i)\) is an ascending sequence of ideals in \(R\), which is Noehterian. It must therefore stabilize. Hence, the chain \(\{X_i\}=\{\V(\I(X_i))\}\) must stabilise.

docs.title: Irreducible subsets
docs.text:
Let \(I = \I(X)\).

a)b) \[X \subseteq K^n ext{ is irreducible } \iff \I(X) ext{ is a prime ideal}\] \[X \subseteq \Spec R ext{ is irreducible } \iff \I(X) ext{ is a prime ideal}\] Proof a)b)

Let \(\V:=\V_K\) and \(\I:=\I_{K[\underline x]}\) as defined in respectively D.1.6 and D.1.15.

\(\implies\)\(\impliedby\)

First of all, \(X\) being irreducible implies \(X eq \emptyset\) and \(\I(X) \properideal K[\underline x]\).

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } &f_1, f_2 \in K[\underline x] ext{ s.t.} \ & f_1 f_2 \in I \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} &\implies X \subseteq \V(f_1 f_2) = \V(f_1) \cup \V(f_2) \ &\implies \left(X \cap \V(f_1) ight) \cup \left(X \cap \V(f_2) ight) \ &\overset{X ext{ irreducible}}{\implies} X = X \cap \V(f_1), & i \in \{1, 2\} \ &\implies f_i \in I \ &\implies I ext{ is prime} & & \square \end{align*} \]

As \(I\) is a prime ideal, \(X eq \emptyset\).

Assume \(X = X_1 \cap X_2\) is a union of two closed subsets in X.

\[ egin{align*} &\implies X_i = X \cap \V(I_i) & I_i \ideal K[\underline x] \ &\implies X \subseteq \V(I_1) \cup \V(I_2) \eqhref{../affine_varieties/#p31}{P.3.1} \V(I_1 \cap I_2) \ &\implies I_1 \cap I_2 \subseteq \I(X) = I \ &\implies \exists\ i \in\{1, 2\} : I_i \subseteq I & ext{as } I ext{ is prime} \ &\implies X \subseteq \V(I_i) \ &\implies X = X \cap \V(I_i) = X_i && \square \end{align*} \]

Let \(\V:=\V_{\Spec R}\) and \(\I:=\I_R\) as defined in D.3.5.

\(\implies\)\(\impliedby\)

First of all, \(X\) being irreducible implies \(X eq \emptyset\) and \(\I(X) \properideal R\).

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } &a_1, a_2 \in R ext{ s.t.} \ & a_1 a_2 \in I \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} &\implies X \subseteq \V(a_1 a_2) = \V(a_1) \cup \V(a_2) \ &\implies \left(X \cap \V(a_1) ight) \cup \left(X \cap \V(a_2) ight) \ &\overset{X ext{ irreducible}}{\implies} X = X \cap \V(a_1), & i \in \{1, 2\} \ &\implies a_i \in I \ &\implies I ext{ is prime} & & \square \end{align*} \]

As \(I\) is a prime ideal, \(X eq \emptyset\).

Assume \(X = X_1 \cap X_2\) is a union of two closed subsets in X.

\[ egin{align*} &\implies X_i = X \cap \V(I_i) & I_i \ideal R \ &\implies X \subseteq \V(I_1) \cup \V(I_2) \eqhref{../affine_varieties/#p31}{P.3.1} \V(I_1 \cap I_2) \ &\implies I_1 \cap I_2 \subseteq \I(X) = I \ &\implies \exists\ i \in\{1, 2\} : I_i \subseteq I & ext{as } I ext{ is prime} \ &\implies X \subseteq \V(I_i) \ &\implies X = X \cap \V(I_i) = X_i && \square \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Decomposition into irreducibles
docs.text:
Let \(X\) be a Noetherian topological space.

a)b)c)

There are closed irreducible subsets \(Z_1, ..., Z_n \subseteq X\) with \(n \in \N_0\) and \(Z_i \subsetneq Z_j\ \forall\ i\neq j\) s.t.

\[X = \bigcup_{i=1}^n Z_i\]

If the closed irreducible \(Z_1, ..., Z_n \subseteq X\) satisfies a), then every irreducible subset \(Z\subseteq X\) is contained in a \(Z_i\).

If the closed irreducible \(Z_1, ..., Z_n \subseteq X\) satisfies a), then they are the maximal irreducible subsets of \(X\).

Moreover, they are uniquelly determined up to order.

Proof a)b)c)

As \(X\) is Noetherian, every nonempty closed subset of \(X\) contains a minimal closed subset (AoC).

Assume \(X\) has a closed subset \(Y \subseteq Y\) that is not a finite union of closed irreducible subsets. Without loss of generality, we may assume \(Y\) to be minimal.

By assumption, \(Y = Y_1 \cap Y_2\) with \(Y_1, Y_2 \subsetneq Y\) closed subsets. However, as \(Y\) is minimal, \(Y_i\) must be a finite union of closed irreducible subsets. Thus, so is \(Y\), which is a contradiction.

Thus, \(X=\bigcup_{i=1}^n Y_i\) is a finite union of closed irreducible subsets. Assuming the \(Y_i\)s to be distinct, and removeing the \(Y_i\) s.t. \(Y_i \subseteq Y_j\), we get a decomposition described in the theorem.

Let \(Z \subseteq X\) be an irreducible subset.

\[ \begin{align*} &\implies Z = \bigcup_{i=1}^n (Z \cap Z_i) \\ &\overset{Z \text{ irred.}}{\implies} \exists\ i \in \{1, ..., n\} : Z \subseteq Z_i \\ \end{align*} \]

Let \(Z\subseteq X\) be a maximal irreducible subset.

\(\begin{align*} \overset{b)}{\implies} Z \subseteq Z_i \overset{\text{maximal}}{\implies} Z=Z_i \end{align*}\)

Thus, the maximal irreducible subsets are among the \(Z_i\)s. It remains to be shown that they are the only ones:

Assume there exists a \(Z_i \subseteq Z\). As \(Z\) is irreducible, by b), \(\exists\ j\) s.t. \(Z \subseteq Z_j\). Then, from a), we get \(Z_i = Z = Z_j.\tab\square\)

docs.title: Irreducible components
docs.text: The \(Z_i\) in T.3.11 are called irreducible components.

docs.title: Minimal prime ideals
docs.text:
Let \(R\) be a Noetherian ring.

a)b)c)d) \[R \text{ has finitely many prime ideals } P_1, ..., P_n \ideal R.\] \[\text{Every prime ideal contains a minimal prime ideal.}\] \[\nil(R) = \bigcap_{i=1}^n P_i = \sqrt{\{0\}}\]

Let \(I \ideal R\) be an ideal.

\[\V_{\Spec R}(I) \text{ contains finitely many minimal elements } Q_1, ..., Q_n.\] \[\sqrt I = \bigcap_{i=1}^n Q_i\] Proof a)b)c)d)

By P.3.6e) and T.3.10b) (minimal) prime ideals in \(R\) cooresponds to (maximal) closed irreducible subsets of \(\Spec R\).

Then, by T.3.11c) there exists only finitely many minimal prime ideals. \(\tab\square\)

Follows from T.3.11b) \(\tab\square\)

From C.1.12, it follows that

\[\nil(R) = \bigcap_{P\in\Spec R}\!\!\!\!\! P\ \ \overset{a)}{=} \bigcap_{i=1}^n P_i \tab\square\]

Apply a) + c) to \(R/I \tab \square\)

docs.title: Spectra
docs.text:
Definition 3.5

Let \(S \subseteq R\) be a subset

\[\V_{\Spec R}(S) = \V(S):=\{P \in \Spec R | S \subseteq P\}\]

For \(X \subseteq \Spec R\):

\[ \begin{align*} \I_R(X) &= \I(X):= \cap X \ideal R \\ \I_R(\emptyset)&:=R \end{align*} \] Proposition 3.6 Remark: Closure Remark: Point sets may not be closed

A point \(P\in\Spec R\) is a prime ideal.

It's closure is given by

\[ \begin{align*} \overline{\{P\}} & =\V(\I\{P\}) \\ &= \V(P) \\ &= \{Q \in \Spec R | P \subseteq Q\} \end{align*} \]

Thus, we have that

\[\{P\} \text{ is closed } \iff P \text{ is maximal}\]

docs.title: Zariski-topology on \(\Spec R\)
docs.text: A subset \(X \subseteq \Spec R\) is called closed if \(X = \V(S)\) for some \(S\subseteq R\).

docs.title: Properties of \(\V_{\Spec R}\) and \(\I_R\)
docs.text:
a)b)c)d)e) Proof a)b)c)d)e) \(\subseteq\)\(\supseteq\)

Let \(P \in \V(S)\)

\[ \begin{align*} &\implies S \subseteq P \\ &\implies (S)_R \subseteq P\\ &\implies (S)_R \cap (T)_R \subseteq P\\ &\implies P \in P((S)_R \cap (T)_R)\\ \end{align*} \]

Let \(P \in P((S)_R \cap (T)_R)\)

Assume \(P \not\in \V(S)\). \(\implies \exists\ a\in S\backslash P\).

Let \(b \in T\)

\[ \begin{align*} &\implies ab \in (S)_R \cap (T)_R) \\ &\implies ab \in P \\ &\implies a \in P \lor b \in P\\ &\implies b \in P \\ &\implies T \subseteq P \\ &\implies P \in \V(T) && \square \end{align*} \]

"Trivial" tautology, by pure logic:

Let \(P \in \Spec R\)

\[ \begin{align*} P \in \bigcup_{S\in\M} \V(S) &\iff S \subseteq P & \forall S \in\M \\ &\iff \cup \M \subseteq P \\ &\iff P \in \V(\cup \M) && \square \end{align*} \]
  • Prime ideals are radical ideals.
  • Intersections of countable radicals stays radical. \(\tab\square\)

See C.1.12

Let \(X \subseteq \Spec R\) be a closed subset.

We need to show that \(\V(\I(X)) = X\).

As \(X\) is closed, we have \(X=\V(S)\) for some \(S\subseteq R\).

\[ \begin{align*} &\implies S \in P & \forall\ P \in X \\ &\implies S \subseteq \cap X = \I(X) \\ &\implies \V(\I(X)) \subseteq \V(S) = X \subseteq \V(\cap X) = \V(\I(X)) & \square \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Finite union of closed subsets
docs.text:
Let \(S, T \subseteq R\) be subsets.

\[\V(S) \cup \V(T) = \V\Big((S)_R \cap (T)_R\Big)\]

docs.title: Arbitrary intersection of closed subsets
docs.text:
For \(\emptyset \ne \M \subseteq P(R)\), a nonempty subset of the powerset:

\[\bigcap_{S\in\M} \V(S) = \V(\cup \M)\]

docs.title: Radical ideal
docs.text: \[\I(X) \ideal R \text{ is a radiacal ideal}\]

docs.title: Nullstellensatz
docs.text:
For \(I \ideal R\):

\[\I(\V(I)) = \sqrt I\]

docs.title: Bijection between radicals and closed subsets
docs.text:
There exists a unique bijection between

  • radical ideals in \(R\)
  • closed subsets of \(\Spec R\)

given by

\[ \begin{align*} I & \mapsto \V(I) \\ X &\mapsto \I(X) \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Closure
docs.text:
Let \(X \subseteq \Spec R\).

The closure \(\overline X\) is given by

\[\overline X := \V(\I(X))\]

docs.title: 8.1 Integral Closure
docs.text:
Definition 8.1

Let \(R \subseteq S\) be rings.

Let \(s \in S\). The monic

\[f(x) = x^n + a_{n-1}x^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0 \in R[x]\]

with \(f(s) = 0\) is called an integral equation for \(s\) over \(R\).

The element \(s\) is called integral over \(R\) if it has an integral equation over \(R\).

\(S\) is called integral over \(R\) if every element of \(S\) is integral over \(R\). Then, \(S\) is called an integral extension of \(R\).

Example 8.2 123

$\sqrt 2 \in \R is integral over \(\Z\).

\(\Z[\sqrt 2]\) is an integral extension of \(\Z\).

\(\frac{1}{\sqrt 2} \in \R\) is not integral over \(\Z\).

Assume it the converse

\[ \begin{align*} \frac{1}{\sqrt 2^n} + a_1 \frac{1}{\sqrt 2^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_n &= 0 \\ 1 + a_1 \sqrt 2 + \cdots + a_n \sqrt 2^n &= 0 \\ 1 + 2a_2 + 4a_4 + \cdots &= 0 \contradiction \end{align*} \]

\(s = \frac{1+\sqrt 5}{2} \in \R\) is integral over \(\Z\) as \(s^2 - s - 1 = 0\). It is also integral over \(\Z[\sqrt 5]\).

Lemma 8.3 Theorem 8.4 Corollary 8.5 Corollary 8.6 Definition 8.7

(a)

\(S'\) is called the integral closure of \(R\) in \(S\).

When \(S' = R\), \(R\) is called integrally closed in \(S\).

(b)

A ring \(R\) that is

  • an integral domain
  • integrally closed in \(\Quot(R)\)

is called normal.

(c)

The set

\[\tilde R:= \{s \in \Quot(R) \mid s \text{ is integral over } R\}\]

is called the normalization of \(R\).

(d)

Let \(X\) be an irreducible affine variety over \(K\). Then, \(X\) is normal if \(K[X]\) is normal.

(e)

An element \(s \in S\) is called almost integral over \(R\) if

\[\exists\ 0 \neq c \in R : cs^n \in R \tab \forall\ n > 0\] Proposition 8.8 Example 8.9 12

P.8.8 \(\implies \Z\) and \(K[\underline x]\) are normal.

\(R:=\Z[\sqrt{5}]\) is not normal, as \(\tilde R = \Z[\frac{1+\sqrt 5}{2}]\).

Proof

Let \(a + b \sqrt 5 \in \Q[\sqrt 5] = \Quot(\tilde R)\) be integral over \(\tilde R\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implieshref{#t84}{T.8.4} & \tilde R \text{ is integral over } \Z \\ \implieshref{#c86}{C.8.6} & a+ b \sqrt 5 \text{ is integral over } \Z \\ \\implies 2a \text{ and } a^2-5b^2 \text{ are integral over } \Z \\ \implies & 2a, a^2-5b^2 \in \Z, \tab \text{ as } \Z \text{ is normal} \\ \implies & a + b \sqrt 5 \in \tilde R \end{align*} \] Proposition 8.10 Lemma 8.11

docs.title: Integral elements and finite modules
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & R \subseteq S \text{ be rings} \\ & s \in S \end{align*}\)

TFAE:

(a) \(s\) is integral over \(R\).

(b) \(R[s] \subseteq S\) is finitely generated as an \(R\)-module.

(c) There exists an \(R[s]\)-module \(M\) with \(\Ann(M) = 0\) and \(M\) is finitely generated as an \(R\)-module.

Proof (a) \(\implies\) (b)(b) \(\implies\) (c)(c) \(\implies\) (a)

Let \(f(x) = x^n + a_{n-1}x^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0 \in R[x]\) be an integral equation for \(s\) over \(R\).

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & N:=(1, s, \ldots, s^{n-1})_R=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}Rs^i \\ & k \ge n \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies s^{k-n} f(s) &= 0 \\ s^k &= -(a_1s^{k-1} + a_2 s^{k-2} + \cdots + a_n s^{k-n}) \end{align*} \] \[ \begin{align*} \implies & s^k \in N \tab \forall\ k\\ \implies & N = R[s] \end{align*} \]

Let \(M = R[s]\)

\(\begin{align*} \implies & 1 \in M \\ \implies & \Ann(M) = \{0\} \\ \overset{b}{\implies} & M \text{ is finitely generated as an } R \text{-module} \end{align*}\)

\(\begin{align*} &M = (m_1, \dots, m_n)_R \\ &\implies \exists a_{ij} \in R : s m_i = \sum_{j=1}^r a_{ij} m_j \\ &\implieshref{../principal_ideal_thm/#l72}{L.7.2} \det(sI - A) \in \Ann(M) = \{0\} \\ &\implies \det(x I - A) \in R[x] \text{ is an integral equation for } s \end{align*}\)

docs.title: Generate by integral elements implies integral
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & R \subseteq S \text{ be rings s.t.} \\ & S = R[a_1, \dots, a_n] \end{align*}\)

TFAE:

(a) All \(a_i\) are integral over \(R\).

(b) \(S\) is integral over \(R\).

(c) \(S\) is finitely generated as an \(R\)-module.

Proof (a) \(\implies\) (c)(c) \(\implies\) (b)(b) \(\implies\) (a)

Induction on \(n\): \(n=1\) is L.8.3

Now, let \(n>1\). Then, \(S' := R[a_1, \dots, a_{n-1}]\) is finitely generated as an \(R\)-module.

\[ \implies S' = (m_1, \dots, m_r)_R = \sum_{i=1}^r Rm_i \]

\(a_n\) is integral over \(R\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implieshref{#l83}{L.8.3} & S \text{ is finitely generated as an } S' \text{-module} \\ & S = \sum_{j=1}^{l}S'n_j \\ \implies & S = \sum_{i=1}^r \sum_{j=1}^l Rm_i n_j \end{align*} \]

Let \(M=S\) be an R[a_i]-module.

\(\overset{c}{\implies} M \text{ is finitely generated as an } R \text{-module}\)

\(1 \in M\).

\(\begin{align*} \implies &\Ann(M) = \{0\} \\ \implieshref{#l83}{L.8.3} & a_i \text{ is integral over } R \end{align*}\)

Trivial.

docs.title: Integral elements form a subalgebra
docs.text:
The set

\[S':= \{s \in S \mid s \text{ is integral over } R\} \subseteq S\]

is an \(R\)-subalgebra of \(S\).

Proof

Let \(a, b \in S'\)

\(\begin{align*} &\implieshref{#t84}{T.8.4} R[a, b] \text{ is integral over } R \\ & \implies a+b, ab \text{ are integral over } R \\ \end{align*}\)

docs.title: Towers of integral extensions
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & R \subseteq S \subseteq T \text{ be rings s.t.} \\ & T \text{ is integral over } S \\ & S \text{ is integral over } R \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies T \text{ is integral over } R \] Proof

Let \(t \in T\).

\(\begin{align*} \implies &\exists\ s_1, \dots, s_n \in S : t^n + t^n + s_1 t^{n-1} + \cdots + s_n = 0 \\ \implies& t \text{ is integral over } S':=R[s_1, \dots, s_n] \subseteq S \\ \implieshref{#l83}{L.8.3} & S'[t] \text{ is finitely generated as an } S' \text{-module} \\ \implieshref{#t84}{T.8.4} & S' \text{ is finitely generated as an } R \text{-module} \\ \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} S' &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} R n_i & n_i \in S'\\ S'[t] &= \sum_{j=1}^{m} S' m_i & m_i \in S'[t] \\ &= \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sum_{j=1}^{m} R n_i m_j \end{align*} \]

So, \(S'[t]\) is an \(R[t]\)-module that is finitely generated as an R-module. Moreover, \(1 \in S'[t]\), so \(\Ann(S'[t]) = \{0\}\).

\(\begin{align*} & \implieshref{#l84}{L.8.3} t \text{ is integral over } R \\ & \implies T \text{ is integral over } R \end{align*}\)

docs.title: Every factorial ring is normal
docs.text:
Proof

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & \frac{a}{b} \in \Quot(R) \text{ be integral over } R \text{ s.t.} \\ & a, b \in R \text{ are coprime} \\ \end{align*}\)

\(\begin{align*} \implies & \frac{a^n}{b^n} + a_1 \frac{a^{n-1}}{b^{n-1}} + \cdots + a_n = 0 \\ & a^n + a_1 b a^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n b^n = 0 \\ \implies & b \mid a^n \\ \implies & \text{Every prime factor in } b \text{ divides } a \\ \end{align*}\)

However, by coprimality, \(b\) has no prime factors.

\(\begin{align*} \implies &b \text{ is a unit in } R \\ \implies & \frac{a}{b} \in R \end{align*}\)

So \(R\) is integrally closed in \(\Quot(R)\), and thus normal.

docs.title: Normal rings and localization
docs.text:
Let \(R\) be an integral domain.

Then, the following are equivalent:

(a) \(R\) is normal.

(b) \(U^{-1}R\) is normal \(\forall\ 0\notin U \subseteq R\) multiplicative.

(c) \(R_\m\) is normal \(\forall\ \m \in \Specmax{R}\).

Proof (a) \(\implies\) (b)(b) \(\implies\) (c)(c) \(\implies\) (a)

Let \(K:=\Quot(R)\).

\[ \implies U^{-1}R \subseteq K \land \Quot(U^{-1}R) = K \]

Let \(a \in K\) be integral over \(U^{-1}R\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \exists\ u \in U, a_1, \dots, a_n \in R \text{ s.t.} \\ & a^n + \frac{a_1}{u} a^{n-1} + \cdots + \frac{a_n}{u} = 0 \\ \implies & u^n a^n + a_1 u^{n-1} a^{n-1} + \cdots + a_n u^{n-1} = 0 \\ & \text{is an integral equation for } ua \text{ over } R \\ \implies & ua \in R, \tab \text{ as } R \text{ is normal} \\ \implies & a \in U^{-1}R \\ \implies & U^{-1}R \text{ is normal} \end{align*} \]

Trivial.

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & a \in K \text{ be integral over } R \\ & I:= \{b \in R \mid ba \in R\} \subseteq R \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies& a \text{ is integral over } R_\m & \forall\ \m \in \Specmax{R} \\ \implies & a \in R_\m \\ \implies &\exists\ b \in I\setminus \m \\ \implies &I=R \\ \implies & a \in R \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Almost integral elements
docs.text:
Let \(R\) be an integral domain.

(a)

Let \(s \in \Quot(R)\) be integral over \(R\).

\[ \implies s \text{ is almost integral over } R \]

(b)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & R \text{ be Noetherial as well} \\ & s \in \Quot(R) \text{ be almost integral over } R \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies s \text{ is integral over } R \] Proof (a)(b) \[ \begin{align*} s \text{ is integral over } R \iffhref{#l83}{L.8.3} & R[s] \text{ is finitely generated as an } R \text{-module} \\ \implies & \exists\ 0 \neq c \in R : cf \in R \tab \forall\ f \in R[s] \\ \implies & c s^n \in R \tab \forall\ n > 0 \end{align*} \]

Let \(s \in \Quot(R)\) be almost integral over \(R\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies& \exists\ 0 \neq c \in R : cs^n \in R \tab \forall\ n > 0 \\ \implies& R[s] \subseteq c^{-1}R \subseteq \Quot(R), \tab c^{-1}R \text{ f.g. } R \text{-module by } c^{-1} \\ \implieshref{../../algebra–geometry_lexicon/noetherian_and_artinian_rings/7noether_rings_modules/#t210}{T.2.10} & R[s] \text{ is Noetherian anf f.g. as an } R \text{-module} \\ \iffhref{#l83}{L.8.3}& s \text{ is integral over } R \end{align*} \]

docs.title: 8.2 Lying over an going up
docs.text:
Theorem 8.12 Corollary 8.13

Let \(R \subseteq S\) be an integral extension.

\[ \implies \dim(R) = \dim(S)\] Proof \(\le\)\(\ge\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & P_0 \subsetneq \dots \subsetneq P_n \in \Spec R\ \\ & \M_{P_i} := \{Q \in \Spec S \mid R \cap Q = P_i\} \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} &\implies \exists\ Q_i \M_{P_i} : Q_0 \subsetneq \dots \subsetneq Q_n \\ &\implies \dim(R) \le \dim(S) \end{align*} \]

Let \(Q_0 \subsetneq \dots \subsetneq Q_n \subseteq Q \in \Spec S\)

\[ \begin{align*} &\implies P_i:=Q_i \cap R \in \Spec R \\ &\implies \dim(R) \ge \dim(S) \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Lying over and going up
docs.text:
\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & R \subseteq S ext{ be an integral extension} \ & P \in \Spec R \ & I \ideal S ext{ s.t. } R \cap I \subseteq P \ & \M := \{Q \in \Spec S \mid R \cap Q = P \land I \subseteq Q \} \end{align*}\)

Then, the following holds:

(a) \(\M eq \emptyset\)

(b) \( ot\exists\ Q, Q' \in \M : Q \subsetneq Q'\)

(c) \(S\) is f.g. as an \(R\)-alggebra \(\implies \M\) is finite

Proof Claim: We can assume \(I=\{0\}\)

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & S':=S/I \ & R':=R/(R\cap I) \ &P':= P/(R \cap I) \end{align*}\)

Then, by L.1.22, there exists an inclusion-preserving bijection

\[\M o \{Q' \in \Spec{S'} \mid R' \cap Q' = P'\}\]

Thus, we can assume \(I=\{0\}\) and replace all objects by their primed versions.

By P.7.12, there exists an inclusion-preserving bijection

\[\M o \Spec{S_{[P]}}\]

Thus, it remains to show that

(a) \(S_{[P]} eq \{0\}\) (b) \(\dim(S_{[P]})=0\) (c) \(|\Spec{S_{[P]}}| < \infty\)

(a)(b)(c)

Assume the converse.

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & U:=\{u + (P)_S \mid u \in R\setminus P\} \ & S_{[P]}:= U^{-1} (S/(P)_S) \end{align*}\)

As \(S eq \{0\}\), we have that \(S_{[P]} = \{0\} \implies 0 \in U\)

\[ egin{align*} S_{[P]} = \{0\} \implies & 0 \in U \ \implies & \exists\ u \in R\setminus P : u \in (P)_S \ \implies & 1 \in (P_P)_{S_P} \ \implies & \exists\ s_i \in S_P, a_i \in P_P : 1 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i a_i \end{align*} \]

Let \( ilde S:= R_P[s_1, \dots, s_n] \subseteq S_P\) be an integral extension.

\[ egin{align*} \implies& (P_P)_{ ilde S} = ilde S \iff P_P ilde S = ilde S \ \implieshref{#t84}{T.8.4} & ilde S ext{ is f.g. as an } R_P ext{-module} \ \implieshref{../principaø_idel_thm/#t73}{T.7.3} & ilde S = \{0\} \ \implies& R_P = \{0\} ab \contradiction \ \implies& S_{[P]} eq \{0\} \end{align*} \]

There exists a homomorphism

\[ egin{align*} K:=\Quot(R/P) & o S_{[P]} \ rac{a+P}{b+P} &\mapsto rac{a+(P)_S}{b+(P)_S} \end{align*} \]

so \(S_{[P]}\) is a \(K\)-algebra.

\( egin{align*} &S ext{ is integral over } R \ &\implies S_{[P]} ext{ is algebraic over } K \ & ext{Let } Q\in \Spec{S_{[P]}} \ &\implies S_{[P]}/Q ext{ is algebraic over } K \ & \implieshref{../../algebra–geometry_lexicon/hilberts_nullstellensatz/2lemma1.1/}{L.1.1(a)} S_{[P]}/Q ext{ is a field} \ &\implies \Spec{S_{[P]}} = \Specmax{S_{[P]}} \ &\implies \dim(S_{[P]}) = 0 \end{align*}\)

\(S\) is f.g. as an \(R\)-algebra

\[ egin{align*} \implies& S_{[P]} ext{ is an affine } K ext{-algebra} \ \implieshref{../krull_dim_trans_deg/#t411}{T.4.11}& | \Specmax{S_{[P]}} < \infty \ \implieshref{../../algebra–geometry_lexicon/noetherian_and_artinian_rings/6noetherian_and_artinian_properties/}{T.2.8} & |\Spec{S_{[P]}}| < \infty \end{align*} \]

docs.title: 8.3 Noether Normalization
docs.text:
Theorem 8.14 Remark 8.15

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & K \text{ be an infinite field} \\ & A=K[a_1, \dots, a_m] \text{ be an affine } K \text{-algebra} \\ &\{c_i\}_{i=1}^{n} \text{ satisfying } \href{#t814}{\text{T.8.14}} \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \exists\ &\gamma_{ij} \in K \text{ s.t.} \\ & c_i = a_i + \sum_{j=n+1}^{m} \gamma_{ij} a_j \end{align*} \] Example 8.16

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X=\V_{K^2}(x_1x_2-1) \\ & K[X] = K[x_1, x_2]/(x_1x_2-1) = K[\overline{x_1}, \overline{x_2}] \\ &c:=\overline{x_1} - \overline{x_2} \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} &\implies 0 = \overline{x_1} \overline{x_2} - 1 = \overline{x_1}^2 - c \overline{x_1} - 1 \\ &\implies K[X] \text{ is integral over } K[c] \end{align*} \]

So the inclusion \(K[c] \to K[X]\) induces the surjective morphism

\[ \begin{align*} F : X &\to K^1 \\ (\xi_1, \xi_2) &\mapsto \xi_1 - \xi_2 \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Noether Normalization
docs.text:
Let \(A eq \{0\}\) be an affine \(K\)-algebra.

\[ egin{align*} \implies \exists\ &c_1, \dots, c_n \in A ext{ algebraically independent over } K ext{ s.t.} \ & A ext{ is integral over } C:=K[c_1, \dots, c_n] \end{align*} \]

Moreover, \(n=\dim(A)\).

Proof

We have that \(A = K[a_1, \dots, a_m] \cong K[x_1, \dots, x_m]/I\).

With \(m=0\), we are done. With \(I=\{0\} \iff a_1, \dots a_m\) algebraically independent, we may choose \(c_i:=x_i + I\).

Now, assume \(I eq \{0\}\) and let \(f\in I\setminus \{0\}\).

\[\implies f = \sum_{(i_1, \dots, i_m)\in S} a_{i_1, \dots, i_m} x_1^{i_1} \cdots x_m^{i_m}, \quad S \subseteq \N_0^m \quad \alpha \in K\setminus \{0\}\]

For \(d > \deg(f)\), define the injective function

\[ egin{align*} s: S & o N_0 \ (i_1, \dots, i_m) &\mapsto \sum_{j=1}^{m} i_j d^{j-1} \end{align*} \]

Then, set \(y_i:= x_i-x_1^{d^{i-1}}\) for \(i\ge 2\).

\[ egin{align*} \implies f &= f(x_1, y_2 + x_1^d, \dots, y_m + x_1^{d^{m-1}}) \ &= \sum_{(i_1, \dots, i_m)\in S} a_{i_1, \dots, i_m} \left(x_1^{s(i_1, \dots, i_m)} + g_{i_1, \dots, i_m}(x_1, y_2, \dots, y_m) ight) \end{align*} \]

with \(\deg_{x_1}(g_{i_1, \dots, i_m}) < s(i_1, \dots, i_m)\).

As \(s\) is injective, \(\exists!\ (j_1, \dots, j_m) \in S\) s.t. \(k:=s(j_1, \dots, j_m)\) is maximal.

\( egin{align*} A eq \{0\} &\implies f otin K \ &\implies k > 0 \end{align*}\)

\( egin{align*} \implies& f = \alpha_{j_1, \dots, j_m} x_1^k + h(x_1, y_2, \dots, y_m), & \deg_{x_1}(h) < k \ \implies& x_1^k + \alpha_{j_1, \dots, j_m}^{-1} h(x_1, y_2, \dots, y_m) \in I \end{align*}\)

Let \(B:=K[y_1 + I, \dots, y_m + I] \subseteq A\)

\( egin{align*} \implies& A=B[x_1 + I] \ \implieshref{#t84}{T.8.4}& A ext{ is integral over } B \ \overset{ ext{ind}}{\implies}& \exists\ c_1, \dots, c_n \in B ext{ algebraically independent over } K[\underline c] ext{ s.t.} \ &\quad B ext{ is integral over } K[\underline c] \ \implieshref{#c86}{C.8.6}& A ext{ is integral over } K[\underline c] \ \implieshref{#c813}{C.8.13}& \dim(A) = \dim(K[\underline c]) \ \implieshref{../krull_dim_trans_deg/#c47}{C.4.7}& \dim(A) = \dim(K[x_1, \dots, x_n]) = n \end{align*}\)

docs.title: 8.4 The Hilbert Function
docs.text:
Definition 8.17

Degree

Let \(t:=x_1^{e_1} \cdots x_n^{e_n} \in K[x_1, \dots, x_n]\).

\[ \deg(t) := \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i \]

For \(f \in K[x_1, \dots, x_n]\), we define

\[\deg(f) := \max\{\deg(t) \mid t \text{ is a monomial in } f\}\]

Hilbert function

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & I \ideal K[x_1, \dots, x_n] \\ & A:=K[x_1, \dots, x_n]/I \\ & d \in \N_0 \\ & A_{\le d} := \{f + I \mid f \in K[x_1, \dots, x_n], \deg(f) \le d\} \end{align*}\)

Then, the Hilbert function of \(I\) is defined as

\[h_I(d):= \dim_K(A_{\le d})\] Remark 8.18

\(A \cong K[x_1, \dots, x_n]/I\)

\(h_A(d):=h_I(d)\) is not well defined.

Lemma 8.19

\(\begin{align*}\delta(I):=\min\{\delta \in \N_0\cup\{-1\} \mid h_I(d) \le p\in \Q[x]_{\le \delta}\}\end{align*}\)

Lemma 8.21

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & I \ideal K[x_1, \dots, x_n] \\ & J \ideal K[y_1, \dots, y_m] \text{ s.t.}\\ & A := K[x_1, \dots, x_n]/I \cong K[y_1, \dots, y_m]/J =: B \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \delta(I) = \delta(J)\] Proof

\(\begin{align*} & \phi : A \to B \text{ is an isomorphism} \\ & \implies \exists\ g_1, \dots, g_m \in K[x_1, \dots, x_n] : \phi(g_i + I) = y_i + J \end{align*}\)

Set \(k:=\max\{\deg(g_i)\}_{i=1}^{m}\).

\(\begin{align*} & \implies B_{\le d} \subseteq \phi(A_{\le kd}) \quad \forall\ d \\ & \implies h_J(d) \le \dim_K(\phi(A_{\le kd})) = h_I(kd) \\ & \implies \delta(J) \le \delta(I) \end{align*}\)

By symmetry, we have that \(\delta(I) \le \delta(J)\), and thus

\[\delta(I) = \delta(J)\] Theorem 8.22

docs.title: The Hilbert function of the zero ideal
docs.text:
For \(\{0\} \ideal K[x_1, \dots, x_n]\), we have that

\[h_{\{0\}}(d) = \binom{n+d}{n}\] Proof

For \(\{0\} \ideal A:=K[x_1, \dots, x_n]\), denote \(h_n(d):=h_{\{0\}}(d)\).

\(n=0 \implies h_0(d) = 1 = \binom{0+d}{0}\)

Now, let \(n>0\). We have that

\[ \begin{align*} &K[x_1, \dots, x_n]_{\le d} = \bigoplus_{i+j=d} K[x_1, \dots, x_n]_{\le i} \cdot x_n^j \\ &\implies h_n(d) = \sum_{i=0}^{d} h_{n-1}(i) = \sum_{i=0}^{d} \binom{n-1+i}{n-1} = \binom{n+d}{n} \end{align*} \]

docs.title: The Krull dimension and \(\delta(I)\)
docs.text:
Let \(A \cong K[x_1, \dots, x_n]/I\)

\[\implies \delta(I) = \dim(A)\] Proof

Assume without loss that \(A \neq \{0\}\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implieshref{#c814}{C.8.14} \exists\ &c_1, \dots, c_m \in A \text{ algebraically independent and } m = \dim(A) \text{ s.t.} \\ & A \text{ is integral over } C:=K[c_1, \dots, c_m] \\ \implieshref{#t84}{T.8.4} \exists\ &b_1, \dots, b_n \in C \text{ s.t.} \\ & A = \sum_{j=1}^{r} C b_j \quad (*) \\ \end{align*} \]

We may assume \(b_1 = 1\).

Let \(y_1, \dots, y_m\) and \(z_1, \dots, z_r\) be indeterminates and \(B:=K[\underline y, \underline z]\).

\(\delta(J)\ge\dim(A)\)\(\delta(J)\le\dim(A)\)

For the homomorphism

\[ \begin{align*} \phi : B:=K[\underline y, \underline z] &\to A \\ f(\underline y, \underline z) &\mapsto f(\underline c, \underline b) \end{align*} \]

let \(J:=\ker(\phi)\). Then, by L.8.21, we get \(\delta(I) = \delta(J)\).

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & B_{\le d} := \{f \in K[\underline y, \underline z] \mid \deg(f) \le d\} \\ & C_{\le d} := \{f \in K[\underline y] \mid \deg(f) \le d\} \\ \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} C_{\le d} \subseteq B_{\le d} \implies & h_J(d) \ge \dim_K(C_{\le d}) \eqhref{#l819}{L.8.19} \binom{m+d}{d} \\ \implies & \delta(J) \ge m \end{align*} \]

For \(1 \le i \le j \le r\), we have

\[b_i b_j \overset{(*)}{=} \sum_{k=1}^{r} a_{i,j,k} b_k \quad a_{i,j,k} \in C\]

and there exists \(e>0\) such that \(a_{i,j,k} \in C_{\le e}\), so

\[b_ib_j \in \sum_{k=1}^{r} C_{\le e} b_k\]

By induction, we have that

\[ \prod_{i=1}^{s} b_i \in \sum_{k=1}^{r} C_{\le (s-1(e} b_k \]

and it follows that

\[B_{\le d} \subseteq C_{\le de} \cdot b_1 + \sum_{s=1}^{d} \sum_{k=1}^{r} C_{\le d-s} \cdot C_{\le (s-1)e} \cdot b_k \subseteq \sum_{k=1}^{r} C_{\le de} \cdot b_k =: V_d\]

Then, we obtain

\[h_J(d) \le \dim_K(V_d) \le r \cdot \dim_K(C_{\le de}) = r \cdot \binom{m+de}{m}\]

so \(\delta(J) \le m\).

docs.title: Krull Dimension and Transcendence Degree
docs.text:
Definition: Length

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } &\M ext{ be a set of sets} \ & c \subseteq \M ext{ be a chain} \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} \length c & := |c| - 1 \in \N_0 \cup \{-1, \infty\} \ \length \M & := \sup_{c\subseteq \M} \left\{\length c ight\} \end{align*} \] Definition 4.1 Example 4.2 123456
  • Singletons have dimension \(\dim \{P\} = 0\)
  • The empty set has dimension \(\dim \emptyset = -1\)

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & K ext{ be an infinite field} \ & X = K^1 ext{ be the affine line} \end{align*}\)

The only closed irreducibles are \(K^1\) and the singletons.

\[\implies \dim K^1 = 1\]

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & P ext{ be a plane} \ & L ext{ be a line trhough } P \ & X = P \cup L \end{align*}\)

Let \(x \in X\) be a point. Some chains of closed irreducible subsets are

  • \(\{x\} \subsetneq L\)
  • \(\{x\} \subsetneq ext{ curve in P} \subsetneq P\)
\[\implies \dim X \ge 2\]
  • \(\dim K = 0\) as a ring
  • \(R\) is an artinian ring \(\implies \dim R \le 0 \iff \Spec R = \Specmax R\)
\[\dim \Z = 1\]

This holds for any PID that is not a field (e.g. \(K[x]\)).

Let \(R=K[x_1, x_2, ...]\) be a polynomial ring of infinite variables.

\[\dim R = \infty\] Proof

Choose \(P_i = (x_1, x_2, ..., x_i) \in \Spec R\). Then we get a chain of infinite length.

Remark

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } &X ext{ be a Noetherian topological space} \ & Z_1, ..., Z_n ext{ be it's irreducible components} \end{align*}\)

\[\dim X = \max \{\dim Z_1, ..., \dim Z_n, -1\}\]

\(X\) is called equidimensional if \(\dim Z_i = \dim Z_j\ orall\ i, j\).

Definition 4.3 Remark 4.4 a)b)

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X ext{ be an affine variety} \ & K = \overline K ext{ be an algebraically closed field} \end{align*}\)

\[ rdeg(K[X]) \ge n \iff \exists\ \phi : K[x_1, .., x_n] o K[X] ext{ an injective homomorphism}\]

This is equivalent to finding a doinant morphism \(f : X o K^n\)

For \(A=K[\underline x]/I\), the transcendence degree can calculated by Gröbner basis methods

Lemma 4.5

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & A = K[S] ext{ be an algebra over the field} K ext{generated by} \ & S \subseteq A \end{align*}\)

\[\dim A \le n:=\sup \{|T| : T \subseteq S ext{is a set of algebraically independent elements}\}\] Proof

The case \(n=\infty\) is trivially correct. Hence, assume \(n \in \N_0\).

Claim: It suffices to show that \( orall\ P \in \Spec A : \dim A/P \le n\).

Any strictly incresing chain of prime ideals in \(A\)

\[P_0 \subsetneq P_1 \subsetneq ... \subsetneq P_m\]

induces a strictly increasing chain of prime ideals in \(A/P_0\)

\[P_0/P_0 \subsetneq P_1/P_0 \subsetneq ... \subsetneq P_m/P_0.\]

Thus, if \(\dim A \le n\), then \(m \le n\). Therefore, if we can show that \(\dim A/P \le n orall\ P\in\Spec A\), then we get that \(\dim A \le n\).

Claim: Substituting \(A\) by \(A/P\) and \(S\) by \( ilde S:=\{a + P | a \in S\}\) does not increase \(n\).

Let \(P \in\Spec A\) and \( ilde T \subseteq ilde S\). Then \( ilde T\) can be written as

\[ ilde T=\{t_1 + P, ..., t_k + P | t_1, ..., t_k \in A\}\]

Then, for \(T=\{T_i\}_{i=1}^k\) we have that \(| ilde T| \le |T|\). In particular, we have that

\[ ilde n:=\sup\{| ilde T| : ilde T\subseteq ilde S ext{ is finite and algebraically independent}\}\le n \] Claim: We may assume w.l.o.g that \(A\) is an integral domain

For a prime ideal \(P\in\Spec A\), the quotient ring \(A/P\) is an integral domain. If the statement holds for all integral domains, it must hold for all \(A/P\), and thus for \(A\), which may not be an integral domain.

Induction on \(n\):

\(n=0\)\(n>0\)

Then \(S\) is algebraic over \(K\).

\[ egin{align*} &\implies \Quot(A) ext{ is algebraic over } K \ &\implies A ext{ is algebraic over } K \ &\implieshref{../../algebra–geometry_lexicon/hilberts_nullstellensatz/2lemma1.1}{L.1.1a)} A ext{ is a field} \ &\implies \dim A = 0 & \square \end{align*} \]

Let \(P_0 \subsetneq P_1 \subsetneq ... \subsetneq P_m\) be a chain of primes in \(A\). We need to show that \(m\le n\).

The chain \(P_1/P_1 \subsetneq P_2/P_1 \subsetneq ... \subsetneq P_m/P_1\) in \(A/P_1\) has length \(m-1\).

Claim: If the subset \( ilde T\subset ilde S\) is algebraically independent in \(A/P_1\), then \(|T| \le n-1\).

\( egin{align*} ext{Assume } &\exists\ a_1, ..., a_n \in S ext{ s.t.} \ & a_i + P_1 ext{ is independent in } A/P_1 \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} \implies &a_1, ..., a_m ext{ are linearly independent in } A \ \overset{ ext{Def of }n}{\implies} & egin{cases} ext{ all elements of } S ext{ are algebraic over } \ L:=\Quot(K[\underline a]) \end{cases} \ \implies &\Quot(A) ext{ is algebraic over } L \end{align*} \]

Pick any \(0 eq a\in P_1\). Then, \(a\) is algebraic over \(L\), i.e

\[ egin{align*} \exists\ 0 eq &g\in L[x] \ & g = \sum_{i=0}^k g_i x^i & ext{s.t. } g(a) = 0 \end{align*} \]

Without loss of generality, we may assume \(g_0 eq 0\) and \(g_i \in K[\underline a]\)

\[ egin{align*} \implies &g_0 = -\sum_{i=1}^k g_i a^i \in P_1 \ \implies & g_0(a_1 + P_1, ..., a_n + P_1) = 0_{A/P_1} \contradiction \end{align*} \]

Then, we have that \(\dim A/P_1 \le n-1\), and by induction:

\[ egin{align*} m-1 &\le n-1 \ m & \le n & \square \end{align*} \] Theorem 4.6 Corollary: 4.7 Example 4.8

Let \(A=K(x_1, ..., x_n)\) be a rational function field.

\[ \implies \dim A = 0 \le n = rdeg A\] Theorem 4.9 Proposition 4.10 Theorem 4.11 Proposition 4.12 Theorem 4.13 Lemma 4.14

docs.title: Krull dimension
docs.text:
Topological spaceRingPointset

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a topological space} \\ & \M \text{ be the set of all closed irreducible subsets of } X \end{align*}\)

\[\dim X := \length \M\] \[\dim R := \dim\left(\Spec R\right) = \length{\Spec R}\]

Let \(X \subseteq K^n\) be a pointset.

\[\dim X \text{ is the dimension of } X \text{ with the Zariski topology}\] Remark

For \(K = \overline K\), and \(X\) an affine variety:

\[\dim X = \dim\left(K[X]\right)\] Remark

For a vector space \(V\) and \(\M\) the set of suspaces, we have

\[\dim V = \length \M\]

docs.title: Transcendence Degree
docs.text:
Let \(A\) be an algebra over a field \(K\).

\[ \begin{align*} \trdeg A &:= \sup \{|T| : T \subseteq A \text{ is finite and algebraically independent}\} \\ \trdeg \{0\} & := -1 \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Dimension of algebras, upper bound
docs.text:
Let \(A\) be a \(K\)-algebra.

\[ \implies \dim A \le \trdeg A\] Proof

Follows directly from L.4.5, using \(S=A\).

docs.title: Dimension of a polynomial ring
docs.text:
\(K[\underline x]\)\(K^n\) \[\dim(K[x_1, ..., x_n]) = n\] \[\dim(K^n) = \begin{cases}n, & K \text{ is an infinite field} \\ 0, & \text{otherwise}\end{cases}\] Proof \(K[\underline x]\)\(K^n\)

Let \(S:=\{x_i\}_{i=1}^n\). Then it follows from L.4.5 that

\[\dim(K[\underline x]) \le |S| = n\]

Moreover, the chain given by

\[\{0\} \subsetneq (x_1) \subsetneq ... \subsetneq (S)\]

has length \(n\) in \(\Spec{K[\underline x]}\). Thus, we have that

\[\dim(K[\underline x] \ge n\]

Every chain of closed irreducibles gives rise to a chain of primes in \(K[x_1, ..., x_n].\) This chain cannot have length greater than \(n\), yielding

\[\dim K^n \le n\]

If \(|K|=\infty\), chains of subspaces provode closed irreducibles, yielding

\[\dim K^n \ge n\]

If instead \(K\) is finite and discrete, the dimension is trivially \(0\).

docs.title: Dimension and transcendence degree
docs.text:
Let \(A\) be an affine algebra.

\[ \implies \dim A = \trdeg A\] Proof

See proof of P.4.10.

docs.title: Calculating the transcendence degree
docs.text:
Let \(A=K[S]\) be an affine \(K\)-algebra

\[ \implies rdeg A = \sup \{|T| : T \subseteq S ext{ is finite and algebraically independent}\}\] Proof

By L.4.5, we have that

\[ egin{align*} \dim A &\le \sup \{|T| : T \subseteq S ext{ is finite and algebraically independent}\} \ & \le rdeg A \end{align*} \]

It remains to be shown that \( rdeg A \le \dim A\).

We will do this with induction on \(n\), with the induction hypothesis being

\[ rdeg A \ge n \implies \dim A \ge n\]

This is trivial for \(n=0\).

For \(n>0\), we have that there exists \(\{a_i\}_{i=1}^n \subseteq A\) being algebraically independent.

By C.2.12, \(A\) is Noetherian. Hence, by C.3.14a) there exists only finitely many minimal ideals \(M_1, ..., M_r\) in \(A\).

Claim: \(\{a_j/M_i\}_{j=1}^n \subseteq A/M_i\) is algebraically independent. \[ egin{align*} \exists\ 0 eq f_i & \in K[\underline x] ext{ s.t.} \ f_i(\underline a) & \in M_i \end{align*} \]

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & f:=\prod_{i=1}^r f_i eq 0 \ & \a := f(\underline a) \in igcap_{i=1}^{r} M_i = il A \end{align*}\)

This implies there exists some \(k\) such that

\[\a^k = f(\underline a)^k = \prod_{i=1}^{r} f_i(\underline a)^k = 0 \contradiction\]

This is a contradiction, as \(\{a_i\}_{i=1}^n\) are algebraically independent. Thus, \(\{a_j/M_i\}_{j=1}^n\) is algebraically independent.

We have that

\[\dim(A/M_i) \ge n \implies \dim(A) \ge n,\]

so we may assume \(A\) to be an integral domain.

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & L:=\Quot(K[a_1]) \subseteq \Quot(A) \ & A':= L \cdot A \subseteq \Quot(A) ext{ be an affine } L ext{-domain}. \end{align*}\)

Thus, \(a_2, ..., a_n \in A'\) are algebraiclly independent over \(L\). By induction, \(\dim A' \ge n-1\), yielding a chain

\[P_0' \subsetneq P_1' \subsetneq \subsetneq ... \subsetneq P_{n-1}'\]

in \(\Spec{A'}\). Let \(P_i:=P_i' \cap A \in \Spec A\). Then, \(P_i \subseteq P_{i+1}\). As \(L \cdot P_i = P_i'\), the inclusions are strict.

Claim: \(a_1 + P_i \in A/P_i\) is transcendental.

Assume it is not.

\[ egin{align*} & \implies egin{cases} \exists &0 eq f \in K[x] ext{ s.t.} \ & 0 eq f(a_1) \in P_i \end{cases} \ & \implies f(a_1) \in P_i' \subseteq L \ & \implies 1 = rac{f(a_1)}{f(a_1)} \in P_i' \contradiction \end{align*} \]

Thus, \(A/P_i\) is not algebraic

\[ egin{align*} & \implies A/P_{n-1} ext{ is not algebraic}\ & \implieshref{../../algebra–geometry_lexicon/hilberts_nullstellensatz/2lemma1.1/}{L.1.1b)} A/P_{n-1} ext{ is not a field} \ & \implies P_{n-1} ext{ is not a maximal ideal} \ & \implies egin{cases} \exists & P_n \in \Spec A ext{ s.t.} \ & P_{n-1} \subsetneq P_n \end{cases} \ & \implies \exists ext{ a chain of length } n \end{align*} \]

docs.title: 0-dimensional affine algebras
docs.text:
Let \(A\) be an affine \(K\)-algebra.

Then, the following are equivalent.

  1. \(\dim A = 0\)
  2. \(A\) is algebraic
  3. \(\dim_K(A) < \infty\) as a \(K\)-vector space
  4. \(A\) is Artinian
  5. \(|\Specmax A| < \infty\)
Proof \((1) \implies (2)\)\((2) \implies (3)\)\((3) \implies (4)\)\((4) \implies (5)\)\((5) \implies (1)\) \[ \begin{align*} (1) & \implieshref{#t49}{T.4.9} \trdeg A = 0 \\ & \implies \text{ There are no algebraically independent elements} \\ & \implies A \text{ is algebraic} \end{align*} \]

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & a_1, ..., a_n \in A \text{ be the generators of } A = K[a_1, ..., a_n] \\ & 0 \neq g_i \in K[x_i] \text{ s.t. } g_i(a_i) = 0 \end{align*}\)

Then, \(A\) is generated by

\[S = \left\{\prod_{i=1}^{n} a_i^{e_i} : 0 \le e_i < \deg g_i\right\}\]

as a \(K\)-space. Thus, \(\dim_K(A) < \infty\) as \(|S| < \infty\).

The subspaces of \(A\) satisfies dcc. Therefore, so do the ideals.

By T.2.8, \(A\) is Noetherian and \(\Spec A = \Specmax A\).

Moreover, by C.3.14a), \(\Spec A\) is finite. Hence, \(\Specmax A\) is finite.

As \(A\) is an affine \(K\)-algebra, it is a Jacobson-ring.

Let \(P \in \Spec A\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & P \text{ is an intersection of maximal ideals} \\ \overset{(5)}{\implies} & P \text{ is a finite intersection of maximal ideals}\\ \implies & P \text{ is a maximal ideal} \\ \implies & \Spec A = \Specmax A \\ \implies & \dim A = 0 \end{align*} \]

docs.title: 0-dimensional pointsets
docs.text:
Let \(X \subseteq K^n\) be a nonempty subset.

\[\dim X = 0 \iff |X| < \infty\] Proof \(\implies\)\(\impliedby\)

As the dimention of \(X\) is 0, \(X\) is a Noetherian subspace. Then, it has a finite set of irreducible components \(\{Z_i\}_{i=1}^r\).

Choose any \(x_i\in Z_i\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & \{x_i\} \subseteq Z_i \\ \overset{\dim=0}{\implies} &Z_i = \{x_i\} \\ \implies &X = \{x_1, ..., x_r\} \end{align*} \]

Let \(X = \{x_1, ..., x_r\}\) be finite. Then, chains are of length 0. Thus, the dimension is 0.

docs.title: Hypersurfaces
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & I \properideal K[x_1, ..., x_n] \\ & A:= K[x_1, ..., x_n]/I \end{align*}\)

Then, the following are equivalent.

  1. \(\Spec A\) is equidimensional of dimension \(n-1\).
  2. Every \(P\in\Spec{K[\underline x]}\) that is minimal over \(I\) is also minimal among nonzero primes. (Height 1)
  3. \(\sqrt I = (g)\) is a prime ideal.
Proof

Let \(\M = \{P \in\Spec{K[\underline x]} | P \text{ minimally oer } I\}\). Then, \(\M\) is finite.

Note that \(A\) is finitely generated, and thus Noetherian.

\((1) \implies (2)\)\((2) \implies (3)\)\((3) \implies (1)\)

The minimal primes of \(A\) are the \(P/I\) for \(P\in\M\). We have from L.1.22 that \(K[\underline x]/P \cong A/(P/I)\). Thus

\[\dim(A/(P/I)) = \dim(K[\underline x]/P) = n-1 \tab \forall P\in\M\]

which implies \(P\neq\{0\}\).

Assume P is not minimal among the zero ideal, i.e. \(\exists Q\in\Spec{K[\underline x]}\) s.t. \(\{0\} \subsetneq Q \subsetneq P\).

Then, there exists a chain of length \(n+1\), which is a contradiction to C.4.7.

For all \(P\in \M\), let \(g_P\) be a generator of \(P\).

Then, by L.3.14d, we have that

\[\sqrt I = \bigcap_{P\in\M} P = \bigcap_{P\in\M} (g_P) = (g), \tab g=\prod_{P\in\M} g_P\]

We have that

\[g=\prod_{i=1}^{r} g_i\]

with \(g_i\) irreducible and

\[\sqrt I = \bigcap_{i=1}^{r} (g_i)\]

Then, by minimality, we get that \(\M \subseteq \{(q_i)\}_{i=1}^r\)

Using T.4.9 + P.4.10 on \(x_i\), we get that \(x_1, ..., x_n\) are algebraically dependent, as \(g_i(\underline x) \equiv 0 \mod q_i\). Thus, \(\dim(K[\underline x]/(q_i)) < n\).

Let \(x_j\) be a variable occuring in \(g_i\). Then \(\{x_i\}_{i\neq j}\) is algebraically independent modulo \(g_i\). Hence, \(\dim(K[\underline x]/(g_i)) = n-1\) by T.4.9 + P.4.10.

docs.title: Height-one prime ideals in a factorial ring
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & R \text{ be a factorial ring} \\ & P \in \Spec R \text{ be minimal among nonzero primes} \\ \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies P = (a) \tab \text{ for some prime element } a\in R\] Proof

Let \(a\in P\) be a prime element. Then, \(\{0\} \subsetneq P \subseteq (a)\).

Then \((a)=P\), as \(P\) is minimal among nonzero primes.

docs.title: Localization
docs.text:
Definition: 5.1

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & M \text{ be an } R \text{-module} \\ & U \subseteq M \text{ be a submonoid of } (R, \cdot) \\ \end{align*}\)

\(U\) is called multiplicative subset.

Define a relation on \(U \times M\) by

\[(u_1, m_1) \sim (u_2, m_1) \iff \exists\ u\in U : u u_2 m_1 = u u_1 m_2\] Claim: This is an equivalence relation Notation \[[(u, m)]_\sim =: \frac{m}{u}\] Example 5.2 1234567

Let \(R\) be an integral domain, \(U:=R\setminus\{0\}\). Then, \(U^{-1}R:=\Quot(R)=R_{\{0\}}\) is the localization of \(R\) at \(\{0\}\).

Let \(U:=\{u \in R | u \text{ is not a zero-divisor} \}\)

Then, \(U^{-1}R\) is called the total ring of fractions of \(R\).

Moreover, $ \varepsilon$ is injective. This is the largest set such that \(\varepsilon\) is injective.

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & R=\Z \\ & P=(2)\in\Spec R \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies R_P = \Z_{(2)} = \text{ the ring of rational numbers with odd denominators}\]

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & R:=\Z/(6) \\ & P=(2)\in\Spec R \\ & U:=R\setminus P \end{align*}\)

Then, we have \(\frac{\overline 2}{\overline 1} = \frac{\overline 0}{\overline 1}\), as \(\overline 3\ \overline 1\ \overline 2 = \overline 3\ \overline 1\ \overline 0\).

We obtain \(R_P \cong \mathbb F_2\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \subseteq X^n \text{ be an affine variety}\\ & R = K[X] \\ & x \in X \\ & \implies m_x \in \Specmax{K[X]} \end{align*}\)

Denote \(K[X]_x:=K[X]_{m_x}\) the localization at \(x\).

Elements are fractions of regular functions such that the denominator does not vanish at \(x\).

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & a \in R \\ & U:=\{a^i | i\in \N_0\} \end{align*}\)

Denote \(M_a:=U^{-1}M\)

Assume \(0\in U\)

\[ \implies U^{-1} M = \{0\}\] Proposition 5.3 Corollary 5.4

Let \(M\) be a Noetherian \(R\)-module.

Then, \(U^{-1} M\) is a Noetherian \(U^{-1} R\)-module.

Theorem 5.5 Remark

Special case:

Let \(U=R\setminus P\) for \(P \in \Spec R\).

Then, there is a bijection between \(A=\{Q \in \Spec R | Q \subseteq P\} \leftrightarrow \Spec{R_P}\)

Corollary 5.6 Definition: 5.7 Corollary 5.8

Let \(P \in \Spec R\).

Then, \(R_P\) is a local ring with maximal ideal \(P_P\).

Example 5.9
  1. Fields are local rings.
  2. \(K[x]/(x^n)\) is a local ring with maximal ideal \((x)/(x^n)\).
  3. \(K\llbrace x \rrbrace\) is a local ring with maximal ideal \((x)\).
  4. \(R=\{0\}\) is not local.
Definition: 5.10 Remark 5.11

Let \(P \in \Spec R\).

  • $\dim(R/P) is the maximal of the lengths of chains of prime ideals in \(R\) starting in \(P\).
  • \(\ht(P)\) is the maxiaml of the lengths of chains of prime ideals in \(R\) ending in \(P\).

Thus, \(\ht(P) + \dim(R/P) = \dim R\).

We often have equality (f.e. for affine domains).

Example 5.12 123 \[P \in\Spec R \text{ minimal} \iff \ht(P) = 0\]

Let \(x=(\xi_1, \dots, \xi_n) \in K^n\).

This forms a chain

\[\{0\} \subsetneq (x_1-\xi_1) \subsetneq \dots \subsetneq m_x\]

implying \(\ht(m_x) \geq n\).

Moreover, \(n=\dim(K[\underline x]) \ge \ht(m_x)\).

\[ \implies \ht(m_x) = n\]

Let \(X=Z_1 \cup Z_2 \subseteq K^n\) be an affine variety, s.t. \(\dim Z_1 < \dim Z_2\), and \(K=\overline K\).

Let \(x\in Z_1\setminus Z_2\).

Then, chains of closed irreducibles in \(X\) startong with \(\{x\}\) lies lies entirely in \(Z_1\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies &\ht(m_x) \text{ is the maximal length of such a chain} \\ \implies &\ht(m_x) \leq \dim Z_1 \\ \end{align*} \]

Moreover, \(\dim(K[X]/m_x) = 0\).

\[ \implies\dim(m_x) + \ht(m_x) \le \dim(Z_1) < \dim(X) = \dim(K[X])\] Definition 5.12

docs.title: Localization
docs.text:
\(U^{-1}M:=\{\frac{m}{u} | m\in M, u\in U\}\) is the localization of \(M\) with respect to \(U\).

\(U^{-1} M\) becomes an \(R\)-module with operations

\[ \begin{align*} \frac{m_1}{u_1} + \frac{m_2}{u_2} &:= \frac{u_2 m_1 + u_1 m_2}{u_1 u_2} \\ r \frac{m}{u} &:= \frac{r m}{u} \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Special case:
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & P\in\Spec R \\ & U:=R\setminus P \\ \end{align*}\)

Then, \(M_P:=U^{-1}M\) is called the localizer at \(P\).

docs.title: Canonical map
docs.text:
We have a map

\[ \begin{align*} \varepsilon : M &\to U^{-1}M \\ m &\mapsto \frac{m}{1} \end{align*} \]

which is a homomorphism of \(R\)-modules. Moreover, \(\Ker (\varepsilon) = \{m \in M | \exists\ u\in U : u m = 0\}\).

docs.title: Properties of localization
docs.text:
a)b)c)d)e)f)g)h)i)

\(U^{-1} R\) becomes a ring with operations

\[ \frac{a_1}{u_1} \frac{a_2}{u_2} = \frac{a_1 a_2}{u_1 u_2}\]

\(\varepsilon\) is a ring homomorphism.

\(\implies U^{-1} R\) is an \(R\)-algebra.

\(U^{-1}M\) is a \(U^{-1}R\)-module with operations

\[\frac{a_1}{u_1} \frac{m_1}{u_2} + \frac{a_2}{u_2} \frac{m_2}{u_2} = \frac{a_1 u_2 m_1 + a_2 u_1 m_2}{u_1 u_2^2}\]

\(\varepsilon(U) \subseteq (U^{-1} R)^\times\)

\((\frac{u}{1})^{-1} = \frac{1}{u}\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } \phi : & R \to S \text{ be a ring homomorphism s.t.} \\ & \phi(U) \subseteq S^\times \end{align*}\)
