Lower box dimensionUpper box dimensionBox dimension \[\underline{\dim}_B M := \liminf_{\varepsilon \to 0^+} \frac{\log N(\varepsilon)}{\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon}}\] \[\overline{\dim}_B M := \limsup_{\varepsilon \to 0^+} \frac{\log N(\varepsilon)}{\log \frac{1}{\varepsilon}}\] \[\underline{\dim}_B M = \overline{\dim}_B M\] Remark a)b)

If \(c_2 \le \varepsilon^d \le N(\varepsilon) \le c_1 \varepsilon^{-d}\)

\[\implies \dim_B M = d\]

Let \(M \subseteq \R^n\) be a bounded subset.

\[\dim_H M \le \dim B M\]

docs.title: 1.2 Iterated Function System
docs.text:
Definition: Iterated Function System (I.F.S.)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Choose } &1 < N \in \N \text{Let } &(X, d) \text{ be a complete metric space} \\ & \F:= \{f_i\}_1^N \text{ be contractions on } X \end{align*}\)

\[(X, F) \text{ is an iterated function system}\] Definition: Hyperspace of compact subsets of \(X\)

Let \((X, d)\) be a complete metric space.

The collection \(\H(X)\) of all nonempty compact subsets of \(X\) is called the hyperspace of compact subsets of \(X\).

Definition: Hausdorff measure

Let \((X, d)\) be a complete metric space.

\[ \begin{align*} h : \H(X)^2 & \to \R \\ (A, B) & \mapsto \max\left{\ \max_{a\in A} \min_{b\in B} d(a, b), \max_{b\in B} \min_{a\in A} d(a, b), right\} \end{align*} \] Proposition \((1)\)\((2)\) \[h \text{ is a metric on } \H(X)\] \[(\H(X), h) \text{ is a complete metric space}\] Lemma 1

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & f \text{ be a contraction on } X \\ & L:= \Lip f < 1 \end{align*}\)

\[\implies h\left(f(A), f(B)\right) \le L h(A, B)\] Proof

It is well defined:

\(\begin{align*} &f \text{ is a contraction} \\ &\implies f \text{ is continuous on } X \\ & \implies \text{ images of compact sets are compact} \end{align*}\)

The rest follows easily (but cumbersome) by the use of the definition of \(h\)

Lemma 2

Let \(A, B, C \in \H(X)\)

\[h(A \cup B, C) \le \max\left\{h(A, C), h(B, C)\right\}\] Definition: Barnsley-Hutchinson operator \[ \begin{align*} \F : \H(X) & \to \H(X) \\ A & \mapsto \bigcup_{i=1}^N f_i(A) \end{align*} \] Lemma 3

Let \(\F\) be the Barnsley-Hutchinson operator.

\[\implies \Lip \F \le \max_i \Lip f_i\] Theorem

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & (X, d) \text{ be a complete metric space} \\ & (X, \F) \text{ be an IFS on } X \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \exists\ \F : \H(X) & \to \H(X) \\ A & \mapsto \bigcup_{f \in \F} f(A) \end{align*} \]

with \(\Lip \F \le \max_{f\in\F} \Lip f < 1\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implieshref{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Banach_fixed-point_theorem}{B.F.T.} \exists!\ \A &\in \H(X) \text{ s.t.} \\ \A & = \F(\A) \end{align*} \] Definition: Attractor of the IFS

The pixed point \(\A = \F(\A) \in \H(X)\) is called

  • the attractor of the IFS \((X, \F)\)
  • the fractal (set) generated by \((X, \F)\)

docs.title: 1.3 Approximation by fractals
Similarity transformation
docs.text:
A map

\[ \begin{align*} S : \ & \R^n \to \R^n \text{ s.t.}\\ & \|S(x) - S(y)\| = s \|x-y\| \tab \forall x, y \in \R^n, s\ge 0 \end{align*} \]

is called a similarity (transformation). \(s\) s the similarity constant.

For \(s < 1\), \(S\) is a contractive similitude.

docs.title: Open set condition (OSC)
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & \emptyset \neq X \subseteq \R^n \\ & (X, \F) \text{ be an IFS of similitudes.} \end{align*}\)

The IFS satisfies the open set condition if

\[ \begin{align*} \exists\ &\emptyset \neq G \overset{open}{\subseteq} \R^n \text{ s.t.} \\ &\bigsqcup_{i=1}^N f_i(G) \subseteq G \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Completeness of the code space
docs.text:
Let the metric \(d_F : \S_N^2 \to \R\) be given by

\[d_F(\s, \tau):=\sum_{i=1}^\infty \frac{|\s_i-\tau_i|}{(N+1)^i}\]

\(\left(\S_N, d_F\right)\) forms a complete metric space.

docs.title: Address set
docs.text:
Let \(a \in \A\). The address set of \(a\) is

\[\gamma^{-1}(a)\]

Each element of \(\gamma^{-1}(a)\) is called an address of \(a\).

docs.title: Totally disconnected IFS
docs.text:
An IFS is called totally disconnected if \(\forall a \in \A\)

\[|\gamma^{-1}(a)|=1\]

docs.title: Maximum of the address set
docs.text:
Let \(a\in\A\). \(\gamma^{-1}(a)\) has a mixaimal element with respect to \(\prec\).

Proof

Let \(\s_1\) be the first component of all elements in \(\gamma^{-1}(a)\).

Choose inductively \(\s_i\) as the smallest \(i\)-th component of elements in \(\gamma^{-1}(a)\) whose first \((i-1)\) components are \(\s_1, ..., \s_{i-1}\)

\[\implies (\s_1, ..., \s_{i-1}, ...) \text{ is the unique maxiaml point}\]

docs.title: Tops Code Space
docs.text: \[\Omega := \tau(\A) \subseteq \S_N\]

docs.title: 1.4 Code space of an IFS
docs.text:
Let \((X, \F)\) be an IFS and \(\A = \lim_{n o\infty} \F^n(A)\) for any \(A\in\H(X)\) be the attractor. Note that

\[\F^n(A) = igcup_{i_1,\ldots,i_n = 1}^N f_{i_1}\circ\cdots\circ f_{i_n}(A)\]

To simplify the notation, we define the code space of the IFS:

Code space of an IFS

The code space of an IFS \((X, \F)\) is the set of all sequences of indices

\[\Sigma_N := \{1, \dots, N\}^{\N}\]

Elements \(\sigma, au, \dots\) are codes

\[\sigma = (\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \dots)\]

with \(\sigma_i\in \{1, \dots, N\}\). Denote the first \(n\) elements of \(\sigma\) by

\[\sigma(n) := (\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \dots, \sigma_n)\]

Then, we can denote

\[f_{\sigma(n)} := f_{\sigma_1}\circ\cdots\circ f_{\sigma_n}\]

and

\[ egin{align*} \F^n(A) &= igcup_{\sigma\in\Sigma_N} f_{\sigma(n)}(A)\ \A &= \lim_{n o\infty} igcup_{\sigma\in\Sigma_N} f_{\sigma(n)}(A) \ A_{\sigma(n)} &:= f_{\sigma(n)}(A) \end{align*} \]

Furthermore, \(\Sigma_N\) can be made into a metric space by the Fréchet metric:

\[d_F(\sigma, au) := \sum_{i=1}^\infty rac{|\sigma_i - au_i|}{(N+1)^i}\] Theorem 4

Let \((X, \F)\) be an IFS with an attractor \(\A\).

Then, the following map is a continuous surjection:

\[ egin{align*} \gamma : \Sigma_N & o \A \ \sigma &\mapsto \lim_{n o\infty} f_{\sigma(n)}(x) \end{align*} \]

where the limit is independent of \(x\in X\).

Proof

By problem set 3, \((\Sigma, d_F)\) is a compact metric space.

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & x, y \in X \ & L := \max_{i=1, \dots, N} \Lip(f_i) \end{align*}\)

\[\implies |f_{\sigma(n)}(x) - f_{\sigma(n)}(y)| \leq L^n|x-y|\]

Assume w.l.o.g that \(X\) is compact (Let \(K\in\H(X)\) and define \(\hat K:=\clos_{\H(X)} igcup_{i\in\N_0}\F^i(K)\)).

ExistenceContinuitySurjectivity

Then, we get

\[ egin{align*} |f_{\sigma(n)}(x) - f_{\sigma(n)}(y)| &\leq L^n \diam(X) \ |f_{\sigma(n)}(x) - f_{\sigma(m)}(y)| &\leq L^{\min\{n,m\}} \diam(X) \ \end{align*} \]

Moreover, we have \(|\gamma(\sigma(n)) - \gamma(\sigma(m))| = |f_{\sigma(n)}(x) - f_{\sigma(m)}(y)|\), so the limit exists and is independent of \(x\in X\).

Let \(\varepsilon > 0\). \(\implies \exists\ n : L^n \diam(X) < \varepsilon\).

Let \(\sigma, au\in\Sigma_N\) with \(\sigma(n) = au(n)\) and \(\sigma_{n+1} eq au_{n+1}\).

\[ egin{align*} \implies & d_F(\sigma, au) = \sum_{i=n+1}^\infty rac{|\sigma_i - au_i|}{(N+1)^i} < rac{1}{(N+1)^n} \ \implies & |\gamma(\sigma) - \gamma( au)| = \lim_{n o\infty} |f_{\sigma(n)}(x) - f_{ au(n)}(x)| \le L^n \diam(X) < \varepsilon \end{align*} \]

Let \(y\in\A\).

\( egin{align*} & \implies \exists\ (\sigma_ u(m))_{ u\in\N} \subseteq \Sigma_N : \lim_{ o\infty} f_{\sigma_ u(n)}(x) = y \ \end{align*}\)

\(\Sigma_N\) is compact \(\implies \exists (\sigma_{ u_\mu})_{\mu\in\N} \subseteq (\sigma_ u)_{ u\in\N} : \sigma_{ u_\mu} o au\)

Let \(c(\mu) := \card\{ \in \N : \sigma_{ u_\mu}(j) = au(j) \}\). Then, \(c(\mu) o \infty\), yielding

\[|f_{\sigma_{ u_\mu(n)}}(x) - f_{ au(n)}(x)| \le L^{c(\mu)} \diam(X) o 0\]

so \(y=\gamma( au)\).

Remark
  1. \(\gamma\) is in general not injective (Sierpinski triangle).
  2. \( orall i eq j\in\{1,\dots,N\} : f_i(A_0) \cap f_j(A_0) = \emptyset \implies \gamma\) is injective.
  3. Let \(\sigma \in \Sigma_N, A\in\H(X)\)
\[\implies \gamma(\sigma) = igcap_{n\in\N} A_{\sigma(n)}\] Definition Definition

For \(\sigma, \omega\in\Sigma_N\), let \(i\) be the first index for which \(\sigma_i eq\sigma_i\) and define the binary operation \(\prec\) by

\[\sigma\prec\omega \iff \omega_i < \sigma_i\]

Then, \(\prec \subset \Sigma_N imes\Sigma_N\) is a strict linear order:

  1. \( orall \sigma\in\Sigma_N : \sigma prec\sigma\)
  2. \( orall \sigma, \omega, au\in\Sigma_N : \sigma\prec au \land au\prec\omega \implies \sigma\prec\omega\)

Let \(\iota := (1, 1, \dots) = \overline 1\) and \(\overline\omega := (N, N, \dots) = \overline N\). Then, \( orall\iota eq\sigma eq\overline\omega : \iota\prec\sigma\prec\overline\omega\).

Proposition

\(\gamma^{-1}(\a)\) has a maximal element w.r.t. \(\prec\) for all \(\a\in\A\).

Proof
  1. Let \(\sigma_1\) be the smallest first component of all elements in \(\gamma^{-1}(\a)\).
  2. Choose inductively \(\sigma_i\) to be the smallest \(i\)-th component of elements in \(\gamma^{-1}(\a)\) whose first \(i-1\) components are \(\sigma_1, \dots, \sigma_{i-1}\).

Then, \(\sigma := (\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \dots)\) is the unique maximal code.

Definition

docs.title: Address set
docs.text:
1. Let \(\a\in\A\). The address set of \(\A\) is given by \(\gamma^{-1}(\A)\).
  2. Each element of \(\gamma^{-1}(\A)\) is called an address of \(\A\).
  3. \(\gamma\) is (sometimes) refered to as the address function of the IFS.

docs.title: Totally disconnected IFS
docs.text: An IFS is called totally disconnected if \(\gamma^{-1}(\a)\) is a singleton for all \(\a\in\A\).

docs.title: Tops code space
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & (X, \F) \text{ be an IFS} \\ & \A &\text{ be the attractor} \\ & \gamma : \Sigma_N \to \A \text{ be the address function} \\ & \a \in \A \end{align*}\)

Define the map

\[\tau(\a) := \max\{\gamma^{-1}(\a)\} \quad \text{w.r.t. } \prec\]

The set \(\Omega := \tau(\A)\) is called the tops code space and \(\tau : \A \to \Omega\) is called the tops function for \((X, \F)\).

Remark

From the above proposition, we see that \(\tau\) is a bajection from \(\A \to \Omega\) with the inverse being \(\gamma\).

As we have seen, \(\tau^{-1} : \Omega \to \A\) is bijective and continuous.

docs.title: 2.1 Fractal Interpolation Functions
docs.text:
\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & Y:= \left\{(x_ u, y_ u) \in \R^2 : egin{matrix} a = x_0 < x_1 < ... < x_N = b \ y_ u \in \R, \ u \in \N_{0,N} \end{matrix} ight\} \ & L_i \in (-1, 1) \ &A_0:=[a, b]^2 \in \H(\R^2) \end{align*}\)

Then, there exists N affine maps \(w_i\) that maps \(A_0\) to the parallelograms \(A_i\) with vertices

\[ egin{align*} egin{bmatrix} x_{i-1} \ y_{i-1} \end{bmatrix} \quad& egin{bmatrix} x_i \ y_i \end{bmatrix}\ egin{bmatrix} x_{i-1} \ y_{i-1}+L_i(b-a) \end{bmatrix} \quad& egin{bmatrix} x_i \ y_i+L_i(b-a) \end{bmatrix} \end{align*} \]

These maps are given by

\[ w_i(x, y):= egin{bmatrix} a_i & 0 \ b_i & L_i \end{bmatrix} egin{bmatrix} x \ y \end{bmatrix} + egin{bmatrix} \alpha_i \ eta_i \end{bmatrix} \]

with

\[ egin{align*} a_i&:= rac{x_i-x_{i-1}}{b-a} \ b_i&:= rac{y_i-y_{i-1}-L_i(y_N-y_0)}{b-a} \ \alpha_i&:= rac{b x_{i-1} - a x_i}{b-a} \ eta_i&= rac{b y_{i-1} - a y_i - L_i(b-a)}{b-a} \end{align*} \]

In addition, \(\Lip(w_i)=\max\{a_i, |L_i|\}\), so the \(w_i\) are contractive. Define the IFS \(([a, b] imes\R, W)\), \(W=\{w_i\}_{i=1}^N\) with

\[W(A)= igcup_{i=1}^{N} \omega_i(A)\]

Then, there exists a unique attractor \(\G\). Barnlsey showed that \(\G\) is the graph of a continuous function

\[f : [a, b] o \R\]

that maps \(x_ u \mapsto y_ u\).

Definition: Affine Fractal Interpolation Function

Define \(f\) such that

\[ egin{align*} \left. egin{cmatrix} \overline x \ f(\overline x) \end{cmatrix} ight|_{\overline x\in[x_{i-1}, x_i]} &=\omega_i \left. egin{cmatrix} x \ f(x) \end{cmatrix} ight|_{x\in[a, b]} \ &=\omega_i \left. egin{cmatrix} a_i & 0 \ b_i & L_i \end{cmatrix} egin{cmatrix} x \ f(x) \end{cmatrix} ight|_{x\in[a, b]} + egin{cmatrix} \alpha_i \ eta_i \end{cmatrix} \end{align*} \]

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } u_i : [a, b] & o [x_{i-1}, x_i] \ x & \mapsto a_i x +´\alpha_i \ p_i : [a, b] & o \R \ x&\mapsto b_i x + eta_i \end{align*}\)

Then, for each \(\overline x\in[x_{i-1}, x_i] \ \exists!\ x \in [a, b] : \overline x = u_i(x)\), i.e. \(x = u_i^{-1}(\overline x)\). Then we get

\[ egin{align*} f(\overline x) &= p_i(x) + L_i f(x) \ &= (p_i \circ u_i^{-1})(\overline x) + L_i (f \circ u_i^{-1})(\overline x) \end{align*} \]

This yields the fixed point equation

\[f = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (p_i \circ u_i^{-1}) \chi_{[x_{i-1}, x_i)} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i(f \circ u_i^{-1}) \chi_{[x_{i-1}, x_i)}\]

Then we can define the Read-Bajraktarevic (RB) operator

\[ egin{align*} T : C[a, b] & o C[a, b] \ g &\mapsto \sum_{i=1}^{N} (p_i \circ u_i^{-1}) \chi_{[x_{i-1}, x_i)} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i(g \circ u_i^{-1}) \chi_{[x_{i-1}, x_i)} \end{align*} \]

One can show that \(Tg\) interpolates \(Y\) iff \(g\) interpolates \(Y\). Then, as \(f\) is defined to interpolate \(Y\), \(Tf\) also interpolates \(Y\)

Theorem

\(T\) is a contraction on \((C[a, b], \|\cdot\|_\infty)\) with \(L=\max |L_i|\)

Proof

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & x\in[a, b] \ & g, h \in C[a, b] \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} \implies |Tg(x) - Th(x)| &\le \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i \left|(g \circ u_i^{-1}(x) - (h \circ u_i^{-1}(x) ight| \chi_{[x_{i-1}, x_i)}(x) \ & \le L \|g-h\|_\infty \end{align*} \]

From this, we get that \(f\) is the unique fixed point of \(T\).

Example

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X = [0, 1] imes \R \ & N=2 \ & Y:=\left\{(0,0), \left( rac{1}{2}, rac{1}{2} ight), (1, 0) ight\} \end{align*}\)

12

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } u_1 : [0, 1] & o [0, 1] \ x & \mapsto rac{1}{2}x \ u_2 : [0, 1] & o [0, 1] \ x & \mapsto rac{1}{2}(x+1) \ p_1 : [0, 1] & o \R \ x & \mapsto rac{1}{2}x \ p_2 : [0, 1] & o \R \ x & \mapsto rac{1}{2}(x-1) \ & Tf(x) = egin{cases} x + rac{1}{4}f(2x), & x \in \left[0, rac{1}{2} ight) \ 1- x + rac{1}{4}f(2x-1), & x \in \left[ rac{1}{2}, 1 ight] \end{cases} \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies f^*(x) = 2x (1-x)\] \[Tf(x)= egin{cases} x+ rac{1}{2}(2x) , x \in \left[0, rac{1}{2} ight) \ 1-x+ rac{1}{2}(2x-1) , x \in \left[ rac{1}{2}, 1 ight] \end{cases} \] \[ \implies f^* ext{ is the Takagi-function}\]

docs.title: Affine Fractal Interpolation Function
docs.text: The \(f\) above is called the affine fractal interpolation function.

docs.title: 2.2 Local Fractal Functions
docs.text:
\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X ext{ be a nonempty set} \ & \{X_i\}_{i=1}^N ext{ be a family of subsets of } X \ & \{u_i : X_i o X\} ext{ bijective s.t.} \ & P:=\{u_i(X_i)\} ext{ forms a partition of } X \end{align*}\)

Now, suppose \((Y, d_y)\) is a complete metric space.

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & B(X, Y) := \{f : X o Y | f ext{ is bounded}\} \ & d(f, g) := \sup_{x \in X} d_y(f(x), g(x)) \ & \implies (B(X, Y), d) ext{ is a complete metric space} \end{align*}\)

Similarly is \((B(X_i, Y), d)\)

Definiton. Metric Linear Space

A metric linear space is a cevtor space with a metric under which the operation of vector addition and scalar multiplication are continuous.

\(B(X, Y)\) and \(B(X_i, Y)\) are metric linear spaces whenever \((Y, d_Y)\) is.

Let \( u_i : X_i imes Y o Y\) be uniformly contractive in the second variable with contractive constant \(\ell\in[0,1)\), and define the R.B. operator \(T: B(X, Y) o B(X, Y)\) by

\[Tf(x):= \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i \left( u_i^{-1}(x), f_i\circ u_i^{-1}(x) ight) \chi_{u_i(X_i)}(x)\]

with \(f_i := f|_{X_i}\)

Proposition

\(T\) is a constractive mapping on \(B(X, Y)\)

Proof

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } u(x, y):= \sum_{i=1}^{N} u_i(x, y) \chi_{X_i}(x)\end{align*}\)

Then, we get

\[ egin{align*} d(Tf, Tg) &= \sup_{x \in X} d_Y(Tf(x), Tg(x)) \ &= \sup_{x \in X} d_Y\left( u(u_i^{-1}(x), f_i\circ u_i^{-1}(x)), u(u_i^{-1}(x), g_i\circ u_i^{-1}(x)) ight) \ &\le \ell\ d(f_i\circ u_i^{-1}, g_i\circ u_i^{-1}) \ &\le \ell\ d(f, g) \end{align*} \]

So \(\exists!\ f^* \in B(X, Y)\) fixed point

Definition

\(f^*\) is called a bounded local fractal function.

Definition

Translation Invariant

\[d(x+a, y+a) = d(x, y)\]

Homogeneous

\[d(ax, ay) = |a|d(x, y)\] Remark

By Kakutani's theorem, any metric space has an equivalent translation invariant metric.

Definition

F-space

A complete metric linear space \((X, d)\) of which \(d\) is translation invariant is called an F-space.

Assume that \(Y\) is an F-space and let

\[ u_i(x,y) := \lambda_i(x) + S_i(x) - y\]

where \(\lambda_i \in B(X,Y), S\in B(X_i, \R)\). Furthermore, let \(s_i:=\|S_i\|_\infty\) and \(s:=\max\{s_i\}\). If in addition \(d_Y\) is homogeneous, we get

\[ egin{align*} d_Y(\lambda_i(x) + S_i(x) y_1, \lambda_i(x) + S_i(x) y_2) &= |S_i(x)|d_Y(y_1, y_2) \ &\le s d_Y(y_1, y_2) \end{align*} \]

so \(T\) is a contraction on $B(X;Y) if \(s < 1\). This yields the fixed point equation

\[f^* \circ u_i^{-1} = \lambda_i + S_i \cdot f^*\] Example

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X:= [0, 1] \ & \{X_i\} ext{ be a collection of appropriate subintervals of } X \ & \{x_0:=0 < x_1 < \cdots < x_N:=1\} ext{ be a partition} \ & \{u_i : X_i o [x_{i-1}, x_i]\} ext{ be a family of affine mappings} \end{align*}\)

Define the RB operator \(T : L^p(0,1) o L^p(0,1)\) by

\[Tg := \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\lambda_i \circ u_i^{-1}) \chi_{u_i(X_i)} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} (S_i \circ u_i^{-1}) \cdot (g_i \circ u_i^{-1}) \chi_{u_i(X_i)}\]

Moreover, if

\[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \|S_i\|_{\infty, X_i}^p < 1, \quad a_i=\Lip( u_i)\]

then \(T\) is a contraction on \(L^p(0,1)\).

Proof \[ egin{align*} \|Ty-Th\|_p^p &= \int_0^1 |Tg(x) - Th(x)|^p dx \ &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \int_{x_{i-1}}^{x_i} |(S_i \circ u_i^{-1})(x) \cdot (g_i \circ u_i^{-1}(x) - h_i \circ u_i^{-1}(x))|^p dx \ &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \int_{X_i} |S_i(x) \cdot (g_i(x) - h_i(x))|^p dx \ &\le \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \|S_i\|_{\infty, X_i}^p \int_{X_i} |g_i(x) - h_i(x)|^p dx \ &\le \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i \|S_i\|_{\infty, X_i}^p \|g_i - h_i\|_p^p \ \end{align*} \]

For \(X_i=X=[0,1]\) and \(|[x_{i-1}, x_i]|= rac{1}{N}\), we get the \(\ell_p\)-average:

\[ rac{1}{N} \sum_{1}^{N} \|S_i\|_{\infty}^p < 1\]

docs.title: 2.3 Basis of Fractal Functions
docs.text:
Let \(X=[0, N]\) and define

\[ egin{align*} u_i : X & o X \ x & \mapsto rac{x}{N} + i - 1 \end{align*} \]

Then, the family \(\{u_i\}_{i=1}^N\) forms a partition of \(X\): \(I_i = u_i(X) =: [x_{i-1}, x_i]\). Choose \(p_i^k \in \Pi^k[0, N]\) a polynomial of order \(k\), and fix \(L_i \in (-1, 1)\). So our function space becomes \((C[0, N], \|\cdot\|_\infty)\). Then, we define the R.B. operator

\[ egin{align*} T : C[0, N] & o C[0, N] \ f &\mapsto \sum_{i=1}^{N} p_i^k \circ u_i^{-1} \Chi_{I_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i(f \circ u_i^{-1}) \Chi_{I_i} \end{align*} \]

Then, for continuity, we require \(Tf(x_i^-) = Tf(x_i^+)\). If \(\max |L_i| < 1\), then \(T\) is a contraction on \((C[0, N], \|\cdot\|_\infty)\), so there exists a unique fixed point \(f^* \in C[0, N]\).

If \(L_i\) are fixed, then the fixed point becomes a function of the polynomials \(p_i^k\):

\[f^* = f^*(p_1^k, \ldots, p_N^k)\]

Denote this space by

\[\mathfrak F := \{f^*(p_1^k, \ldots, p_N^k) \mid p_i^k \in \Pi^k[0, N]\}\] Theorem

The map

\[ egin{align*} \Theta : (\Pi^k[0, N])^N & o \mathfrak F^k[0, N] \ \end{align*} \]

is a linear isomorphism.

Proof LinearityInjectivitySurjectivity

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & p^k, q^k \in (\Pi^k[0, N])^N \ & \alpha \in \R \end{align*}\)

Then, \(\alpha p^k + q^k \in (\Pi^k[0, N])^N\), and

\[ egin{align*} (1) && f^*(\alpha p^k + q^k) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\alpha p_i^k + q_i^k) \circ u_i^{-1} \Chi_{I_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i(f^*(\alpha p^k + q^k) \circ u_i^{-1}) \Chi_{I_i} \ (2) && \alpha f^*(p^k) + f^*(q^k) &= \alpha T f^*(p^k) + T f^*(q^k) \ &&&= T(\alpha f^*(p^k) + f^*(q^k)) \ &&& \cdots \ &&& = \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\alpha p_i^k + q_i^k) \circ u_i^{-1} \Chi_{I_i} + \sum_{i=1}^{N} L_i f^*(\alpha p_i^k + q_i^k) \circ u_i^{-1} \Chi_{I_i} \end{align*} \]

So \((1) = (2)\), and by the uniqueness of the fixed point, \(f^*(\alpha p^k + q^k) = \alpha f^*(p^k) + f^*(q^k)\).

\[f^*(p^k) = 0 \iff p^k = 0\]

Let \(f^* \in \mathfrak F^k[0, N]\), and focus on \(I_i\):

\[f^* \circ u_i = p_i^k + L_i f^*\]

So, let \(p_i^k := f^* \circ u_i - L_i f^*\). Then, \(f^* = f^*(p_1^k, \ldots, p_N^k)\).

Example: Special case for \(k=2\)

Let \(Y=\{( u, y_ u) \mid u \in \{1, \dots, N\}\}\). Then, \(p^2 \in (\Pi^2[0, N])^N\) depends uniquely on \(Y\):

  • \(N+1\) interpolation points
  • \(N\)-tuple of affine functions: \(2N\) parameters, \(N-1\) join-up conditions
  • \(2N-(N-1) = N+1\) free parameters

So, \(\dim \mathfrak F^2[0, N] = N+1\).

Proposition

Each \(f^* \in \mathfrak F^2[0, N]\) can be written uniquelly in the form

\[f^* = \sum_{ u = 0}^{N} y_ u e_ u^*\]

where \(e_ u^* \in \mathfrak F^2[0, N]\) s.t. \(e_ u^*( u) = 1\) and \(e_ u^*(\mu) = 0\).

docs.title: 2.4 The fractal Operator
docs.text:
Let \(I := [x_0, x_N]\) and \(f \in C(I)\). Define the space of continuous functions with the same endpoints as \(f\) by

\[C_f(I) := \{g \in C(I) \mid g(x_0) = f(x_0) \land g(x_N) = f(x_N)\}\]

Note that \((C_f(I), d_{\infty})\) is a complete metric space.

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & \Delta := \{x_0, x_1, \ldots, x_N\} \text{ be a partition of } I \\ & u_i : I \to I \text{ be contractive homeomorphisms s.t. } u_i(x_0) = x_{i-1} \land u_i(x_N) = x_i \\ & \alpha \in \R^N \\ & b \in C_f(I) \text{ be a base function} \end{align*}\)

More generally, \(b = L f\), where \(L : C_f(I) \to C_f(I)\) is linear and bounded. Define an RB operator

\[ \begin{align*} T_\Delta^\alpha : C_f(I) & \to C_f(I) \\ g & \mapsto f + \alpha_i \cdot (g - b) \circ u_i^{-1} \end{align*} \]

on each interval \(u_i(I)\). One can show that

  • \(T_\Delta^\alpha g(x_i^-) = T_\Delta^\alpha g(x_i^+)\)
  • \(T_\Delta^\alpha g(x_0) = f(x_0)\)
  • \(T_\Delta^\alpha g(x_N) = f(x_N)\).

If \(\alpha_\infty:=\|\alpha\|_\infty < 1\), then \(T_\Delta^\alpha\) is a contraction on \((C_f(I), d_\infty)\). Thus, there exists a unique fixed point \(f_\Delta^\alpha \in C_f(I)\). The family of fractal functions \(\{f_\Delta^\alpha\}_{\Delta, \alpha}\) are parametrized by \(\Delta\) and \(\alpha\). For \(\alpha=0\), we have \(f_\Delta^0 = f\).

Proposition

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & f \in C_f(I) \\ & f_\Delta^\alpha \text{ be the unique fixed point} \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \|f_\Delta^\alpha - f\|_\infty \leq \frac{\alpha_\infty}{1 - \alpha_\infty} \cdot \|f\|_\infty\]

The fractal operator

\[ \begin{align*} \F_\Delta^\alpha : C_f(I) & \to C_f(I) \\ f & \mapsto f_\Delta^\alpha \end{align*} \]

is linear and bounded.

docs.title: 2.5 Box Dimension of Affine Fractal Functions
docs.text:
For a fractal (interpolation) function \(f\) with non-uniform interpolation points \(\{x_i\}\) and maps \(u_i(x) = a_i x + b_i\), \(a_i \neq a_j\), it can be shown that the box dimension of the graph of \(f\) is given by the unique positive solution \(d\) of

\[ \sum_{i=1}^{N} a_i^{d-1} |L_i| = 1 \]

provided that the interpolation points are not collinear and \(\sum |L_i| > 1\). Otherwise, \(\dim_B\graph f = 1\).

docs.title: 10 Duality
docs.text:
Definition 10.1

Let \((X, \| \cdot \|_X)\) and \((Y, \| \cdot \|_Y)\) be Banach spaces.

IsomorphicIsometric

\(X \cong Y\) if \(\exists\) continuous isomorphism \(T: X \to Y\).

\(X\) and \(Y\) are isomorphic and \(\|x\|_X = \|Tx\|_Y \quad \forall\ x \in X\).

Theorem 10.1 Theorem 10.2 Lemma 10.1 Theorem 10.3 Corollary 10.1 Example 10.1

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X = \ell^\infty \\ & c \subseteq \ell^\infty \text{ be the subspace of convergent sequences} \\ \end{align*}\)

and define

\[ \begin{align*} \ell : c & \to \R \\ (a_n) & \mapsto \lim_{n\to\infty} a_n \end{align*} \] Claim: \(\ell\) is continuous \[|\ell(a)| = \left|\lim_{n\to\infty} a_n\right| \le \sup_j |a_j| = \|a\|_\infty\] \[ \begin{align*} \implieshref{#c101}{C.10.1} & \exists\ L : \ell^\infty \to \R \text{ continuous linear s.t.} \\ & L|_c = \ell \land \|L\| = \|\ell\| \end{align*} \] Corollary 10.2

Let \(X\) be a normed vector space.

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \forall\ x \in & X \setminus \{0\} : \exists\ L \in X^* \text{ s.t. } \\ & \|L\| = 1 \land L(x) = \|x\| \end{align*} \] Proof

Define the functional

\[ \begin{align*} \ell : \Span\{x\} & \to \K \\ \lambda x & \mapsto \lambda \|x\| \end{align*} \]

\(\begin{align*} \implies & \begin{cases} \ell(x) = \|x\| \\ \|\ell\| = 1 \end{cases} \\ \implieshref{#c101}{C.10.1} & \exists\ L : X \to \K \text{ continuous linear extension of } \ell \end{align*}\)

Corollary 10.3 Corollary 10.4 Corollary 10.5

docs.title: Dual space of a Hilbert space
docs.text:
Let \(X\) be a Hilbert space.

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \begin{cases} X \cong X^* \\ \begin{matrix} T: &\!\!\!\!\!\!X \to X^* \\ &y \mapsto \langle \cdot, y \rangle \end{matrix} \text{ is an isometric isomorphism} \end{cases} \end{align*} \] Proof SurjectiveInjectiveContinuousIsometric

Follows from Riesz representation theorem T.9.1.

Assume \(Ty = \inner{\cdot}{y} = 0_{X^*}\).

\[ \begin{align*} \iff& \inner{x}{y} = 0 \quad \forall\ x \in X \\ \implies& \inner{y}{y} = 0 \\ \implies& y = 0 \end{align*} \]

This is clear.

\[ \begin{align*} \|Ty\|_{X^*} &= \sup_{0 \neq x \in X} \frac{|\inner{x}{y}|}{\|x\|_X} \\ &= \sup_{\|x\|_X = 1} |\inner{x}{y}| \\ &= \left|\inner{\frac{\pm y}{\|y\|_X}}{y}\right| \\ &= \|y\|_X \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Duals of \(\ell^p\) and \(L^p\) spaces
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & 1 \le p < \infty \\ & 1 \le q \le \infty \\ & \quad\quad \text{s.t. } \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1 \\ & \Omega \text{ measurable} \end{align*}\)

(a)(b) \[(\ell^p)^* \cong \ell^q\]

The map \(T: \ell^q \to (\ell^p)^*\) defined by

\[(Ty)(x) := \sum_{j=1}^\infty y_j \overline x_j, \quad y \in \ell^q, x \in \ell^p\]

is an isometric isomorphism.

\[(L^p(\Omega))^* \cong L^q(\Omega)\]

The map \(T: L^q(\Omega) \to (L^p(\Omega))^*\) defined by

\[(Tg)(f) := \int_\Omega g(x) \overline{f(x)} dx, \quad g \in L^q(\Omega), f \in L^p(\Omega)\]

is an isometric isomorphism.

Proof (a)(b)

Proof for \(\K = \R\).

Well-definedInjectiveSurjectiveIsometric

We need to show that \(\forall y\in \ell^q : Ty \in (\ell^p)^*\):

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & x \in \ell^p \\ & y \in \ell^q \end{align*}\)

By the Hölder inequality, we have

\[|(Ty)(x)| = \left|\sum_{j=1}^{\infty} x_j y_j \right| \le \|x\|_p \|y\|_q\]

Thus, yielding

\[\|Ty\|_{(\ell^p)^*} \le \|y\|_q < \infty\]

so, \(Ty \in (\ell^p)^*\).

\[ \begin{align*} Ty = 0 & \iff (Ty)(x) = 0 \quad \forall\ x \in \ell^p \\ & \iff \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} x_j y_j = 0 \quad \forall\ x \in \ell^p \\ & \implies \sum_{j=1}^{\infty} \delta_{j,k} y_j = 0 \quad \forall\ k \in \N \\ & \implies y = 0 \end{align*} \]

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & L \in (\ell^p)^* \\ & y_n := L(e_n) \\ & y := (y_1, y_2, \dots) \end{align*}\)

Claim: \(y \in \ell^q\)

\(\text{Let } \tilde y_n := \begin{cases} |y_n|^{1-1} \cdot \sgn(y_n) & y_n \neq 0 \\ 0 & \text{else} \end{cases}\)

Then, we have that

\[ \begin{align*} (1) && \sum_{n=1}^{N} |\tilde y_n|^p &= \sum_{n=1}^{N} |y_n|^{p(q-1)} = \sum_{n=1}^{N} |y_n|^q \\ (2) &&& \sum_{n=1}^{N} |\tilde y_n|^q = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \tilde y_n y_n = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \tilde y_n L(e_n) \\ &&& = L\left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} \tilde y_n e_n\right) = L\Big((\tilde y_1, \dots, \tilde y_N)\Big) \\ &&& \le \|L\|_{(\ell^p)^*} \cdot \|(y_1, \dots, y_N)\|_p \\ &&& \overset{(1)}{=} \|L\|_{(\ell^p)^*} \cdot \left(\sum_{n=1}^{N} |y_n|^q\right)^{1/p} \end{align*} \]

Then we get an upper bound, and let \(N\to\infty\), yielding

\[\|y\|_q = \left(\sum_{n=1}^\infty |y_n|^q\right)^{1-1/p} \le \|L\|_{(\ell^p)^*} < \infty\]

so \(y \in \ell^q\).

Thus, \(L = Ty\) on \(\overline{d}^{\|\cdot\|_p}\).

For \(1 \le p < \infty\), we have that \(d\) is dense in \(\ell^p\). Moreover, \(Ty\) and \(L\) are continuous on \(\ell^p\), so \(L = Ty\) on \(\ell^p\).

Follows from some of the claculations in the proof of well-definedness and surjectivity.

\(\supsetsim\)\(\subsetsim\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & f \in L^p \\ & g \in L^q \end{align*}\)

Define \(L_g: L^p \to \K\) by

\[L_g(f) := \int_\Omega f \overline g\]

Then, by the Hölder inequality, we have

\[\left|\int_\Omega f g \right| \le \|f\|_p \|g\|_q\]

Thus \((L^p)^* \supsetsim L^q\).

Let \(L \in (L^p)^*\) and define

\[ \begin{align*} I : L^p & \to \R \\ f & \mapsto \frac{1}{p} \|f\|_p^p - L(f) \end{align*} \] Claim: \(\exists \text{ minimizer of } I\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & \{f_j\} \subseteq L^p \text{ be a minimizing sequence of } I \\ & d := \lim_{j\to\infty} I(f_j) \\ \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies d & \gets \frac{1}{2} (I(f_j) + I(f_k)) \\ & = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{1}{p} \|f_j\|_p^p + \frac{1}{p} \|f_k\|_p^p\right) - L\left(\frac{f_j + f_k}{2}\right) \\ & \!\oversethref{#l101}{L.10.1}{\le} \frac{1}{p} \left\|\frac{f_j + f_k}{2}\right\|_p^p - L\left(\frac{f_j + f_k}{2}\right) + \frac{1}{p} \left\|\frac{f_j - f_k}{2}\right\|_p^p \\ & \ge d + \frac{1}{p} \left\|\frac{f_j - f_k}{2}\right\|_p^p \end{align*} \]

Thus, \(\{f_j\}\) is a Cauchy sequence in \(L^p\), which is complete, so the limit exists.

Let \(f \in L^p\) be a minimizer of \(I\).

Claim: \(L(\phi) = \int_\Omega \phi |f|^{p-1} \sgn(f)\)

Then, we have that

\[I(f + \varepsilon \phi) \ge I(f) \quad \forall\ \varepsilon \in \R, \phi \in L^p\]

Then, we have that

\[ \begin{align*} 0 = \left.\frac{d}{d\varepsilon} \right|_{\varepsilon=0} I(f + \varepsilon \phi) &= \left.\frac{d}{d\varepsilon} \right|_{\varepsilon=0} \left(\int_\Omega \frac{1}{p} |f + \varepsilon \phi|^p d\mu - L(f + \varepsilon \phi)\right) \\ &= \left.\int_\Omega |f+ \varepsilon\phi|^{p-1} \sgn(f) \phi d\mu - L(\phi)\right|_{\varepsilon=0} \\ &= \int_\Omega |f|^{p-1} \sgn(f) \phi d\mu - L(\phi) \end{align*} \] \[\int_\Omega |g|^q = \int_\Omega |f|^{(p-1)q} = \int_\Omega |f|^{p} < \infty\]

Thus, \(g \in L^q\).

docs.title: Clarkson's inequality
docs.text: \[ \begin{align*}     (a) && 2 \le p < \infty & \implies \|\frac{f+g}{2}\|_p^p + \|\frac{f-g}{2}\|_p^p \le \frac{1}{2}(\|f\|_p^p + \|g\|_p^p) \\     \\     (b) && \begin{rcases}         & 1 < p < 2 \\         & \frac{1}{p} + \frac{1}{q} = 1     \end{rcases}     & \implies \|\frac{f+g}{2}\|_p^q + \|\frac{f-g}{2}\|_p^q \le \left(\frac{\|f\|_p^p + \|g\|_p^p}{2}\right)^{q-1} \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Hahn-Banach theorem
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a real or complex vector space} \\ & U \subseteq X \text{ be a subspace} \\ & p: X \to \R \text{ be a sublinear functional} \\ & \ell : U \to \K \text{ be a linear functional s.t.} \\ & \quad \Re(\ell) < p \text{ on } U \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \exists\ & L : X \to \K \text{ linear s.t.} \\ & L|_U = \ell \land \Re(L) \le p \text{ on } X \end{align*} \] Proof

For \(\K=\R\):

Using induction on \(n:= \codim(U) = \dim(X/U)\):

\(n=1\)\(n>1\)

Let \(x_0 \in X \setminus U\).

\[\implies \forall x \in X : \exists!\ u \in U, \lambda \in \R : x = u + \lambda x_0\]

For any \(r \in \R\), define

\[L(x) := \ell(u) + \lambda r\]

R.T.P: \(\exists\ r \in \R : L \le p \text{ on } X\)

Assume \(L \le p\) on \(X\). That is if and only if

\[ \begin{align*} \ell(u) + \lambda r &\le p(u + \lambda x_0) \quad \forall\ u \in U, \lambda \in \R \\ r \lambda &\le p(u + \lambda x_0) - \ell(u) \\ r &\le p\left(\frac{u}{\lambda} + x_0\right) - \ell\left(\frac{u}{\lambda}\right) \\ r & \le \inf_{v \in U} \left(p(v + x_0) - \ell(v)\right) & (1) \\ r & \le \sup_{w \in U} \left(\ell(w) - p(w + x_0)\right) & (2) \end{align*} \]

So \(r\) exists iff \((1) \le (2)\).

\[\ell(w) - p(w - x_0) \le p(v + x_0) - \ell(v) \iff \ell(w) + \ell(v) \le p(v + x_0) + p(w - x_0)\]

This is the case, as

\[\ell(w) + \ell(v) = \ell(v + w) \le p(v + w) \le p(v + x_0) + p(w - x_0)\]

The set

\[A:= \{(V, L_V) \mid U \subseteq V \subseteq X \text{ subspace}, L_V : V \to \R \text{ a } p \text{-bounded linear extension of } \ell\}\]

is partially ordered given by

\[(V_1, L_{V_1}) \le (V_2, L_{V_2}) \iff V_1 \subseteq V_2 \land L_{V_2}|_{V_1} = L_{V_1}\]

and is non-empty as \((U, \ell) \in A\).

Claim: \(\exists\) an upper bound

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & \{(V_i, L_{V_i})\}_{i \in I} \subseteq A \text{ be a totally ordered chain} \\ & V := \bigcup_{i \in I} V_i \\ & L : V \to \R \text{ be defined by } L(x) := L_{V_i}(x) \text{ for } x \in V_i \end{align*}\)

Then, \((V, L)\) is an upper bound of \(\{(V_i, L_{V_i})\}_{i \in I}\).

So by Zorn's lemma, \(A\) has a maximal element \((X_0, L_{X_0})\).

Suppose \(X_0 \subsetneq X\).

Then, by induction, \(\exists\ (\tilde X_0, L_{\tilde X_0}) > (X_0, L_{X_0})\), which is a contradiction.

docs.title: Hahn-Banach theorem for normed spaces
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a normed space} \\ & U \subseteq X \text{ be a subspace} \\ & \ell : U \to \K \text{ be a continuous linear functional} \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \exists\ & L : X \to \K \text{ continuous linear s.t.} \\ & L|_U = \ell \land \|L\| = \|\ell\| \end{align*} \] Proof

Let \(p(x) := \|\ell\| \|x\|\).

\(\begin{align*} \implies & |\ell(x)| \le \|\ell\| \|x\| = p(x) \quad \forall\ x \in U \\ \implieshref{#t103}{T.10.3} & \exists\ L : X \to \R \text{ linear s.t.} \\ & L(x) \le p(x) \quad \forall\ x \in X \\ \implies & |L(x)| = \max\{L(x), -L(x)\} \le p(x) \quad \forall\ x \in X \\ \implies & \|L\| \le \|\ell\| \\ \implies & L \text{ is continuous} \end{align*}\)

docs.title: Dual characteristic of norm
docs.text:
Let \(X\) be a normed vector space.

\[\implies \|x\| = \sup_{L\in X^*\setminus\{0\}} \frac{|Lx|}{\|L\|}\] Proof \(\ge\)\(\le\) \[\frac{|Lx|}{\|x\|} \le \sup_{y\in X\setminus\{0\}} \frac{|Ly|}{\|y\|} = \|L\| \quad \forall L \in X^*\]

Choose \(L\) from C.10.2.

\[\implies \frac{|Lx|}{\|L\|} = \frac{\|x\|}{1}\]

docs.title: Separate point from closed subspaces
docs.text:
\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X ext{ be a Banach space} \ & U \subseteq X ext{ be a closed subspace} \ & x_0 \in X \setminus U \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} \implies \exists\ &L \in X^* ext{ s.t.} \ & L_U = 0 \land Lx_0 eq 0 \end{align*} \] Proof

Let \(q : X o X/U\) be the quotient map.

\[ \implies egin{cases} q(u) = 0 & orall u \in U \ q(x_0) eq 0 \end{cases} \]

Let \(\ell : \Span\{q(x_0)\} o \K\) be a linear functoinal s.t. \(\ell(q(x_0)) eq 0\).

\[\implieshref{#c101}{C.10.1} \exists\ ilde L : X/U o \K ext{ continuous linear extension of } \ell\]

Finally, define

\[ egin{align*} L : X & o \K \ x & \mapsto ilde L(q(x)) \end{align*} \]

Then, \(L(x_0) eq 0\) and \(L|_U = 0\).

docs.title: Existence of closed complement
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Banach space} \\ & U \subseteq X \text{ be a f.d. subspace} \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \exists\ \text{ closed complement of } U\] Proof

Let \(\{e_i\}_{i = 1}^n\) be a basis of \(U\) and define the maps

\[ \begin{matrix} \begin{aligned} L_i : U & \to \K \\ \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j e_j & \mapsto c_i \\ \end{aligned} &&& \begin{aligned} P : U &\to X \\ x & \mapsto \sum_{j=1}^{n} L_j(x) e_j \end{aligned} \end{matrix} \]

Then, by C.10.1, we have

\[\exists\ \tilde L_i \in X^* : \tilde L_i|_U = L_i \land \|\tilde L_i\| = \|L_i\|\]

Let \(\tilde P : X \to X\) be the resulting extension of \(P\).

Claim: \(\ker \tilde P\) is closed

Let \(\{x_n\} \subseteq \ker \tilde P\) be a Cauchy sequence with limit \(x\in X\). As \(\tilde P\) is continuous, we have

\[0 = \lim_{n \to \infty} \tilde P(x_n) = \tilde P(x)\]

Thus, \(x \in \ker \tilde P\).

Claim: \(\tilde P^2 = \tilde P\) \[ \begin{align*} \tilde P^2(x) &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} \tilde L_j^2(x) e_j \\ &= \sum_{j=1}^{n} \tilde L_j(\x) e_j \\ &= \tilde P(x) \end{align*} \]

Let \(x \in X\). Then, we have

\[x = \tilde Px - (P-I)x\]

where \(\tilde Px \in U\) and \((P-I)x \in \ker \tilde P\), so \(X = U \oplus \ker \tilde P\).

docs.title: 9 Duality in Hilbert spaces
docs.text:
Theorem 9.1 Definition: Adjoint operator Proposition 9.1 Lemma 9.1

Let \(X\) be a Hilbert space.

\[\inner{x}{\eta} = \inner{x}{\eta'} \quad \forall\ x \in X \iff \eta = \eta'\] Proof \[ \begin{align*} &\inner{x}{\eta-\eta'} = 0 \quad \forall\ x \in X \\ &\implies \inner{\eta-\eta'}{\eta-\eta'} = 0 \\ &\implies \eta = \eta' \end{align*} \] Proposition 9.2 Definition 9.2 Proposition 9.3

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hilbert space} \\ & A \in L(X, X) \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} A &\text{ self-adjoint} & \implies &\Spec A \subseteq \R \\ A &\text{ shew-symmetric} & \implies &\Spec A \subseteq i\R \\ A &\text{ unitary} & \implies &\Spec A \subseteq s^1= \{\lambda \in \C : |\lambda| = 1\} \\ \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Riesz Representation Theorem
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hilbert space} \\ & l \in X^* = L(X, \K) \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \exists!\ & \eta \in X \text{ s.t.} \\ & l(x) = \inner{x}{\eta} \quad \forall\ x \in X \end{align*} \]

Remark: \(\text{T.9.1}\implies X \cong X^*\)

docs.title: Adjoint operator
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hilbert space} \\ & A \in L(X, X) \\ & l_y(x):= \inner{Ax}{y} \quad \forall\ y \in X \end{align*}\)

Then, \(l_y \in X^*\) and by T.9.1, \(\exists! \eta =: A^*y \in X\) s.t.

\[\inner{Ax}{y} = \inner{x}{\eta} = \inner{x}{A^*y} \quad \forall\ x \in X\]

\(A^* : X \to X\) is called the adjoint operator of \(A\).

docs.title: The adjoint operator is bounded and linear
docs.text:
Proof LinearityBoundedness

Follows from the linearity of the inner product.

Follows from P.9.2.

docs.title: Properties of the adjoint operator
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hilbert space} \\ & A, B \in L(X, X) \\ & \lambda \in \K \end{align*}\)

Then, the following holds:

  1. \((A^*)^* = A\)
  2. \((A + B)^* = A^* + B^*\)
  3. \((\lambda A)^* = \overline \lambda A^*\)
  4. \((AB)^* = B^*A^*\)
  5. \(\|A^*\| = \|A\|\)

docs.title: 7 Index Theory
docs.text:
Definition: Cokernel

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X, Y \text{ be normed spaces} \\ & T : X \to Y \text{ be a linear map} \\ \end{align*}\)

The cokernel of \(T\) is the quotient space \(Y/\Im(T)\).

Definition: Fredholm Operator

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X, Y \text{ be Banach spaces} \\ & T \in L(X, Y) \end{align*}\)

\(T\) is a Fredholm operator if

  • \(\dim \ker T < \infty\)
  • \(\dim \coker T < \infty\)

\(\overline f(X, Y)\) is the set of Fredholm operators.

Definition: Index

Let \(X, Y\) be Banach spaces.

The index of \(T \in \overline f(X, Y)\) is

\[\ind T := \dim(\ker T) - \dim(\coker T)\] Theorem 7.1 Example \(A_t\)\(S^+\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X = Y = \ell_2 \ \\ & A_t \in \overline f(X, X) \end{align*}\)

sunch that

\[A_t(a_1, a_2, ...) = (ta_1, ta_2, ...)\]

First of all, \(t\neq 0 \iff A_t\) is invertible.

For \(t=0\), we have that \(\ker A_0 = \{\(a, 0, 0, ...\) \in \ell_2 : a \in \C\}\).

\[ \implies \dim \ker A_0 = 1\]

Moreover, \(\coker A_0 = \ell_2 / \Im A_0 \cong \Ker A_0\).

\[ \begin{align*} & \implies \dim(\coker A_0) = 1 \\ & \implies \ind A_0 = 0 \end{align*} \]

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X=Y=\ell_2 \\ & S^+ \in \overline f(X, X) \text{ be the right shift operator} \end{align*}\)

Then, we have \(\dim \ker S^+ = 0\) and \(\coker S^+ \cong \Span\{e_1\}\).

\[ \implies \ind S^+ = -1\] Lemma 7.1 Corollary 7.1

Let \(X=Y=\ell_2\).

Then there exists an open set of non-invertible operators.

In aprticular, the set of invertible operators is not dense.

Remark

Does not hold for finite dimensional spaces, as non-invertible operators lies on the surface \(\det A = 0\).

Lemma 7.2

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X, Y \text{ be Banach} \\ & T \in L(X, Y) \\ \end{align*}\)

\[ \dim(\coker T) < \infty \iff \Im T \text{ is cloesd}\] Lemma 7.3

If \(X\) is Banach and \(U\subseteq X\) is a finite dimensional subspace

\[ \exists\ \text{ closed subspace } V \text{ s.t. } X = U \oplus V\]

docs.title: Index theory
docs.text:
Let \(X, Y\) be Banach spaces.

123

\(\overline f(X, Y)\) is an open subset of \(L(X, Y)\).

\(\ind: \overline f(X, Y) \to \Z\) is locally constant.

The index

\[ \ind: (\overline f(X, X), \oplus) \to (\Z, +)\]

is a semi-group homomorphism.

\[\ind(T \circ S) = \ind T + \ind S\] Proof 1

Idea: Make \(T\) invertible and use that invertibility is preserved under small perturbations.

Notation: \(X = U \oplus V\) if \(U\) is a complement of \(V\) in \(X\).

Step 1: Decompose \(X\).

We know that \(\dim \ker T < \infty\). This implies there exists a closed subspace \(V \subseteq X\) such that

\[ X = \ker T \oplus V\]

Step 2: Decompose \(Y\).

\(\dim(\coker T) < \infty\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & [y_1, ..., y_n] \text{ be a basis of } \coker T \\ & W:= \Span\{y_1, ..., y_n\} \end{align*}\)

Claim: \(Y=W + \Im T\) ExistenceUniqueness

Let \(y\in Y\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies &[y] \in \coker T \\ \implies & [y] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i [y_i] \\ \implies &y = (y - \sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i y_i) + \sum_{i} \alpha_i y_i \\ \end{align*} \]

where \(y-\sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i y_i \in \Im T\) and \(\sum_{i=1}^n \alpha_i y_i \in W\).

Easy? Do it at home.

Step 4: manufacture an invertible operator from \(T\)

Define the operator

\[ \begin{align*} \tilde T : V \oplus W & \to Y \\ (v, w) &\mapsto Tv + w \end{align*} \]

\(\tilde T\) is bijective, as we have factored out \(\Ker T\).

Let \(S \in L(X, Y)\) and \(\tilde S : V \times W \to Y\) defined in the same way as \(\tilde T\).

Since surjectivity is preserved under small perturbations T.6.1, Since surjectivity is preserved under small perturbations T.6.1, we have that \(\tilde S\) is bijective if \(\|\tilde S - \tilde T\|\) is small.

\[ \begin{align*} (\tilde S - \tilde T)(v, w) & = (Sv + w) - (Tv + w) \\ & = (S-T)v \\ \|\tilde S - \tilde T\| &= \|S-T\| \end{align*} \]

Thus, \(\tilde S\) is invertible if \(\|S-T\|\) is small.

Step 5: Check that S fredholm and \(\ind S = \ind T\)

Claim: \(\ker S \subset \Ker T\)

We have that \(S|_V = \tilde S|_{V\times \{0\}}\) is injective.

\[ \begin{align*} \implies &\ker S \cap V &= \{0\} \\ \implies &\ker S \subseteq \ker T \end{align*} \]

Choose a compliment \(Z\) of \(\ker S\) in \(\Ker T\). This implies that

\[ \begin{align*} X = \ker T \oplus V \\ \Ker T = \Ker S \oplus Z \end{align*} \]

impies \(X = V \oplus \Ker S \oplus Z\).

Claim: \(Y=W\oplus \Im S|_V\)

Existance:

As \(\tilde S\) is onto, we have that

\[\forall y\in Y, \exists\ (v, w) \in V \oplus W \text{ s.t. } \tilde S(v, w) = y\]

Skips uniqueness

Since \(S\) is fredholm, we have that \(Y= W' \oplus \Im S\) with \(\dim W' = \dim(\coker S)\).

\[ \begin{align*} Y &= W' \oplus \Im S \\ &= W' \oplus \Im S|_{Z \oplus V} \\ &= W' \oplus \Im S|_Z \oplus \Im S|_V \\ \implies \dim (\ker T) &= \dim(\ker S) + \dim Z \\ \implies \dim(\coker T) &= \dim W'\\ &= \dim W' + \dim(\Im S|_Z) \\ &= \dim(\coker S) + \dim Z \\ \dim(\Ker T) - \dim(\coker T) &= \dim(\ker S) + \dim Z - \dim(\coker S) - \dim Z \\ &= \dim(\ker S) - \dim(\coker S) \\ \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Completeness of quotient spaces
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be Banach} \\ & U \subseteq X \text{ be closed} \end{align*}\)

Then, we have that

  1. \(\|[x]\| = \inf_{u \in U} \|x-u\|\) is a norm on \(X/U\).
  2. \(X/U\) is Banach.
Proof 12 HomogeneityTriangle inequalityPositive definiteness

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & 0 \neq \lambda \in \K \\ & x \in X \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \|\lambda[x]\| &= \inf_{u \in U} \|\lambda x - u\| \\ &= \inf_{u \in U} |\lambda| \|x - \frac{1}{\lambda} u\| \\ &= |\lambda| \inf_{u \in U} \|x - \frac{1}{\lambda} u\| \\ &= |\lambda| \|[x]\| \end{align*} \]

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & x, y \in X \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \|[x+y]\| &= \inf_{u \in U} \|x+y+u\| \\ &= \inf_{u \in U} \|x+u + y+u\| \\ &\le \inf_{u \in U} \|x+u\| + \|y+u\| \\ &\le \inf_{u \in U} \|x+u\| + \inf_{u \in U} \|y+u\| \\ &= \|[x]\| + \|[y]\| \end{align*} \] \[ \begin{align*} 0 = \|[x]\| &= \inf_{u \in U} \|x+u\| \\ &= \inf_{u \in U} \|x-u\| \\ \end{align*} \] \[ \begin{align*} \implies &\exists\ \{U_k\} \subseteq U \text{ s.t. } u_k\to x\in \overline U=U \\ \implies &[x] = [0] \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Compact perturbations
docs.text:
Definition 7.1 Definition 7.2

Let \(X, Y\) be Banach spaces.

\(T \in L(X, Y)\) is called compact if

  • for every bounded sequence \(\{x_n\} \subseteq X\), \(\{Tx_n\}\) has a convergent subsequence.in \(Y\)
  • Alternatively, \(T\) maps bounded sets to relatively compact sets.
Theorem 7.2

Let \(X, Y\) be Banach spaces.

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & T \in \f(X, Y) \\ & K \in L(X, Y) \text{ be compact} \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies T-K \in \f(X, X) \land \ind(T-K) = \ind T\]

In particular, if \(T\in L(X, Y)\) is invertible and \(K\in L(X, Y)\) is compact, then \(T+K\) is fredholm and \(\ind(T-K) = 0\).

Remark

As opposed to T.7.1, this is a global result, so perturbations can be large.

docs.title: Compact subsets
docs.text:
Let \(X\) be a Banach space.

\(A \subseteq X\) is called compact if

  • Every sequence in \(A\) has an accumulation point in \(X\).
  • Alternatively every open cover of \(A\) has a finite subcover.

\(A\) is called relatively compact if its closure \(\overline A\) is compact.

docs.title: 6 Invertibility; notion of spectrum
docs.text:
Lemma 6.1

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & (X, \|\cdot\|_X) \text{ be a normed space} \\ & (Y, \|\cdot\|_Y) \text{ be a Banach space} \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies B(X, Y) \text{ is a Banach space}\] Proof

djks

Lemma 6.2 Lemma 6.3 Theorem 6.1 Definition 6.1

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be Banach} \\ & A \in L(X, X) \\ \end{align*}\)

The spectrum of \(A\) is defined as

\[\Spec A := \{\lambda \in \C : A-\lambda \I \text{ is not invertible}\}\]

The eigenvalues of \(A\) are defined as

\[\rho(A) := \C \setminus \Spec A\]

For \(\lambda \in \rho(A)\), we say that \((A-\lambda \I)^{-1}\) is the resolvent of \(A\) at \(\lambda\).

The point spectrum is defined as

\[\ptspec A := \{\lambda \in \C : \ker(A-\lambda \I) \neq \{0\}\}\]

The eigenvectors are the points \(x\in X\setminus\{0\}\) s.t. \(Ax = \lambda x\) for some \(\lambda \in \rho(A)\).

Example 6.1 123

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X = \ell^2 \\ & A = S_- \in L(X, X) \end{align*}\)

Then, \(\lambda = \frac{1}{2} \in \ptspec A\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\) with eigenvector \(x = (1, \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2^2}, \dots)\).

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X = \ell^2 \\ & A = S_- \in L(X, X) \end{align*}\)

Then \(\lambda = 2\) is not an eigenvalue of \(A\), as

\[2 \I - S_- = 2 \left(I - \frac{1}{2} S_-\right)\]

is invertible by L6.3.

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X = \ell^2 \\ & A = S^+ \in L(X, X) \end{align*}\)

Then \(\lambda = 0\in \Spec{S^+}\), but is not an eigenvalue of \(A\).

docs.title: Submultiplicativity of the operator norm
docs.text:
\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & A \in L(Y, Z) \ & B \in L(X, Y) \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies \|A B\| \le \|A\| \|B\|\] Proof \[ egin{align*} \|AB\| &= \sup_{x eq 0} rac{\|ABx\|}{\|x\|} \ &= \sup_{x, Bx eq 0} rac{\|AB x\|}{\|Bx\|} rac{\|Bx\|}{\|x\|} \ &\le \sup_{y eq 0} rac{\|A y\|}{y} \sup_{x eq 0} rac{\|B x\|}{\|x\|} \ &= \|A\| \|B\| \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Invertibility of operators
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be Banach} \\ & A \in L(X, X) \text{ s.t. } \|A-\I\| < 1 \end{align*}\)

\[ \implies A \text{ is invertible}\]

Moreover,

\[A^{-1} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \sum_{k=0}^{n} \tilde A^k, \tab \tilde A = \I-A\] Proof

Let \(\begin{align*}S_n:=\sum_{k=1}^{n} \tilde A^k\end{align*}\)

Claim: \(\{S_n\}\) is cauchy in \(L(X, Y)\) \[ \begin{align*} \|S_m - S_n\| &\le \sum_{k=n+1}^{m} \|\tilde A\|^k \\ &= \|\tilde A\|^n \sum_{k=0}^{m-n} \|\tilde A\|^k \\ &\le \|\tilde A\|^n \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \|\tilde A\|^k \\ &= \|\tilde A\|^n \frac{1}{1-\|\tilde A\|} \to 0 \end{align*} \]

As \(L(X, Y)\) is Banach, \(S_n\) converges to some \(S\in L(X, Y)\).

Claim: \(S(\I-\tilde A) = \I\) \[ \begin{align*} S_n(\I-\tilde A) &= \sum_{k=1}^{n} \tilde A^k - \sum_{k=1}^{n} \tilde A^{k+1} \\ &= \tilde A - \tilde A^{n+1} \\ &= \I - \tilde A^{n+1}\\ S(\I-\tilde A) &= \I \\ \end{align*} \] Claim: \((\I-\tilde A)A = \I\)

Follows from the same steps as in the above claim.

Thus, we get that \(A^{-1} = S\)

docs.title: Perturbation theory
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X, Y \text{ be Banach} \\ & T, S \in B(X, Y) \text{ with } T \text{ invertible} \\ \end{align*}\)

\[ \|T-S\| \le \frac{1}{\|T^{-1}\|} \implies S \text{ is invertible}\] Proof

We have that

\[S = T-(T-S) = T(\I - T^{-1}(T-S)\]

We have that \(S\) is invertible if \(\I - T^{-1}(T-S\) is invertible.

\[ \begin{align*} \|T^{-1} (T-S)\| &\le \|T^{-1}\| \|T-S\| \\ & \overset{\text{Hyp}}{\le} \|T^{-1}\| \frac{1}{\|T^{-1}\|} \\ &= 1 \end{align*} \]

Thus, \(\I - T^{-1}(T-S)\) is invertible.

Remark: Explicit inverse \[ \begin{align*} S^{-1} &= \left(T(I-T^{-1}(T-S))\right)^{-1} \\ &= \left(\I - T^{-1}(T-S)\right)^{-1} T^{-1} \\ &= (\I + T^{-1}(T-S) + T^{-1}(T-S)^2 + \dots) T^{-1} \\ &= T^{-1} + T^{-1}(T-S)T^{-1} + T^{-1}(T-S)^2T^{-1} + \dots \\ \end{align*} \]

docs.text:
Theorem 5.1

Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be banach spaces, and \(T\) be a linear, continuous and bijective.

\[ \implies T^{-1} \text{ is continuous}\] Proof

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X, Y \text{ be normed spaces} \\ & I \text{ be an index set} \\ & \{T_i\}_{i\in I} \text{ be a family of operators in } L(X, Y) \end{align*}\)

Point wise boundedUniformly bounded \[\sup_{i\in I} \|T_i(x)\|_Y < \infty\] \[\sup_{i\in I} \|T_i\|_{L(X,Y)} < \infty\] Theorem 5.3 Definition 5.3 Theorem 5.4

\[A \text{ is closed } \iff A \text{ is continuous}\] Proof

Using OMT.

Example 5.1

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X=C^\infty[0, 1] \\ & A=\frac{\mathrm d}{\mathrm d x} \end{align*}\)

  • \(A\) is not continuous for any norm

docs.title: Nowhere dense
docs.text:
The set \(A\) is called nowhere dense if \(\overline A\) does not contain an open set.

Terminology: A metric space \(X\) is called

  • of first category if it is a countable union of nowhere dense sets
  • of second category otherwise.

docs.title: Bair Category Theorem (BCT)
docs.text:
A complete metric space is of second category.

Proof

Assume the complete metric space \(X\) is of first category:

\[ X = \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n, \tab A_n \text{ is nowhere dense}\]

The idea is to construct an element \(x\) which is not in this union:

Let \(M_n:=\overline A_n\) be closed, nowhere dense. Then, \(\begin{align*}X = \bigcup_{n=1}^{\infty} M_n\end{align*}\)

Claim: \(X\backslash M_1\) is open and dense

Assume it is not dense. Then, there exists \(x \in X\) s.t.

\[ \operatorname{dist}\big(x, X\backslash M_1\big) > 0\]

Them, we get

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \exists\ \varepsilon > 0 : & B_\varepsilon(x) \cap X \backslash M_1 = \emptyset \\ \implies & B_\varepsilon(x) \subseteq M_1 \contradiction \end{align*} \]

which is a contradiction, as \(M_1\) is nowhere dense.

Step 1Step 2Step n

Since \(X\backslash M_1\) is nonempty and open, there exists a closed ball \(S_1 \subseteq X \backslash M_1\)

\[S_1:=\overline{B_{r_1}(x_1)}, \tab r_1 \le \frac{1}{2}\]

Since \(X\backslash (M_1 \cup M_2)\) is nonempty and open, there exists a closed ball \(S_2 \subseteq X \backslash (M_1 \cup M_2)\)

\[S_1:=B_{r_2}(x_2), \tab r_2 \le \frac{1}{2^2}\]

Since \(\begin{align*}X\backslash \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} M_i \end{align*}\) is nonempty and open, there exists a closed ball \(\begin{align*}S_n \subseteq X\backslash \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} M_i \end{align*}\)

\[S_n:=B_{r_n}(x_n), \tab r_n \le \frac{1}{2^n}\]

This creates a cauchy sequence \(\{x_n\}\) which must converge to an element \(x \in X\).

Claim: \(x \in S_n \tab \forall\ n\).

It is certainly true that

\[x_m \in S_n \tab \forall\ m \ge n\]

As \(S_n\) is closed, it must hold for \(x\) as well.

Hence, \(x \not\in M_n \tab\forall n \contradiction\)

Thus, \(X\) must be of second category.

docs.title: Open mapping theorem
docs.text:
Let \(T : X \to Y\) be a linear, continuous, surjective mapping between banach spaces.

\[ \implies T \text{ is open.}\] Proof

Fix any \(\varepsilon > 0\).

Claim: \(\overline{T(B_\varepsilon(0))}\) contains an open subset.

We have that

\[X = \bigcup_{n = 1}^{\infty} B_{n \varepsilon}(0)\]

As \(T\) is surjective, we get

\[ \begin{align*} \implies & Y = T(X) = \bigcup_{n\in\N} T(B_{n \varepsilon}(0)) \\ \implieshref{#BCT}{BCT} & \text{ not all } T(B_{n \varepsilon}(0)) \text{ are nowhere dense} \\ \iff &\exists\ \tilde V_0 \subseteq \overline{T(B_{n \varepsilon}(0))} : V_0 \text{ is open} \\ \implies & \overline{T(B_{\varepsilon}(0))} = \frac{1}{n} \overline{T(B_{n \varepsilon}(0))} \supseteq \frac{1}{n} \tilde V_0 =: V_0 \end{align*} \]

\(V_0\) is open.

Claim: \(\overline{T(B_\varepsilon(0))}\) contains a ball around \(0\).

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & y_o \in V_0 \cap T(B_{\varepsilon}(0)) \\ & x_0 \in B_{ \varepsilon}(0) \text{ s.t. } T(x_0) = y_0 \end{align*}\)

As \(B_{2 \varepsilon}(0) \supseteq B_\varepsilon(0) -x_0 \ (*)\) and \(V_0\) is open \((**)\), we get

\[ \begin{align*} \overline{T(B_{2 \varepsilon}(0))} & \overset{(*)}{\supseteq} \overline{T(B_\varepsilon(0)-x_0)} \\ &= \overline{T(B_\varepsilon(0))} - T(x_o) \\ & \supseteq V_0-y_0 \\ & \overset{(**)}{\supseteq} B_\delta(y_0) - y_0 \\ &= B_\delta(0) \end{align*} \]

for some \(\delta > 0\).

Claim: \(T(B_{2\varepsilon}(0))\) contains a ball around \(0\).

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & B_{2 \varepsilon} \text{ be a ball around } 0 \\ & \varepsilon_i = 2^{-i} \varepsilon_0 \end{align*}\)

Then, \(\forall \varepsilon_i \exists\ \delta_i > 0\) such that

\[\overline{T(B_{\varepsilon_i)}} \supseteq B_{\delta_i}'\]

Let \(y \in B_{\delta_0}\)

Claim: \(\exists\ x \in B_{2 \varepsilon} : Tx = y\). \[ \begin{align*} &y \in \overline{T(B_{ \varepsilon_0})} \\ & \implies \exists\ x_0\in B_{\varepsilon_0} : \|y-Tx_0\| < \delta_1 \\ 6 \implies y-Tx_0\in B_{\delta_1}' \end{align*} \]

By the same argument,

\[\exists\ x_1\in B_{\varepsilon_1} : \|y-T(x_0-x_1)\| < \delta_2\]

Moreover, we get

\[\exists\ x_n\in B_{\varepsilon_n} : \|y-T\tilde x_n\| < \delta_{n+1} \to 0\]

with \(\begin{align*}\tilde x_n = \sum_{i=0}^{n} x_i\end{align*}\)$

This forms a cauchy sequence as

\[\|\tilde x_n - \tilde x_m\| = \left\|\sum_{i=n+1}^{m} x_i\right\| < \varepsilon_0 2^{-m} \to 0\]

This sequence has a limit \(x\in X\), as \(X\) is banach. As \(T\) is continuous, we have that

\[T \tilde x_n \to Tx=y\]

This yields

\[ \begin{align*} \|x\| &= \left\|\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} x_i\right\| \\ &\le \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \|x_i\| \\ &< \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} 2^{-1} \varepsilon_o \\ &=2 \varepsilon_0 \\ \implies & x \in B_{2 \varepsilon_0} \end{align*} \]

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & \Omega \subseteq X \text{ be an open set} \\ & x \in \omega \\ & y = T(X) \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies T(\Omega) &\supseteq T(B_\varepsilon(x)) \\ &= T(B_\varepsilon(0) + x) \\ &= T(B_\varepsilon(0)) + Tx \\ & \supseteq B_\delta'(0) + Tx \\ & \supseteq B_\delta'(Tx) \\ & \supseteq B_\delta'(y) \\ \end{align*} \]

So for all \(y\in\Omega\) there exists an open ball \(B_\delta'/y) \subseteq T(\Omega)\). Thus, \(T(\Omega)\) is open.

docs.title: Principle of uniform boundedness
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a banach space} \\ & Y \text{ be a normed space} \\ & I \text{ be an index set} \\ & \{T_i\}_{i\in I} \subseteq L(X, Y) \end{align*}\)

\[T_i \text{ is pointwise bounded } \iff T_i \text{ is uniformly bounded}\] Proof

Using BCT, the proof is similar to that of OMP.

docs.title: Closed Operator
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X, Y \text{ be normed spaces} \\ & A \in L(X, Y) \end{align*}\)

\(A\) is called closed if the graph of \(A\)

\[\graph A := \{(x, y) \in X\times Y : y=Ax\}\]

is closed. Alternativesly if

\[ \begin{rcases} x_i &\to x \\ A x_i &\to Ax \end{rcases} \implies Ax = y \]

docs.title: Spectrum of a compact operator
docs.text:
Theorem 8.1 Example: Eigenvalue problem for ODE \[u' = \lambda u ab 2\pi ext{-periodic}\]

Which function space?

\[ egin{align*} C_{2\pi,0}^1 &:= \left\{u \in C^1[0, 2\pi] : egin{aligned} u(0) &= u(2\pi) \ u'(0) &= u'(2\pi) \ \int_0^{2\pi} u(x) dx &= 0 \end{aligned} ight\} \ C_{2\pi, 0}&:= \left\{u \in C[0, 2\pi] : egin{aligned} u(0) &= u(2\pi) \ \int_0^{2\pi} u(x) dx &= 0 \end{aligned} ight\} \ \|u\|_{C_{2\pi,0}^1} &:= \|u\|_\infty + \|u'\|_\infty \end{align*} \]

Which operator?

\[ egin{align*} L : C_{2\pi,0}^1 & o C_{2\pi,0} \ u \mapsto u' \end{align*} \]

Note that

\[u \in C_{2\pi,0}^1 \implies \int_0^{2\pi} Lu dx = u(2\pi) - u(0) = 0\]

Thus yields two problems:

  • Domain space \( eq\) target space
  • \(L\) is not compact

Solution: consider the inverse operation.

Inverse is integral operator

\[ egin{align*} Lu = f &\iff u' = f \ &\iff u ext{ is the antiderivative with zero mean} \ &\iff \left\{ egin{aligned} u(x) &= \int_0^x f(s) ds - rac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi} \int_0^x f(s) ds dx \ &=\int_0^{2\pi} \left[\mathbb{1}_{[0, x]}(s) - rac{2\pi-s}{2\pi} ight]f(s) ds \ &= \int_0^{2\pi} k(x, s) f(s) ds \end{aligned} ight. \end{align*} \]

Now, set

\[(Kf)(x) := \int_0^{2\pi} k(x, s) f(s) ds\]

By construction \(L^{-1} = K\). We can consider \(K : C_{2\pi,0} oself\).

Compactness of \(K\)

\(A:=\{u\in C^1[a, b] : \|u\|_{C^1} \leq R\}\) is relatively compact in \(C[a, b]\).

  • Special case of Arzela-Ascoli theorem
  • Proof: see here

...

Spectrum of \(K\)

T.8.1 \(\implies\) \(0 \in \Spec K\)

\[ egin{align*} \lambda \in \Spec K \setminus \{0\} \iff & \lambda ext{ is an eigenvalue of } K \ \iff & \exists\ f eq 0 : Kf = \lambda f \ \iff & f = \lambda Lf \ \iff & rac{1}{\lambda} f = Lf \ \lambda ext{ is an eigenvalue of } K \iff & rac{1}{\lambda} ext{ is an eigenvalue of } L \ L f = \mu f \iff & f' = \mu f \iff f(x) = A e^{\mu x} \ f \in C_{2\pi,0} \implies& f(0) = f(2\pi) = 0 \ \overset{A eq 0}{\implies}& 1 = e^{\mu 2\pi} \ \implies& \mu = ik, k \in \Z \ \int_0^{2\pi} f(x) dx = 0 \implies& k eq 0 \ \implies& \Spec K = \left\{ rac{1}{ik} : k \in \Z \setminus \{0\} ight\} \cup \{0\} \end{align*} \]

Eigenfunctions

\(\lambda = rac{1}{ik}\)

\[\implies \ker (K- \lambda \I) = \Span\{e^{ikx}\}\]

\(\dots\)

Lemma 8.2 Lemma 8.3

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X ext{ be a Banach space} \ & M, L \subseteq X ext{ be closed subspaces} \ & M \subsetneq L \ & K \in L(X, X) \ & T = \I - K ext{ s.t.}\ &T(L) \subseteq M \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} \implies \exists\ & a \in L ext{ with } \|a\| = 1 \ &\|Ka - Kx\| \ge rac{1}{2} & orall\ x \in M \end{align*} \] Proof

By T.2.1 (Almost orthogonal elements)

\[ egin{align*} \exists\ & a \in L\setminus M ext{ with } \|a\| = 1 \ & \dist(a, M) \ge rac{1}{2} \ \end{align*} \]

Let \( ilde x := Ta = a - Ka \in M\).

\[ egin{align*} \implies& Ka = a - ilde x \ \implies& K a - K x = a - ilde x - K x \ \implies& \|K a - K x\| \ge \dist(a, M) \ge rac{1}{2} \end{align*} \] Proposition 8.1 Lemma 8.4

docs.title: Spectrum of a compact operator
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Banach space} \\ & T \in K(X, X) \\ & \K = \C \end{align*}\)

a)b)c)

\(\Spec T\) is nonempty and excatly one of the following holds:

  • \(\Spec T\) is finite
  • \(\Spec T\) is countable and \(\Spec T = \{\lambda_n\}_{n=1}^\infty \cup \{0\}\) with \(\lambda_n \to 0\).
\[\dim X = \infty \implies 0 \in \Spec T\] \[\lambda \in \Spec T \setminus \{0\} \implies \lambda \text{ is an eigenvalue of } T\] Proof a)b)c)

By L.8.4, we have that

\[\Spec T \subseteq \overline{B_{\|T\|}(0)}\]

R.T.P: \(|\Spec T| = \infty \implies \Spec T\) is countable.

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & A_1:=\{\lambda \in \C : \|T\| \ge |\lambda| \ge 1\} \\ & A_i := \left\{\lambda \in \C : \frac{1}{i-1} > |\lambda| \ge \frac{1}{i}\right\} \tab i \in \N_{>1} \\ \end{align*}\)

Claim: \(A_i \cap \Spec K\) is finite

If not, it would by B.W. contain a nonzero accumulation point. This is a contradiction to P.8.1.

So the spectrum

\[\implies \Spec T\setminus \{0\} = \bigcup_{i=1}^\infty (A_i \cap \Spec T)\]

is a countable union of finite sets, hence countable. From b) we know that \(0 \in \Spec T\).

Assume \(0 \notin \Spec T\).

\[ \begin{align*} \iff & T \text{ is invertible} \\ \iff & \overline{B_1(0)} = T^{-1}T(B_1(0)) \\ \end{align*} \]

So \(\overline{B_1(0)}\) is an image of a continuous map, hence compact. Then, by Riesz's lemma, \(\dim X < \infty\).

This follows from index theory.

Assume \(0 \neq \lambda \in \Spec T\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies& \lambda \I - T \text{ is not invertible} \\ \implieshref{../index_theory/#t72}{T.7.2}& \ind(\lambda \I - T) = 0 \\ \end{align*} \] \[\lambda \text{ is an eigenvalue } \iff \lambda \I - T \text{ is not invertible } \iff \lambda \I - T \text{ is not surjective} \iff \lambda \I - T \text{ is not injective}\]

docs.title: Linear independence of eigenvectors
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a vector space} \\ & T : X \to X \text{ be linear} \\ & \{v_i\}_{i=1}^r \subset X \text{ be eigenvectors} \\ & \{\lambda_i\}_{i=1}^r \subset \K \text{ be the corresponding eigenvalues s.t. } \lambda_i \neq \lambda_j \iff i \neq j \\ \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \{v_i\}_{i=1}^r \text{ is linearly independent}\] Proof

This is trivial for \(r=1\).

Let \(r > 1\) and assume \(\{v_i\}_{i=1}^r\) is linearly dependent.

\[ \begin{align*} \implies& \exists\ a_i \text{ not all of which are } 0 : \sum_{i=1}^r a_i v_i = 0 \\ \implies& \sum_{i=1}^{r} \lambda_i a_i v_i = 0 \\ \implies& \sum_{i=1}^{r-1} (\lambda_i - \lambda_r) a_i v_i = 0 \\ \overset{\text{ind}}{\implies}& (\lambda_i - \lambda_r) a_i = 0 & \forall\ i \in \{1, \dots, r-1\} \\ \implies& a_i = 0 & \forall\ i \in \{1, \dots, r-1\} \\ \implies& a_r = 0 \tab\tab \contradiction\\ \implies \{v_i\}_{i=1}^r \text{ is linearly independent} \end{align*} \]

docs.title: No nonzero accumulation points
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Banach space} \\ & K \in L(X, X) \text{ be compact} \\ \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \Spec K \text{ has no accumulation point } x \neq 0\] Proof

Assume the converse, and let \(0\neq \lambda^* \in \Spec K\) be an accumulation point.

Choose \(\{\lambda_j\}\) distinct s.t. \(\lambda_j \to \lambda^*\).

\[\implies \left\|\frac{1}{\lambda_k} - \frac{1}{\lambda^*}\right\| < \varepsilon\]

By T.8.2, \(\{v_j\}\) is linearly independent.

Let \(U_n := \Span\{v_1, \dots, v_n\}\).

\[\overset{\text{lin. indep.}}{\implies} U_1 \subsetneq U_2 \subsetneq \dots\]

By T.8.3, \(\exists\ x_n \in U_n\setminus U_{n-1}\) with \(\|x_n\| = 1\) s.t.

\[\left\|\frac{1}{\lambda_n} K x_n - \frac{1}{\lambda_n} K x\right\| \ge \frac{1}{2} \tab \forall\ x \in U_{n-1}\]

\(\begin{align*} \implies \frac{1}{2} &\le \left\|\frac{1}{\lambda_n} K x_n - \frac{1}{\lambda_n} K x_m\right\| \\ &\le \left\|\frac{1}{\lambda^*} K x_n - \frac{1}{\lambda^*} K x_m\right\| + \left\|\left(\frac{1}{\lambda^*} - \frac{1}{\lambda_m}\right) K x_m\right\| + \left\|\left(\frac{1}{\lambda^*} - \frac{1}{\lambda_m}\right) K x_m\right\| \\ &\le \frac{1}{\lambda^*} \left\|K x_n - K x_m\right\| + \varepsilon \|K\| + \varepsilon \|K\| \end{align*}\)

\(\begin{align*} &\implies \frac{1}{2} - 2 \varepsilon \|K\| \le \frac{1}{\lambda^*} \left\|K x_n - K x_m\right\| \\ &\implies \{K x_n\} \text{ has no convergent subsequence} \\ &\implies K \text{ is not compact} \tab \contradiction \end{align*}\)

docs.title: Eigenvalues are bounded by the operator norm
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be Banach} \\ & T \in L(X, X) \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \Spec T \subseteq \{\lambda \in \C : |\lambda| \le \|T\|\}\] Proof

Suppose \(\lambda > \|T\|\).

\[ \begin{align*} \|\lambda \I - (T - \lambda \I) \| =& \|T\| \\ <& \lambda \\ =& \frac{1}{\|(\lambda \I)^{-1}\|} \\ \implieshref{../invertibility/#t61}{T.6.1}& T - \lambda \I \text{ is invertible} \\ \implies& \lambda \notin \Spec T \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Special case for compact self-adjoint operators
docs.text:
Theorem 8.2 Proposition 8.2

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hilbert space} \\ & T \in L(X, X) \text{ be self-adjoint} \end{align*}\)

(a) \ptspec T \subseteq \R

(b) The eigenvektors are oerthogonal

Proof (a)(b)

Let \(x, \lambda\) be an eigenvalue-eigenvektor pair s.t. \(\|x\| = 1\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \lambda &= \inner{x}{x} \\ &= \inner{\lambda x}{x} \\ &= \inner{Tx}{x} \\ &= \inner{x}{Tx} \\ &= \inner{x}{\lambda x} \\ &= \overline{\lambda} \inner{x}{x} \\ &= \overline{\lambda} \end{align*} \]

Let \((x, \lambda)\) and \((y, \mu)\) be eigenvalue-eigenvektor pairs s.t. \(\lambda \neq \mu\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implies& \lambda, \mu \in \R \\ \implies& (\lambda - \mu) \inner{x}{y} = \inner{T x}{y} - \inner{x}{T y} = 0 \\ \implies& \inner{x}{y} = 0 \end{align*} \] Lemma 8.5

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hilbert space} \\ & A \in L(X, X) \text{ be self-adjoint} \\ & U \subseteq X \text{ be an invariant subspace of } A \end{align*}\)

\[\implies U^\perp \text{ is an invariant subspace of } A\] Proof

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & x \in U \\ & y \in U^\perp \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \inner{Ay}{x} &= \inner{y}{Ax} = 0 \\ \implies& Ay \in U^\perp \end{align*} \] Theorem 8.3 Lemma 8.6 Lemma 8.7

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hilbert space} \\ & B \in L(X, X) \text{ be self-adjoint s.t. } \inner{Bx}{x} \ge 0 \tab \forall\ x \in X \\ \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \|Bx\|^2 \le \|B\| \inner{Bx}{x} \tab \forall\ x \in X\]

docs.title: Special case for compact self-adjoint operators
docs.text:
\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X ext{ be a Hilbert space} \ & T \in L(X, X) ext{ be compact and self-adjoint} \end{align*}\)

(a)(b)

There exists at most countably many eigenvalues \(\lambda_j \in \K\setminus\{0\}\) s.t,

\[|\lambda_j| \ge |\lambda_{j+1}| > 0\]

and corresponding eigenvectors \(e_j \in X\) s.t.

(i) \(X = \ker T \oplus \overline{\Span}\{e_j\}_{j=1}^\infty\)

(ii) \(Tx = \sum_{j \ge 1} \lambda_j \langle x, e_j angle e_j ab orall\ x \in X\)

\(X\) is separable \(\implies \exists\) an orthonormal basis of \(X\) consisting of the eigenvectors of \(T\).

Proof (a)

Assume without loss of generality that \(T eq 0\).

We know that

\[X = \ker T \oplus (\ker T)^\perp\]

\( egin{align*} \href{#l85}{ ext{L.8.5}} \implies & (\ker T)^\perp ext{ is invariant under } T \ \href{#t81}{ ext{T.8.1}} \implies & \Spec T ext{ is at most countable} \ \href{#l86}{ ext{L.8.6}} \implies & \|T\| = \sup_{\|x\| = 1} |\inner{Tx}{x}| \ \implieshref{#t83}{T.8.3} & \exists\ 0 eq \lambda ext{ eigenvalue} \end{align*}\)

Let \(E_\lambda := \{x \in X : Tx = \lambda x\}\).

Claim: \(E_\lambda\) is finite-dimensional}

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & \{e_1, e_2, \dots\} ext{ be the union of the orthonormal basis of all the } E_\lambda \ & V:= \overline{\Span}\{e_1, e_2, \dots\} \ & W:= V^\perp \cap (\ker T)^\perp ext{ be the orthogonal complement of } V ext{ in } (\ker T)^\perp \end{align*}\)

Assume \(W eq \{0\}\).

\[ egin{align*} \implies& T |_W eq 0 \ \implieshref{#t83}{T.8.3}& \exists\ 0 eq \mu ext{ eigenvalue of } T |_W \ \implies & W ot\perp V ab \contradiction \ \implies & W = \{0\} \ \implies & V = (\ker T)^\perp \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Rayleigh-Ritz variational principle
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hiblert space} \\ & A \in L(X, X) \text{ be self-adjoint and compact} \end{align*}\)

Then, either \(\pm \|A\|\) is an eigenvalue of \(A\).

Proof

Let \(\lambda:=\sup_{\|x\| = 1} \inner{Ax}{x}\).

\[\implies B:=\lambda\I - A \ge 0\]

Let \(B:=\lambda\I - A\).

\[ \begin{align*} \implieshref{#l86}{L.8.6}& \|Bx\|^2 \le \|B\| \inner{Bx}{x} & (*)\\ \implies& \exists\ \|x_n\|=1 : \inner{Ax_n}{x_n} \to \lambda \\ \implies& B x_n \to 0 \\ \implies& Ax_n \to y \in X, \tab \text{ as } A \text{ is compact} \\ \end{align*} \] Claim: \(y\neq 0\)

Assume \(y = 0\).

\[|\lambda| \gets |\inner{Ax_n}{x_n}| \le \|Ax_n\| \|x_n\| \to 0 \tab \contradiction\] Claim: \(y\) is an eigenvalue \[\lambda y \gets \lambda Ax_n \to A y \implies Ay - \lambda y = 0\]

docs.title: Norm of self-adjoint operators
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a Hilbert space} \\ & A \in L(X, X) \text{ be self-adjoint} \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \|A\| = \sup_{\|x\| = 1} |\inner{Ax}{x}|\] Proof \[ \begin{align*} \|A\| &= \sup_{\|x\| = 1} \inner{Ax}{\frac{Ax}{\|A x\|}} \\ &\le \sup_{\|x\| = \|y\| = 1} \inner{Ax}{y} \\ &= \frac{1}{4} \left(\sup_{\|x\| = \|y\| = 1} \inner{A(x+y)}{x+y} - \inner{A(x-y)}{x-y}\right) \\ &\le \sup_{\|z\| = 1} \inner{Az}{z} \\ &\le \sup_{\|z\| = 1} \|A\| \|z\|^2 = \|A\| \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Fourier representation
Fourier series representation
docs.text:
\(\{e^{ikx}\}_{k \in \Z}\) is an orthonormal basis of \(L^2([0, 2\pi])\).

\[\lim_{N\to\infty} \sum_{k=-N}^N \inner{f}{e_k} e_k \in [f]\] Proof

Define the integral operator \(K\) by

\[Kf(x) := \int_0^{2\pi} f(s) ds - \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^x f(s) ds dx\] Claim: \(Kf\in L_0^2\tab \forall\ f \in L_0^2\)

Moreover, \(Kf\) is continuous:

\[ \begin{align*} |Kf(x) - Kf(y)| &= \left|\int_0^x f(s) ds - \int_0^y f(s) ds\right| \\ &= \left|\int_x^y f(s) ds\right| \\ &\le \left|\int_x^y 1^2ds\right|^\frac{1}{2} \left|\int_x^y |f(s)|^2 ds\right|^\frac{1}{2} \\ &= \sqrt{|x-y|} \|f\|_{L^2} \end{align*} \] Claim: \(K\) has the same eigenvalues as \(\frac{d}{dx}\)

Let \(u, \lambda\) be an eigenfunction-eigenvalue pair of \(K\).

\[Ku = \lambda u \iff \frac{1}{\lambda}Ku \implies u \in C([0, 2\pi])\] Claim: \(K : L_0^2 \to L_0^2\) is compact

R.T.P.: Given any bounded sequence \(\{f_j\}\) in \(L_0^2\), \(\{Kf_j\}\) has a convergent subsequence.

\[ \begin{align*} f_j \text{ is bounded in } L^2 \implies& Kf_j \text{ is bounded in } C^{1/2}([0, 2\pi]) \\ \implieshref{#t85}{T.8.5}& \{Kf_j\} \text{ is relatively compact in } C([0, 2\pi]) \\ \implies& \{Kf_j\} \text{ has a convergent subsequence in } C([0, 2\pi]) \end{align*} \] Claim: \(iK\) is self-adjoint on \(L_0^2\)

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & u, v \in C_{2\pi, 0} \\ & f:=K u \\ & g:=K v \end{align*}\)

\[ \begin{align*} \implies \inner{iKu}{v} &= \int_0^{2\pi} i f\ \overline g' dx \\ &= \int_0^{2\pi} i f\ \overline g dx - \int_0^{2\pi} i f' \overline g dx \\ &= \int_0^{2\pi} f' \overline{ig} dx \\ &= \int_0^{2\pi} u \overline{iKv} dx \\ &= \inner{u}{iKv} \end{align*} \]

So \(K\) is self-adjoint on \(C_{2\pi, 0}\). We have that

  • \(C_{2\pi, 0}\) is dense in \(L_0^2\)
  • \(\inner{\cdot}{\cdot}\) and \(K\) are continuous.

Thus, \(K\) is self-adjoint on \(L_0^2\) too.

Then, by T.8.2, \(\{e^{ikx}\}_{k \in \Z\setminus\{0\}}\) is an orthonormal basis of \(L_0^2\).

Abolishing the zero mean requirement, we have for \(f \in L^2([0, 2\pi])\) that

  • \(f - \langle f \rangle \in L_0^2\)
  • \(\langle f \rangle \in \Span\{e_0\}\)

Hence, \(\{e^{ikx}\}_{k \in \Z}\) is an orthonormal basis of \(L^2([0, 2\pi])\).

docs.title: 11 Weak convergence
docs.text:
Definition 11.1

Let \(X\) be a Banach space

  1. \(\quad x_j \weakto x \in X \iff orall\ L \in X^* : L(x_j) o L(x)\)
  2. \(\quad L_j \weakstarto L \in X^* \iff orall\ x \in X : L_j(x) o L(x)\)
Proposition 11.1 Example 11.1

The converse of P.11.1 is not true.

Let \(X = \ell^2\)

Claim: \(e_j \weakto 0\)

Let \(L \in X^*\).

\( egin{align*} \implies \exists\ & a = (a_1, a_2, \ldots) \in \ell^2 : \ & L(e_j) = \inner{e_j}{a} = a_j o 0 \end{align*}\)

However, \(e_j ot o 0\).

Proposition 11.2

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X ext{ be a Banach space} \ & x_j \in X \ & x, x' \in X \end{align*}\)

\[ egin{align*} a) && x_j \weakto x \land x_j \weakto x' &\implies x = x' \ b) && x_j \weakto x &\implies \{x_j\} ext{ is bounded} \ c) && x_j \weakto x &\implies \|x\| \leq \liminf_{j o \infty} \|x_j\| \ d) && x_j \weakto x \land x_j \in U ext{ closed subspace} &\implies x \in U \end{align*} \] Proof \(a)\)\(b)\)\(c)\)\(d)\)

By continuity, we have that

\[ orall\ L \in X^* : L(x - x') = \lim_{j o \infty} L(x_j - x') = 0\]

In particular, by the Hahn-Banach theorem C.10.2, we have

\[\exists\ L \in X^* : L(x - x') = \|x - x'\| = 0\]

so \(x = x'\).

We have the following equivalences:

\[ egin{align*} x_n \weakto x &\iff orall\ L \in X^* : L(x_n) o L(x) \ &\iff orall\ L \in X^* : (x_n)_{ev}(L) o x_{ev}(L) \ &\iff orall\ L \in X^* : (x_n)_{ev}(L) ext{ is bounded} \ \end{align*} \]

So \((x_n)_{ev}\) is pointwise bounded, and by the uniform boundedness principle, it is uniformly bounded.

\[ egin{align*} \|(x_n)_{ev}\|_{L(X^*, \K)} =& \sup_{L \in X^*\setminus\{0\}} rac{|(x_n)_{ev}(L)|}{\|L\|} \ =& \sup_{L \in X^*\setminus\{0\}} rac{|L(x_n)|}{\|L\|} \ \eqhref{../duality/#c103}{C.10.3} & \|x_n\| \end{align*} \]

Hence, \(\{x_n\}\) is bounded.

\[ egin{align*} |L(x)| =& \lim_{j o \infty} |L(x_j)| \leq \|L\| \liminf_{j o \infty} \|x_j\| \ \implieshref{../duality/#c102}{C.10.2}& \exists L \in X^* : L x = \|x\| \land \|L\| = 1 \ \implies & \|x\| \le \liminf_{j o \infty} \|x_j\| \end{align*} \]

Assume \(x otin U\). By the Hanh-Banach theorem C.10.4 we have

\[\exists\ L \in X^* : L|_U = 0 \land L(x) eq 0\]

Thus, we get \(0=L(x_j) o L(x) eq 0\), which is a contradiction.

Definition 11.2 Theorem 11.1 Theorem 11.2 Lemma 11.1 Lemma 11.2

Let \(Y\) be a Banach space.

\[Y^* ext{ is separable} \implies Y ext{ is separable}\] Proof

Exercise 52

Lemma 11.3

\( egin{align*} ext{Let } & X ext{ be a reflexive Banach space} \ & U \subseteq X ext{ be a closed subspace} \end{align*}\)

\[\implies U ext{ is reflexive}\]

Proof: Omitted

docs.title: Justification of the name "weak"
docs.text:
1. \(\quad x_j \to x \implies x_j \weakto x\)
  2. \(\quad L_j \to L \implies L_j \weakstarto L\)
Proof 1.2.

Follows from continuity of \(L\).

\[ \begin{align*} L_j \to L & \iff \|L_j - L\| \to 0 \\ & \iff |L_jx - Lx| \to 0 \quad \forall\ x \in X \\ & \implies L_j \weakstarto L \end{align*} \]

docs.title: Reflexive Banach space
docs.text:
A Banach space \(X\) is called reflexive if

\[\forall f \in L(X^*, \K) : \exists\ x \in X : \forall\ L \in X^* : f(L) = L(x)\]

Informally, we write \(X \cong X^{**}\).

docs.title: Replacement for the Bolzano-Weierstrass theorem
docs.text:
Let \(X\) be a Banach space.

\[X \text{ is reflexive} \implies \text{every bounded sequence in } X \text{ has a weakly convergent subsequence}\] Proof Separable spaceNon-separable space \[ \begin{align*} (1) &&& \begin{aligned} x_j \weakto x \in X & \iff \forall\ L \in X^* : L(x_j) \to L(x) \\ & \iff (x_j)_{ev} \weakto x_{ev} \in X^{**} \end{aligned} \\ (2) &&& (x_j) \text{ bounded in } X \iffhref{../duality/#c103}{C.10.3} ((x_j)_{ev}) \text{ bounded in } X^{**} \\ (3) &&& \begin{aligned} &X^{**} \cong X \text{ is separable} \\ & \implieshref{#l112}{L.11.2} X^* \text{ is separable} \end{aligned} \end{align*} \]

This yields

\[ \begin{align*} (x_j) \text{ bounded in } X \overset{(2)}{\iff}& ((x_j)_{ev}) \text{ bounded in } X^{**} \\ \implieshref{#t112}{T.11.2} & \exists\ \text{ subsequence } (x_{j_n})_{ev} \weakto x \in X^{**} \\ \overset{(1)}{\implies} & x_{j_n} \weakto x \in X \end{align*} \]

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be non-separable} \\ & \{x_j\} \subseteq X \text{ bounded} \\ & U = \overline{\Span}\{x_j\} \subseteq X \end{align*}\)

Then, \(U\) is separable, and by L.11.3 reflexive. Thus, \((x_j)\) has a weakly convergent subsequence in \(U\).

docs.title: Banach-Alaoglu theorem
docs.text:
Let \(X\) be a Banach space.

\[X \text{ is separable} \implies \text{ Every bounded sequence in } X^* \text{ has a weak* convergent subsequence}\] Proof

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & (L_j) \subseteq X^* \text{ be bounded} \\ & \{x_1, x_2, \ldots\} \subseteq X \text{ be a dense subset} \end{align*}\)

Claim: \((L_k(x_k))_j\) is a bounded sequence in \(\K\)

From the boundedness of \((L_j)\), we have

\[|L_j(x_k)| \leq \|L_j\| \|x_k\| < \infty\]

\(\begin{align*} \implieshref{https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bolzano%E2%80%93Weierstrass_theorem}{B.W.} & \exists\ \text{ convergent subsequence } (L_{j_n}(x_k))_n \\ \overset{\text{diag}}{\implies} & \exists\ \text{ subsequence } (L_{j_n})_n : (L_{j_n}(x_k))_n \text{ converges } \forall\ k \end{align*}\)

Let \(L : \Span\{x_1, x_2, \ldots\} \to \K\) be a function defined by

\[L(x_k) := \lim_{n \to \infty} L_{j_n}(x_k)\] Claim: \(L\) is linear

Follows from the continuity of linear combinations and linearity of the \(L_j\).

Claim: \(L\) is continuous \[|L(x_k)| = \lim_{n\to \infty} |L_{j_n}(x_k)| \leq \sup_j \|L_j\| \cdot \|x_k\| < \infty\]

\(\implieshref{#l111}{L.11.1} \exists!\ \tilde L : \overline{\Span}\{x_1, x_2, \ldots\} \to \K\) linear continuous extension of \(L\).

Then, it remains tp show that \(|L_{j_n}(x) - \tilde L(x)| \to 0\) for all \(x \in X\).

We have that

  1. \(|L_{j_n}(x_k) - \tilde L(x_k)| \to 0\) for all \(k\)
  2. \(\forall\ x \in X, \varepsilon > 0 \exists\ k : \|x - x_k\| < \varepsilon\)
\[ \begin{align*} \implies |L_{j_n}(x) - \tilde L(x)| &\leq |L_{j_n}(x) - L_{j_n}(x_k)| + |L_{j_n}(x_k) - \tilde L(x_k)| + |\tilde L(x_k) - \tilde L(x)| \\ &\!\overset{n\to\infty}{\leq} \sup_m \|L_m\| \cdot \|x - x_k\| + \|\tilde L\| \cdot \|x - x_k\| \\ & < \left(\sup_m \|L_m\| + \|\tilde L\|\right) \varepsilon \end{align*} \]

Therefore, we get

\[\forall\ x \in X : |L_{j_n}(x) - \tilde L(x)| \to 0 \iff L_{j_n} \weakstarto \tilde L \in X^*\]

docs.title: Continuous extension of linear operators
docs.text:
\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & X \text{ be a normed space} \\ & Y \text{ be a Banach space} \\ & D \subseteq X \text{ be a dense subspace} \\ & T \in L(D, Y) \end{align*}\)

\[\implies \exists!\ \tilde T \in L(X, Y) : \tilde T|_D = T\] Proof

\(\begin{align*} \text{Let } & x \in X \\ & \{x_j\} \subseteq D : x_j \to x \end{align*}\)

Then, we have

\[ \begin{align*} (x_j) \text{ is convergent } \implies& (x_j) \text{ is Cauchy} \\ \overset{\text{cont}}{\implies}& (Tx_j) \text{ is Cauchy} \\ \implies& (Tx_j) \text{ is convergent} \end{align*} \]

Define

\[\tilde T(x) := \lim_{j \to \infty} T(x_j)\]
